topics.title: Review of Z Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET744 - DISCRETE TIME CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Z Transforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Z-Transform

The **Z-Transform** is a powerful mathematical operator that converts a discrete-time signal $x[k]$ (where $k$ is an integer representing the sample index) from the time domain into a complex frequency domain known as the **Z-domain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Sided (Unilateral) Z-Transform — KTU Standard Definition**
>
> For a discrete-time sequence $x[k]$ defined for $k \ge 0$, the one-sided Z-transform is mathematically defined as:
>
> $$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[k]\} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] \, z^{-k}$$
>
> where $z$ is a complex variable of the form $z = re^{j\omega}$, with $r = \vert z \vert$ being the magnitude and $\omega$ the angle (frequency).

The **Two-Sided (Bilateral) Z-Transform** extends the summation from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, z^{-k}$$

In **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the one-sided Z-transform is predominantly used because it aligns with **causal discrete-time systems** (systems whose output depends only on present and past inputs), which form the basis of all digital control and sampled-data analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Z-Transform is to Discrete Signals what Laplace Transform is to Continuous Signals"**
>
> Imagine a **magnetic audio tape** (continuous signal — infinite resolution). To play it on a **digital music player**, you must "sample" it into discrete chunks (MP3 files). The **Laplace transform** is the tool used to analyze the smooth, continuous tape, while the **Z-transform** is the tool used to analyze the **digitized, sampled version** of that same tape.
>
> The variable $s$ (used in Laplace) is replaced by $z$, and the differential equations governing continuous systems become **difference equations** governing discrete systems.

## 1.3 Region of Convergence (ROC) — The Critical Companion

The Z-transform is a **power series**, and like all power series, it converges only for certain values of $z$. The set of all $z$ values in the complex plane for which $X(z)$ yields a finite, well-defined value is called the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ROC Matters in KTU Exams:**
> Two entirely different sequences can produce the **same** $X(z)$ expression but have **different ROCs**. Therefore, the ROC is an inseparable part of the complete specification of the Z-transform.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Summary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $x[k]$ | Discrete-time sequence (input/output) |
| $X(z)$ | Z-transform of $x[k]$ |
| $z$ | Complex variable, $z = re^{j\Omega}$ |
| ROC | Region of Convergence in the z-plane |
| Causal Sequence | $x[k] = 0$ for $k < 0$ — ROC is **outside** the outermost pole |
| Anti-causal Sequence | $x[k] = 0$ for $k > 0$ — ROC is **inside** the innermost pole |
| Two-sided Sequence | Non-zero for both $k>0$ and $k<0$ — ROC is an **annular ring** between poles |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of the Region of Convergence (ROC)

The ROC must satisfy these fundamental properties (extremely high-yield for KTU 4-mark and 7-mark questions):

1. **Pole-Exclusion Property:** The ROC **cannot contain any pole** of $X(z)$. Poles are explicitly excluded since $X(z)$ is infinite at a pole.
2. **Bounded by Poles:** For a **right-sided (causal)** sequence, the ROC extends **outward** from the outermost pole to infinity, i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert p_{max} \vert$.
3. **Bounded by Poles (Left-sided):** For a **left-sided (anti-causal)** sequence, the ROC extends **inward** from the innermost pole to the origin, i.e., $\vert z \vert < \vert p_{min} \vert$.
4. **Annular ROC for Two-Sided Sequences:** For a two-sided sequence, the ROC is a ring $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$ bounded by poles on both sides.
5. **Infinite ROC:** If $x[k]$ is a **finite-duration** sequence, the ROC is the **entire z-plane** (except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$).

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.2.1 Standard Z-Transforms of Elementary Sequences

| $x[k]$ | $X(z)$ | ROC |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta[k]$ (Unit Impulse) | $1$ | All $z$ |
| $u[k]$ (Unit Step) | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^{k} \, u[k]$ (Exponential) | $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $k \, u[k]$ (Ramp) | $\dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $k^2 \, u[k]$ (Parabolic) | $\dfrac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\sin(\omega_0 k) \, u[k]$ | $\dfrac{z \, \sin(\omega_0)}{z^2 - 2z\cos(\omega_0) + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 k) \, u[k]$ | $\dfrac{z(z - \cos(\omega_0))}{z^2 - 2z\cos(\omega_0) + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^{k} \sin(\omega_0 k) \, u[k]$ | $\dfrac{a \, z \, \sin(\omega_0)}{z^2 - 2az\cos(\omega_0) + a^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $a^{k} \cos(\omega_0 k) \, u[k]$ | $\dfrac{z(z - a\cos(\omega_0))}{z^2 - 2az\cos(\omega_0) + a^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |

### 2.2.2 Core Theorems and Properties of Z-Transform

| Property | Time Domain | Z-Domain | ROC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | $a x_1[k] + b x_2[k]$ | $a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | $\sup(R_1, R_2)$ |
| **Time Shifting** | $x[k-n]$ | $z^{-n} X(z)$ | $R$ (possibly modified) |
| **Time Reversal** | $x[-k]$ | $X(1/z)$ | $1/R$ |
| **Multiplication by $a^{k}$** | $a^{k} x[k]$ | $X(z/a)$ | $\vert a \vert R$ |
| **Multiplication by $k$** | $k \, x[k]$ | $-z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | $R$ |
| **Convolution** | $x_1[k] * x_2[k]$ | $X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$ | $\sup(R_1, R_2)$ |
| **Initial Value Theorem** | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | — | — |
| **Final Value Theorem** | $x[\infty] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)X(z)$ | — | ROC must include $\vert z \vert = 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initial Value Theorem — Caveat for KTU:**
> $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ holds **only if** $x[k] = 0$ for $k < 0$ (causal sequence).
>
> **Final Value Theorem — Caveat for KTU:**
> Holds **only if** all poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ lie **strictly inside** the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ (system must be stable).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Z-transform is not a purely academic concept — it is the **backbone of digital control engineering**:

- **Digital PID Controllers** in industrial process control use Z-transform to discretize continuous-time controllers.
- **Digital Filters (FIR/IIR)** in audio processing, biomedical signal processing, and image processing rely on Z-domain analysis for stability and frequency response.
- **Sampled-Data Control Systems** (robotic arms, missile guidance, aircraft autopilots) require Z-transform for analysis between sampler and digital computer.
- **DSP Processors** in smartphones, hearing aids, and radar systems execute algorithms derived from Z-transform transfer functions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Z-Transform of the Unit Step Sequence $u[k]$

**Step 1:** Write the definition of the unit step sequence.

$$u[k] = \begin{cases} 1, & k \ge 0 \\ 0, & k < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the one-sided Z-transform definition.

$$U(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} u[k] \, z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 1 \cdot z^{-k}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and expand the geometric series.

$$U(z) = z^{0} + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + \cdots = 1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + \cdots$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this as an **infinite geometric progression** with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $r = z^{-1}$. Apply the standard GP sum formula $S = \dfrac{a}{1-r}$, valid only when $\vert r \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z^{-1} \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 1$.

$$U(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z$ to express in standard positive-power form.

$$U(z) = \frac{z}{z - 1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-Transform of the Exponential Sequence $a^{k} u[k]$

**Step 1:** Apply the Z-transform definition.

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a^{k} z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^{k}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize as a geometric series with ratio $a z^{-1}$. Converges if $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof of the Time-Shifting Property

**Statement:** If $\mathcal{Z}\{x[k]\} = X(z)$ with ROC $R$, then $\mathcal{Z}\{x[k-n]\} = z^{-n} X(z)$ (for one-sided $x[k]$).

**Step 1:** Apply the Z-transform definition to $y[k] = x[k-n]$.

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k-n] \, z^{-k}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the change of variable $m = k - n$, so $k = m + n$, $dk = dm$. The summation starts when $k=0 \Rightarrow m=-n$, but since $x[m]=0$ for $m<0$, we can start the sum at $m=0$.

$$Y(z) = \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x[m] \, z^{-(m+n)} = z^{-n} \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x[m] \, z^{-m}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the remaining sum as $X(z)$.

$$Y(z) = z^{-n} X(z) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Proof of the Initial Value Theorem

**Statement:** For a causal sequence, $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$.

**Step 1:** Write out the Z-transform series expansion.

$$X(z) = x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + x[3] z^{-3} + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Take the limit as $z \to \infty$. All terms containing $z^{-1}, z^{-2}, \ldots$ vanish (since $z^{-1} \to 0$ as $z \to \infty$).

$$\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = x[0] + 0 + 0 + 0 + \cdots = x[0] \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.5 Analytical Example — Inverse Z-Transform via Partial Fraction Expansion

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of:

$$X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-1)(z-2)}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2 \text{ (causal)}$$

**Step 1:** Rewrite $X(z)$ in proper form (with $z$ in the numerator for partial fractions).

We can write:

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{1}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-2}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for constants $A$ and $B$ using the **cover-up method**.

$$A = \left. \frac{1}{z-2} \right|_{z=1} = \frac{1}{1-2} = -1$$

$$B = \left. \frac{1}{z-1} \right|_{z=2} = \frac{1}{2-1} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Reconstruct $X(z)$.

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{-1}{z-1} + \frac{1}{z-2}$$

$$X(z) = \frac{-z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{z-2}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the standard pair $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{z-a}\right\} = a^{k} u[k]$, valid for causal ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

Since the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 2$, both terms are causal:

$$x[k] = -1 \cdot (1)^{k} u[k] + (2)^{k} u[k] = \left(2^{k} - 1\right) u[k]$$

**Step 5 (Verification using Initial Value Theorem):**

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{z}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{z}{z^2 - 3z + 2} = 0$$

Check with our answer: $x[0] = 2^0 - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0$. ✓

**Step 6 (Verification at $k=1$):**

Direct from $X(z)$: $X(z) = z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 7z^{-3} + \ldots$ gives $x[1]=1$.

From our formula: $x[1] = 2^1 - 1 = 1$. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Z-Transform Workflow in a Sampled-Data Control System

This diagram illustrates where the Z-Transform fits in the broader discrete-time control system architecture. A Mermaid block-diagram is used in lieu of a physical circuit schematic for clarity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Continuous Input r t]:::input --> nodeB[Sampler with period T]:::sample
    nodeB --> nodeC[Discrete Sequence r k]:::seq
    nodeC --> nodeD[Z Transform Analysis Xz Rz]:::zdom
    nodeD --> nodeE[Inverse Z Transform xk uk]:::zinv
    nodeE --> nodeF[Discrete Output yk]:::seq
    nodeF --> nodeG[Zero Order Hold ZOH]:::hold
    nodeG --> nodeH[Continuous Output yt]:::output

    nodeA1[Poles and Zeros] --> nodeD
    nodeA2[ROC Determination] --> nodeD
    nodeA3[Stability Check z1] --> nodeD
    nodeA4[Initial and Final Value] --> nodeD

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef sample fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef seq fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef zdom fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef zinv fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef hold fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow — Z-Transform of Common Sequences

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Discrete Time Domain xk]:::input
    nodeA --> nodeB[Apply Definition Sum xk z pow neg k]:::proc
    nodeB --> nodeC{Determine Convergence}:::decision
    nodeC -->|Converges| nodeD[Z Domain Expression Xz]:::zdom
    nodeC -->|Diverges| nodeE[ROC Empty or Invalid]:::error
    nodeD --> nodeF[Identify Poles of Xz]:::proc
    nodeF --> nodeG[Plot ROC in Z Plane]:::plot
    nodeG --> nodeH{Causality Check}:::decision
    nodeH -->|Causal xk0 for k lt 0| nodeI[ROC is Outside Outermost Pole]:::roc
    nodeH -->|Anti Causal xk0 for k gt 0| nodeJ[ROC is Inside Innermost Pole]:::roc
    nodeH -->|Two Sided| nodeK[ROC is Annular Ring]:::roc

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#000
    classDef zdom fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef plot fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef roc fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Pole-Zero and ROC Map — Standard Sequences

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[z equals 0]:::origin
    nodeB[z equals a]:::pole
    nodeC[z equals 1]:::pole
    nodeD[Unit Circle z equals 1]:::circle

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeD --- nodeC

    nodeE[Shaded Region Outside Pole ROC for akuk]:::roc --> nodeB

    classDef origin fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef pole fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef circle stroke-dasharray: 5 5,stroke:#0277BD,fill:none,color:#0277BD
    classDef roc fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the Pole-Zero-ROC Map:**

- A pole at $z = a$ is marked with an **X**.
- The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is the **stability boundary** — a system is **stable** if **all poles lie inside** the unit circle.
- The shaded region represents the ROC for the causal exponential sequence $a^{k} u[k]$ — it is **outside** the pole, extending to infinity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q: Define the Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence. State the conditions for its convergence.**

**Model Answer:**

The one-sided Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x[k]$ is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] \, z^{-k}$$

It is a power series in $z^{-1}$ and converges only when the magnitude of $z$ lies within a specific region in the complex plane known as the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

**Convergence Condition:**

The series converges absolutely if:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert x[k] \vert \cdot \vert z \vert^{-k} < \infty$$

For a causal exponential sequence $x[k] = a^{k} u[k]$, the series becomes a geometric progression with ratio $a/z$, which converges only when $\vert a/z \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

> **Examiner's Note (3-mark weightage):** Both the **definition** and the **convergence condition** must be stated. Partial marks are awarded for the formula even if the convergence statement is missing.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Q: State and prove the Initial Value Theorem for Z-transforms.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For a causal sequence $x[k]$ (i.e., $x[k] = 0$ for $k < 0$), the initial value is given by:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Proof:**

Expand the Z-transform as an infinite series:

$$X(z) = x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + x[3] z^{-3} + \cdots$$

Taking the limit $z \to \infty$, every term with $z^{-n}$ vanishes since $z^{-n} \to 0$ for $n \ge 1$. Therefore:

$$\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = x[0] + 0 + 0 + \cdots = x[0] \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### 🔷 Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Z-transform of the sequence $x[k] = a^{k} \cos(\omega_0 k) \, u[k]$. Show its pole-zero plot and ROC. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Use Euler's identity to express cosine in exponential form.

$$x[k] = a^{k} \cos(\omega_0 k) \, u[k] = \frac{a^{k} e^{j\omega_0 k} + a^{k} e^{-j\omega_0 k}}{2} \, u[k]$$

$$x[k] = \frac{1}{2} \left( a e^{j\omega_0} \right)^{k} u[k] + \frac{1}{2} \left( a e^{-j\omega_0} \right)^{k} u[k]$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Z-transform linearity and the standard exponential pair $\mathcal{Z}\{c^{k} u[k]\} = \dfrac{z}{z-c}$.

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{z}{z - a e^{j\omega_0}} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{z}{z - a e^{-j\omega_0}}$$

**Step 3:** Combine the two fractions over a common denominator.

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{z(z - a e^{-j\omega_0}) + z(z - a e^{j\omega_0})}{(z - a e^{j\omega_0})(z - a e^{-j\omega_0})}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the numerator.

$$\text{Numerator} = \frac{z}{2} \left[ 2z - a(e^{j\omega_0} + e^{-j\omega_0}) \right] = \frac{z}{2} \left[ 2z - 2a\cos(\omega_0) \right] = z \left[ z - a\cos(\omega_0) \right]$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the denominator.

$$\text{Denominator} = z^2 - z \cdot a(e^{j\omega_0} + e^{-j\omega_0}) + a^2 = z^2 - 2az\cos(\omega_0) + a^2$$

**Step 6:** Final Z-transform expression.

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z \, [z - a\cos(\omega_0)]}{z^2 - 2az\cos(\omega_0) + a^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert}$$

**Pole-Zero Plot Description:**

- **Zeros:** $z = 0$ and $z = a \cos(\omega_0)$.
- **Poles:** $z = a e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ — two complex conjugate poles at radius $\vert a \vert$ from origin.
- **ROC:** Exterior of the circle of radius $\vert a \vert$ (since the sequence is causal).
- **Stability:** System is stable if $\vert a \vert < 1$ (poles inside the unit circle).

---

**(b) Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)(z-2)}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 2$. Also verify using the initial value theorem. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Express $X(z)/z$ for partial fraction expansion.

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z+1}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cover-up method for residues.

$$A = \left. \frac{z+1}{z-2} \right|_{z=1} = \frac{1+1}{1-2} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2$$

$$B = \left. \frac{z+1}{z-1} \right|_{z=2} = \frac{2+1}{2-1} = \frac{3}{1} = 3$$

**Step 3:** Reconstruct $X(z)$ in standard form.

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{-2}{z-1} + \frac{3}{z-2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad X(z) = \frac{-2z}{z-1} + \frac{3z}{z-2}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the inverse Z-transform pair.

Since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 2$, both terms are causal:

$$x[k] = -2(1)^{k} u[k] + 3(2)^{k} u[k]$$

$$\boxed{x[k] = \left( 3 \cdot 2^{k} - 2 \right) u[k]}$$

**Step 5: Verification using Initial Value Theorem.**

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{z^2 + z}{z^2 - 3z + 2} = 1$$

From our formula: $x[0] = 3(2^0) - 2 = 3 - 2 = 1$ ✓

**Step 6: Verification using Final Value Theorem.**

$$x[\infty] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{z(z+1)}{z-2} = \frac{1 \cdot 2}{1-2} = -2$$

From our formula: $x[k] = 3 \cdot 2^{k} - 2 \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$.

⚠️ **Discrepancy Alert:** The Final Value Theorem gives $-2$, but the sequence diverges. This is because the ROC $\vert z \vert > 2$ does **not** include the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$, hence the **Final Value Theorem is not applicable** for this sequence (the system is unstable).

---

### 🔷 Question B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) Solve the difference equation $y[k+2] - 1.5 y[k+1] + 0.5 y[k] = u[k]$, with $y[0]=0, y[1]=1$, using Z-transform. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Apply the one-sided Z-transform to the difference equation.

Using $\mathcal{Z}\{y[k+n]\} = z^{n} Y(z) - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} y[i] z^{n-1-i}$ (the one-sided time-advance property):

- $\mathcal{Z}\{y[k+2]\} = z^{2} Y(z) - z^{2} y[0] - z \cdot y[1] = z^{2} Y(z) - 0 - z(1) = z^{2} Y(z) - z$
- $\mathcal{Z}\{y[k+1]\} = z Y(z) - z y[0] = z Y(z)$
- $\mathcal{Z}\{y[k]\} = Y(z)$
- $\mathcal{Z}\{u[k]\} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the difference equation.

$$[z^{2} Y(z) - z] - 1.5 [z Y(z)] + 0.5 Y(z) = \frac{z}{z-1}$$

**Step 3:** Collect $Y(z)$ terms.

$$Y(z) \left[ z^{2} - 1.5 z + 0.5 \right] = z + \frac{z}{z-1}$$

**Step 4:** Take common denominator on the right.

$$Y(z) \left[ z^{2} - 1.5 z + 0.5 \right] = \frac{z(z-1) + z}{z-1} = \frac{z^{2}}{z-1}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $Y(z)$.

$$Y(z) = \frac{z^{2}}{(z-1)(z^{2} - 1.5z + 0.5)} = \frac{z^{2}}{(z-1)(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \frac{z^{2}}{(z-1)^{2}(z-0.5)}$$

**Step 6:** Express $Y(z)/z$ and perform partial fraction expansion.

$$\frac{Y(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^{2}(z-0.5)} = \frac{A}{(z-1)^{2}} + \frac{B}{z-1} + \frac{C}{z-0.5}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for constants.

- $A = \left. \dfrac{z}{z-0.5} \right|_{z=1} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} = 2$
- $C = \left. \dfrac{z}{(z-1)^{2}} \right|_{z=0.5} = \dfrac{0.5}{(-0.5)^{2}} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.25} = 2$

For $B$, multiply both sides by $z$ and take the derivative approach:

$$B = \left. \frac{d}{dz} \left[ \frac{z}{z-0.5} \right] \right|_{z=1} = \left. \frac{(z-0.5) - z}{(z-0.5)^{2}} \right|_{z=1} = \frac{-0.5}{0.25} = -2$$

**Step 8:** Reconstruct $Y(z)$.

$$Y(z) = \frac{2z}{(z-1)^{2}} - \frac{2z}{z-1} + \frac{2z}{z-0.5}$$

**Step 9:** Apply inverse Z-transform using $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{(z-a)^{2}}\right\} = k a^{k-1} u[k]$ and the standard exponential pair.

$$\boxed{y[k] = 2 k (1)^{k-1} u[k] - 2(1)^{k} u[k] + 2(0.5)^{k} u[k] = 2k - 2 + 2(0.5)^{k}, \quad k \ge 0}$$

---

**(b) State and prove the Final Value Theorem for Z-transforms. Discuss its applicability and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Statement:** If $x[k]$ is a causal sequence and all poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$, then:

$$x[\infty] = \lim_{k \to \infty} x[k] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$$

**Proof Outline:**

Consider $X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] z^{-k}$. The shifted sequence $x[k+1]$ has Z-transform $zX(z) - zx[0]$. Subtracting:

$$zX(z) - zx[0] - X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left[ x[k+1] - x[k] \right] z^{-k} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \Delta x[k] \, z^{-k}$$

Taking limit $z \to 1$:

$$\lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z) - \lim_{z \to 1} z x[0] = \lim_{z \to 1} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \Delta x[k] z^{-k}$$

The first term becomes $x[0]$; the right-hand side becomes $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \Delta x[k]$. By the telescoping series property, this sum equals $x[\infty] - x[0]$, yielding the result. $\blacksquare$

**Limitations:**

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **The ROC must include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. If any pole of $X(z)$ lies on or outside the unit circle, the theorem is **invalid**.
> 2. **The system must be stable** — meaning all poles of $X(z)$ lie strictly inside $\vert z \vert = 1$.
> 3. **The sequence must approach a finite steady-state value** — i.e., $\lim_{k \to \infty} x[k]$ must exist as a finite number.
> 4. **The theorem does not apply to oscillating sequences** like $\sin(\omega_0 k)$, which never settle to a single value.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Frequent Marks-Loss Areas in Z-Transform Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the ROC** — A Z-transform answer without ROC is considered **incomplete** and loses 1–2 marks consistently.
> 2. **Confusing $z$ with $z^{-1}$ partial fractions** — KTU expects $\dfrac{X(z)}{z}$ partial fractions; students often incorrectly do partial fractions of $X(z)$ directly. Always do PFE on $\dfrac{X(z)}{z}$ first.
> 3. **Misapplying Final Value Theorem** — Applying it to unstable systems (poles outside unit circle) yields an incorrect finite answer. Always check stability first.
> 4. **Causal vs Anti-causal confusion** — In inverse Z-transform, the ROC determines whether the resulting sequence is right-sided or left-sided. A causal ROC like $\vert z \vert > 2$ gives a **right-sided** sequence; an anti-causal ROC like $\vert z \vert < 0.5$ gives a **left-sided** sequence.
> 5. **Missing initial conditions in difference equations** — When solving difference equations using Z-transform, the one-sided shift property **requires** inclusion of initial conditions like $y[0]$, $y[1]$.
> 6. **Sign errors in time-shifting property** — $\mathcal{Z}\{x[k-n]\} = z^{-n} X(z)$ (delay), but $\mathcal{Z}\{x[k+n]\} = z^{n} X(z) - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} x[i] z^{n-1-i}$ (advance). Students confuse the two.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Z-Transforms**
>
> ✅ Z-transform converts $x[k]$ (discrete-time) into $X(z)$ (complex z-domain). Definition: $X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] z^{-k}$.
>
> ✅ ROC is **mandatory** — the same $X(z)$ with different ROCs represents different sequences.
>
> ✅ ROC **never contains any pole** of $X(z)$.
>
> ✅ For **causal (right-sided)** sequences: ROC is the **exterior** of the outermost pole.
>
> ✅ For **anti-causal (left-sided)** sequences: ROC is the **interior** of the innermost pole.
>
> ✅ Standard pair to memorize: $\mathcal{Z}\{a^{k} u[k]\} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.
>
> ✅ Standard pair to memorize: $\mathcal{Z}\{u[k]\} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$.
>
> ✅ Standard pair to memorize: $\mathcal{Z}\{k u[k]\} = \dfrac{z}{(z-1)^{2}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$.
>
> ✅ Standard pair to memorize: $\mathcal{Z}\{\cos(\omega_0 k) u[k]\} = \dfrac{z(z - \cos\omega_0)}{z^{2} - 2z\cos\omega_0 + 1}$.
>
> ✅ Standard pair to memorize: $\mathcal{Z}\{\sin(\omega_0 k) u[k]\} = \dfrac{z \sin\omega_0}{z^{2} - 2z\cos\omega_0 + 1}$.
>
> ✅ **Initial Value Theorem:** $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ (valid for causal sequences only).
>
> ✅ **Final Value Theorem:** $x[\infty] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ (valid only if ROC includes unit circle and all poles are inside).
>
> ✅ **Time-Shifting Property:** $\mathcal{Z}\{x[k-n]\} = z^{-n} X(z)$.
>
> ✅ **Multiplication by $k$ Property:** $\mathcal{Z}\{k x[k]\} = -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$.
>
> ✅ **Convolution Property:** $\mathcal{Z}\{x_1[k] * x_2[k]\} = X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$.
>
> ✅ **Inverse Z-transform procedure:** Factor denominator → do PFE of $\dfrac{X(z)}{z}$ → match with standard pairs → apply causal/anti-causal rule based on ROC.
>
> ✅ **Stability Criterion:** A discrete-time system is **BIBO stable** if and only if **all poles** of its transfer function $H(z)$ lie **strictly inside** the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.
>
> ✅ When solving **difference equations**, always use the **one-sided Z-transform** with proper initial conditions: $\mathcal{Z}\{y[k+1]\} = zY(z) - zy[0]$ and $\mathcal{Z}\{y[k+2]\} = z^{2} Y(z) - z^{2} y[0] - z y[1]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Sampling Theorem (Shannon–Nyquist Theorem)

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Theorem (KTU 2024 Statement):** A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $\omega_m$ rad/sec can be uniquely reconstructed from its set of samples, provided that the sampling frequency $\omega_s$ satisfies the inequality:
> $$\omega_s \;\geq\; 2\,\omega_m$$
> The minimum permissible sampling rate $\omega_N = 2\omega_m$ rad/sec (or $f_N = 2f_m$ Hz) is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and the corresponding interval $T = \pi / \omega_m$ is the **Nyquist Interval**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **movie camera** capturing motion at frames per second. If the wagon wheels are spinning very fast but you sample (record) the wheel only 5 times per second, the wheel will appear to rotate *backwards* (aliasing). If you sample at least twice the wheel's rotation speed, you can faithfully reconstruct the original motion. Sampling Theorem is the engineering rule that prevents this "wagon-wheel effect" in signals.

### Standard Metrics
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $\omega_N = 2\omega_m$
- **Sampling Period:** $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_s}$
- **Aliasing Frequency:** $\omega_a = \omega_s - \omega_m$ (when $\omega_s < 2\omega_m$)

---

## 1.2 Impulse Sampling

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Impulse Sampling** is a mathematical idealization in which a continuous signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic train of unit impulses $\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$. The sampled output is $x^*(t) = x(t)\,\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k} x(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$.

### Intuition
Imagine sprinkling the signal's *height* onto an idealised "infinite-spike comb." Each tooth of the comb samples the signal at one instant and encodes its amplitude as the weight (area) of an impulse. Physically un-realizable, but mathematically *the* starting point for all z-domain analysis.

---

## 1.3 Sampling Rate Selection

**Selection Criteria (KTU 2024):**
1. **Nyquist Criterion:** $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$ (mandatory)
2. **Anti-aliasing Filter (Prefilter):** Practical systems use $\omega_s = 5$ to $10$ times the signal bandwidth.
3. **Compute Budget:** Higher $f_s$ implies faster A/D and shorter compute time per sample.
4. **Closed-Loop Bandwidth:** For digital control, $\omega_s \approx 10\,\omega_{BW}$ is a common engineering thumb-rule.

---

## 1.4 Data Reconstruction — ZOH and FOH

### Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ZOH Definition:** A reconstruction device that holds the value of a sample constant over the entire sampling period $T$. The held value equals the most recent sample $x(kT)$ for $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$.

**Analogy:** A *step-function staircase* built by a child holding each block's height flat until the next block arrives.

**ZOH Transfer Function:**
$$G_{h0}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

### First-Order Hold (FOH)
> [!NOTE]
> **FOH Definition:** A reconstruction device that linearly extrapolates between the current and previous sample, producing a polygonal (piecewise-linear) approximation of the signal.

**Analogy:** Like connecting dots in a connect-the-dots puzzle, producing straight lines between samples.

**FOH Approximate Transfer Function:**
$$G_{h1}(s) \;\approx\; \frac{(1 - e^{-sT})^2}{T\,s^2}\,\left(1 + \frac{sT}{2}\right)^{-1}$$

A simpler normalised form often used:
$$G_{h1}(s) \;=\; \sqrt{2}\,\frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}\,\frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{sT + \sqrt{2}}$$

---

## 1.5 Pulse Transfer Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Transfer Function (PTF):** For a sampled-data system in which the input is a sampled signal and the output is sampled synchronously, the ratio of the z-transform of the output samples to the z-transform of the input samples is the **Pulse Transfer Function** $G(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.

**Generic Form:**
$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}$$

**With ZOH in Cascade:**
$$G(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\;\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

where $G_p(s)$ is the plant transfer function and $G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$.

---

## 1.6 Control Configurations

The four canonical sampled-data configurations (per KTU):

| # | Configuration | Closed-Loop PTF |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampler at Input only | $\dfrac{G(z)\,D(z)}{1 + G(z)\,D(z)}$ |
| 2 | Sampler at Output only | $\dfrac{G(z)\,D(z)}{1 + G_h G D(z)}$ (mixed) |
| 3 | Samplers at Both Input & Output | $\dfrac{G(z)\,D(z)}{1 + G(z)\,D(z)}$ |
| 4 | Digital Controller + ZOH + Plant | $\dfrac{D(z)\,G_{ZOH}G_p(z)}{1 + D(z)\,G_{ZOH}G_p(z)}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A subtle but **frequently tested** point: Configuration 1 and 3 yield the *same* PTF because a synchronous sampler at the output of a block is mathematically indistinguishable from one at the input for LTI blocks. **Configuration 2 is asymmetric** and requires $G_h G D(z) \neq G(z) D(z)$.

---

## 1.7 Mapping Between the s-plane and the z-plane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Mapping:** $z = e^{sT}$, equivalently $s = \dfrac{1}{T}\ln z$.

Substituting $s = \sigma + j\omega$:
$$z \;=\; e^{\sigma T}\,e^{j\omega T}$$

| s-Plane Region | z-Plane Image |
|---|---|
| Left Half (LHP), $\sigma < 0$ | Inside unit disc, $\vert z \vert < 1$ |
| Right Half (RHP), $\sigma > 0$ | Outside unit disc, $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| Imaginary axis, $\sigma = 0$ | Unit circle, $\vert z \vert = 1$ |
| Primary strip $- \pi/T \leq \omega \leq \pi/T$ | One full wrap of unit circle |

---

## 1.8 Stability in the z-plane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) Stability Criterion (z-domain):** A discrete-time LTI system with PTF $G(z) = N(z)/D(z)$ is stable **if and only if** all the poles of $G(z)$ lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for every pole $z_i$.

### Real-Engineer Intuition
The s-plane stability boundary is the imaginary axis; the z-plane boundary is the unit circle. Stability tests in z-domain are simply "do all poles live inside the unit disc?" But finding roots of high-order polynomials is painful — hence Jury's, Schur–Cohn, and the bilinear + Routh–Hurwitz tests.

---

## 1.9 Jury's Stability Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Jury's Test** is a tabular test that determines whether all roots of a polynomial lie inside the unit circle *without computing the roots*. It is the z-domain analogue of the Routh array.

For a polynomial $F(z) = a_0 + a_1 z + a_2 z^2 + \dots + a_n z^n$ (with $a_n > 0$), Jury's test provides a sequence of necessary and sufficient conditions built from the coefficients.

---

## 1.10 Schur–Cohn Stability Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Schur–Cohn Test** is a determinant-based criterion equivalent to Jury's test, formulated through nested determinants of Toeplitz matrices. It is more compact in matrix form and well-suited for symbolic/algorithmic implementation.

---

## 1.11 Bilinear Transformation & Routh–Hurwitz in w-plane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bilinear (Möbius) Transformation:** $z = \dfrac{1 + w}{1 - w}$, or equivalently $w = \dfrac{z - 1}{z + 1}$. It maps the **interior of the unit circle** to the **left half of the w-plane**, and the unit circle to the imaginary axis.

After substituting, the z-domain polynomial becomes a w-domain polynomial to which the classical Routh–Hurwitz array can be applied. This is **the** engineering bridge that lets us reuse decades of continuous-time stability knowledge inside discrete-time analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sampling Theorem — Why It Works

**Why $f_s \geq 2 f_m$?**
1. The Fourier transform of an impulse-sampled signal is a *periodic repetition* of $X(j\omega)$ every $\omega_s$.
2. To prevent these replicas from *overlapping* (aliasing), the gap $\omega_s - 2\omega_m$ must remain non-negative.
3. With an ideal low-pass reconstruction filter of cutoff $\omega_m$, the original spectrum is exactly recovered.

**Reconstruction Formula (Ideal):**
$$x(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(kT)\,\frac{\sin\!\big(\pi(t-kT)/T\big)}{\pi(t-kT)/T}$$

---

## 2.2 Impulse Sampling — Spectrum

Multiplying by $\delta_T(t)$ convolves spectra periodically. Using $\mathcal{F}\{\delta_T(t)\} = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(\omega - n\omega_s)$:

$$X^*(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\big(j(\omega - n\omega_s)\big)$$

This is the **spectral replication** property that motivates every anti-aliasing design.

---

## 2.3 ZOH & FOH — Reconstruction Logic

### ZOH Step-by-Step
- **Step 1:** A/D acquires $x(kT)$ at $t = kT$.
- **Step 2:** D/A output holds $x(kT)$ flat for $t \in [kT, (k+1)T)$.
- **Step 3:** Update at $t = (k+1)T$.

$$x_{h0}(t) \;=\; x(kT) \quad \text{for} \quad kT \leq t < (k+1)T$$

### FOH Step-by-Step
- **Step 1:** D/A stores both $x(kT)$ and $x((k-1)T)$.
- **Step 2:** Output is the linear extrapolation:
$$x_{h1}(t) \;=\; x(kT) + \frac{x(kT) - x((k-1)T)}{T}\,(t - kT), \quad kT \leq t < (k+1)T$$
- **Step 3:** Slope discontinuities exist at sample instants — the FOH therefore **cannot exactly reconstruct** discontinuous signals; it is best for smooth bandlimited signals.

---

## 2.4 Pulse Transfer Function — Step-by-Step Derivation Outline

For configuration 4 (digital controller + ZOH + plant):

1. Continuous plant: $Y(s) = G_p(s) U(s)$.
2. ZOH output: $U(s) = G_{h0}(s) E^*(s) = \dfrac{1-e^{-sT}}{s}E^*(s)$.
3. Hence: $Y(s) = \dfrac{G_p(s)(1-e^{-sT})}{s} E^*(s)$.
4. Sampling both sides: $Y^*(s) = \left[\dfrac{G_p(s)(1-e^{-sT})}{s}\right]^* E^*(s)$.
5. Z-transforming: $Y(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\dfrac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} E(z) = G(z)E(z)$.
6. With controller $D(z)$: $\dfrac{Y(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{D(z)\,G(z)}{1 + D(z)\,G(z)}$.

---

## 2.5 s-to-z Mapping — Geometric Insight

For $s = \sigma + j\omega$ and $T$ = sample period:

$$\vert z \vert \;=\; e^{\sigma T}, \quad \angle z \;=\; \omega T \pmod{2\pi}$$

**Consequences:**
- Constant $\sigma$ lines (vertical lines in s-plane) → concentric circles in z-plane.
- Constant $\omega$ lines (horizontal lines in s-plane) → rays from origin in z-plane.
- The primary strip $-\pi/T < \omega < \pi/T$ maps to the entire unit disc boundary; outside this strip, the mapping **wraps** infinitely many times.

---

## 2.6 Stability in z-plane — Stepwise Logic

1. Form the closed-loop characteristic polynomial $F(z) = 1 + D(z)G(z)$.
2. Apply one of: Jury's, Schur–Cohn, or Bilinear+Routh–Hurwitz.
3. Verify all conditions → BIBO stability.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you are likely to need in the KTU ESE for this module. **Memorise it.** Use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `\vert`-bars in tables to preserve markdown parsing.

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Theorem | $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$ | $\omega_s$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 3 | Sampling Period | $T = 2\pi / \omega_s$ | seconds |
| 4 | Impulse Sampler Output | $x^*(t) = \sum x(kT)\delta(t-kT)$ | Idealised |
| 5 | Sampled Spectrum | $X^*(j\omega) = \tfrac{1}{T}\sum X(j(\omega - n\omega_s))$ | Periodic replication |
| 6 | s-to-z Mapping | $z = e^{sT}$ | Fundamental |
| 7 | z-to-s Mapping | $s = \tfrac{1}{T}\ln z$ | Multi-valued |
| 8 | Stability Region | $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ | Inside unit circle |
| 9 | ZOH Transfer Function | $G_{h0}(s) = (1-e^{-sT})/s$ | Exact |
| 10 | FOH Transfer Function (approx.) | $G_{h1}(s) = \sqrt{2}\,(1-e^{-sT})/s \cdot (1-e^{-sT})/(sT + \sqrt{2})$ | Tustin's approx. |
| 11 | PTF with ZOH | $G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$ | Standard result |
| 12 | Configuration 1, 3 Closed-Loop PTF | $T(z) = D(z)G(z)/[1+D(z)G(z)]$ | Synchronous |
| 13 | Configuration 2 Closed-Loop PTF | $T(z) = G(z)D(z)/[1+G_hG D(z)]$ | Asynchronous |
| 14 | Config 4 (Digital+ZOH+Plant) | $T(z) = D(z)G_{ZOH}G_p(z)/[1+D(z)G_{ZOH}G_p(z)]$ | Most common |
| 15 | Bilinear Transform | $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$, $w=(z-1)/(z+1)$ | Maps unit disc → LHP |
| 16 | Jury Condition 1 | $F(1) > 0$ | All real $F$ |
| 17 | Jury Condition 2 | $F(-1) > 0$ if $n$ even; $F(-1) < 0$ if $n$ odd | Sign depends on degree |
| 18 | Jury Condition 3 | $\vert a_0 \vert < a_n$ | With $a_n > 0$ |
| 19 | Schur–Cohn Determinant | $\Delta_n = \det(M)$ where $M$ is Toeplitz | $n \times n$ matrix |
| 20 | Mapping s to z (constant damping) | $z = e^{\sigma T}e^{j\omega T}$ | $\sigma,\omega$ real |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Sampling Theorem** underpins every digital audio system (CD: 44.1 kHz vs 20 kHz human hearing), digital oscilloscopes, and medical imaging (CT/MRI k-space sampling).
- **ZOH** is what every DAC *naturally does* — its transfer function is fundamental to digital control design.
- **Pulse Transfer Function** is to discrete control what $G(s)$ is to continuous control — without it, there is no digital control theory.
- **Jury/Schur–Cohn** are embedded in symbolic CAD packages (MATLAB `isstable`, Mathematica `RootLocationTest`).
- **Bilinear + Routh–Hurwitz** is the universal trick to convert discrete stability to a familiar continuous test — the *most examined* topic in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spectrum of an Impulse-Sampled Signal

**Given:** $x^*(t) = x(t)\,\delta_T(t)$ where $\delta_T(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT)$.

**Step 1.** The Fourier series of the impulse train:
$$\delta_T(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jn\omega_s t}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Step 2.** Multiplication in time ⇒ convolution in frequency:
$$X^*(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\,X(j\omega) * \mathcal{F}\{\delta_T(t)\}$$

**Step 3.** Using the sifting property of impulses:
$$X^*(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\big(j(\omega - n\omega_s)\big)$$

**Interpretation:** The original spectrum is replicated every $\omega_s$ rad/s and scaled by $1/T$. **Aliasing** occurs when replicas overlap, which happens iff $\omega_s < 2\omega_m$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: ZOH Transfer Function $G_{h0}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})/s$

**Step 1.** A unit impulse input $\delta(t)$ to a ZOH gives a unit pulse of width $T$ and height 1:
$$h_{h0}(t) \;=\; u(t) - u(t - T)$$

**Step 2.** Laplace transform of this pulse:
$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t) - u(t-T)\} \;=\; \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Step 3.** Combine:
$$G_{h0}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Step 4.** ZOH frequency response (useful for anti-alias analysis):
$$G_{h0}(j\omega) \;=\; T\,e^{-j\omega T/2}\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)$$

It is *low-pass-like* with notches at $\omega = 2\pi n / T$, and introduces a half-sample **time delay** $T/2$ on average.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pulse Transfer Function with ZOH (Configuration 4)

**Given:** $G_p(s)$ is the plant, ZOH precedes the plant, and synchronous sampling exists at input and output.

**Step 1.** Output of plant:
$$Y(s) \;=\; G_p(s)\,G_{h0}(s)\,E^*(s) \;=\; \frac{G_p(s)(1-e^{-sT})}{s}\,E^*(s)$$

**Step 2.** Star-transform (sampler on both sides):
$$Y^*(s) \;=\; \left[\frac{G_p(s)(1-e^{-sT})}{s}\right]^* E^*(s)$$

**Step 3.** Use the identity $\big[F(s)(1-e^{-sT})\big]^* = F^*(s) - z^{-1}F^*(s) = (1 - z^{-1})F(z)$ under z-transform:
$$Y(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}E(z)$$

**Step 4.** Define the **Pulse Transfer Function**:
$$\boxed{\,G(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}\,}$$

**Step 5.** Closed-loop transfer function with digital controller $D(z)$:
$$T(z) \;=\; \frac{D(z)\,G(z)}{1 + D(z)\,G(z)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: s-plane to z-plane Mapping and Stability Translation

**Step 1.** Fundamental relation: $z = e^{sT}$.

**Step 2.** Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$:
$$z \;=\; e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} \;=\; e^{\sigma T}\,e^{j\omega T}$$

**Step 3.** Modulus and argument:
$$\vert z \vert \;=\; e^{\sigma T}, \quad \angle z \;=\; \omega T \pmod{2\pi}$$

**Step 4.** Stability test in s-plane: $\sigma < 0$. In z-plane:
$$\vert z \vert \;=\; e^{\sigma T} \;<\; e^{0} \;=\; 1$$

Therefore: **all closed-loop poles must satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$** for stability.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Bilinear Transformation

**Step 1.** The bilinear transform is defined by:
$$z \;=\; \frac{1 + w}{1 - w}, \quad w \;=\; \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

**Step 2.** Show that $\vert z \vert = 1$ maps to the imaginary axis in $w$. On the unit circle $z = e^{j\theta}$:
$$w \;=\; \frac{e^{j\theta} - 1}{e^{j\theta} + 1} \;=\; \frac{e^{j\theta/2}(e^{j\theta/2} - e^{-j\theta/2})}{e^{j\theta/2}(e^{j\theta/2} + e^{-j\theta/2})} \;=\; j\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

This is **purely imaginary** for all real $\theta$. ✓

**Step 3.** Interior of unit disc $\vert z \vert < 1$ maps to LHP ($\mathrm{Re}(w) < 0$):
- Take $z = r\,e^{j\theta}$ with $r < 1$:
$$w \;=\; \frac{r e^{j\theta} - 1}{r e^{j\theta} + 1}$$
- Algebra shows $\mathrm{Re}(w) = \dfrac{r^2 - 1}{\vert r e^{j\theta} + 1\vert^2} < 0$. ✓

**Step 4.** Stability check procedure:
1. Take characteristic polynomial $F(z) = a_0 + a_1 z + \dots + a_n z^n$.
2. Substitute $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ to obtain $F_w(w) = b_0 + b_1 w + \dots + b_n w^n$.
3. Build Routh array on $F_w(w)$.
4. All first-column entries $> 0$ ⇒ closed-loop stable in z-domain.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Jury's Stability Test (Complete Procedure)

**Given:** $F(z) = a_0 + a_1 z + a_2 z^2 + \dots + a_n z^n$ with $a_n > 0$.

**Necessary & Sufficient Conditions for all roots inside unit circle:**

| Row | Condition | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_1$ | $F(1) > 0$ |
| 2 | $C_2$ | $F(-1) > 0$ for $n$ even; $F(-1) < 0$ for $n$ odd |
| 3 | $C_3$ | $\vert a_0 \vert < a_n$ |
| 4+ | $C_4, \dots, C_{n}$ | Built from Jury table reductions |

**Jury Table Construction:**

For row index $k$, define coefficients recursively. The classic Jury table for $F(z) = a_0 + a_1 z + a_2 z^2 + a_3 z^3$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | Col 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_0$ | $a_1$ | $a_2$ | $a_3$ |
| 2 | $a_3$ | $a_2$ | $a_1$ | $a_0$ |
| 3 | $b_0$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | 0 |
| 4 | $b_2$ | $b_1$ | $b_0$ | 0 |
| 5 | $c_0$ | $c_1$ | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | $c_1$ | $c_0$ | 0 | 0 |
| 7 | $d_0$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |

where:
$$b_0 = a_3 a_0 - a_0 a_3 = a_3^2 - a_0^2, \quad b_1 = a_3 a_1 - a_0 a_2, \quad b_2 = a_3 a_2 - a_0 a_1$$
(and analogously for $c$, $d$).

**Final stability conditions:**
1. $F(1) > 0$
2. $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$
3. $\vert a_0 \vert < a_n$
4. $\vert b_0 \vert > \vert b_{n-1} \vert$
5. $\vert c_0 \vert > \vert c_{n-2} \vert$
6. $\vert d_0 \vert > \vert d_1 \vert$ (for cubic: $d_0 > 0$)

If all hold, the polynomial has all roots strictly inside the unit disc.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Jury's Test for a Cubic

**Problem:** Test stability of $F(z) = z^3 + 0.5 z^2 - 0.4 z + 0.1$.

**Identify coefficients:** $a_0 = 0.1$, $a_1 = -0.4$, $a_2 = 0.5$, $a_3 = 1$.

**Step 1:** $F(1) = 1 + 0.5 - 0.4 + 0.1 = 1.2 > 0$ ✓

**Step 2:** $n = 3$ (odd), so need $F(-1) < 0$.
$F(-1) = -1 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.0$
$0 < 0$ is **false** (it equals zero). ✗

**Conclusion:** System is **not stable** (pole on the unit circle at $z = -1$).

---

## 3.8 Worked Example: Bilinear + Routh–Hurwitz

**Problem:** $F(z) = z^2 - 0.5 z + 0.06$. Test stability.

**Step 1.** Apply $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$:
$$F_w(w) \;=\; \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^{\!2} - 0.5\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.06$$

**Step 2.** Multiply by $(1-w)^2$:
$$F_w(w) \;=\; (1+w)^2 - 0.5(1+w)(1-w) + 0.06(1-w)^2$$

**Step 3.** Expand:
- $(1+w)^2 = 1 + 2w + w^2$
- $(1+w)(1-w) = 1 - w^2$
- $(1-w)^2 = 1 - 2w + w^2$

So:
$$F_w(w) \;=\; (1 + 2w + w^2) - 0.5(1 - w^2) + 0.06(1 - 2w + w^2)$$

**Step 4.** Collect terms:
- Constant: $1 - 0.5 + 0.06 = 0.56$
- $w$ coefficient: $2 - 0.12 = 1.88$
- $w^2$ coefficient: $1 + 0.5 + 0.06 = 1.56$

$$F_w(w) \;=\; 0.56 + 1.88 w + 1.56 w^2 \;=\; 0.56\,(1 + 3.357 w + 2.786 w^2)$$

**Step 5.** Routh array for $1.56 w^2 + 1.88 w + 0.56$:

| | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $w^2$ | 1.56 | 0.56 |
| $w^1$ | 1.88 | 0 |
| $w^0$ | 0.56 | |

**Step 6.** First column: $1.56, 1.88, 0.56$ — **all positive** ⇒ LHP roots in $w$ ⇒ **stable in z-domain**.

(Verify: roots of $F(z)$ are $z = 0.3, 0.2$, both $\vert z \vert < 1$ ✓.)

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**State the Sampling Theorem. What is the Nyquist rate? What is the consequence of violating the Nyquist criterion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Sampling Theorem (1 mark):** A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with no frequency components above $\omega_m$ rad/sec can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(kT)$ provided the sampling frequency $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$.

> **Nyquist Rate (1 mark):** The minimum sampling rate that just satisfies the theorem is $\omega_N = 2\omega_m$ rad/sec (or $f_N = 2 f_m$ Hz). The corresponding sampling period is $T_N = \pi/\omega_m$.

> **Consequence of violation (1 mark):** If $\omega_s < 2\omega_m$, the periodic replicas of the spectrum overlap, causing **aliasing** — high-frequency components masquerade as lower-frequency ones, and the original signal *cannot* be uniquely recovered.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) and derive its transfer function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **ZOH Definition (1 mark):** A ZOH is a data reconstruction device that holds the value of each sample $x(kT)$ constant over the interval $kT \leq t < (k+1)T$, producing a staircase approximation of the original signal.

> **Impulse Response (1 mark):** $h_{h0}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)$.

> **Transfer Function (1 mark):** $\mathcal{L}\{u(t) - u(t-T)\} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$. Hence $G_{h0}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the **sampling theorem** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the effect of choosing a sampling rate **higher** than and **lower** than the Nyquist rate. What practical considerations dictate the choice of $\omega_s$ in a real digital control system? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a sampled-data control system with the open-loop pulse transfer function
$$G(z) \, D(z) \;=\; \frac{0.368\,z + 0.264}{(z - 1)(z - 0.368)}$$
determine the **closed-loop stability** using (i) Jury's test, and (ii) the bilinear transformation with Routh–Hurwitz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sampling Theorem & Sampling Rate Choice (7 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):**
- A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with $\omega_{max} = \omega_m$ can be uniquely recovered from its samples $x(kT)$ iff
$$\omega_s \;\geq\; 2\,\omega_m$$
where $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ is the sampling frequency and $T$ is the sampling period.

**Block Diagram of Sampling & Reconstruction:**
1. Continuous signal $x(t)$ → Sampler → Impulse train $x^*(t)$ → Ideal low-pass filter → $\hat{x}(t)$.

**Effects (3 marks):**
- **$\omega_s > 2\omega_m$ (oversampling):** Spectral replicas are well separated; easier reconstruction, but uses more bandwidth, more A/D samples, more compute. Practical anti-aliasing filter is relaxed.
- **$\omega_s = 2\omega_m$ (critical):** Replicas just touch; ideal sinc reconstruction is theoretically possible but extremely sensitive to filter imperfections.
- **$\omega_s < 2\omega_m$ (undersampling / aliasing):** Replicas overlap; high-frequency content folds back into the baseband — information is **permanently lost**. Cannot be reversed.

**Practical Considerations (2 marks):**
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** $\omega_s$ chosen $5$–$10\times$ the signal bandwidth so that a realizable filter rolls off sharply.
- **Closed-loop performance:** Sampling rate should be $\geq 10\times$ the closed-loop bandwidth for negligible discretisation effects.
- **Hardware cost:** Higher $\omega_s$ → costlier A/D, D/A, and processor.
- **Computation time per sample:** $T$ must exceed worst-case control algorithm runtime.
- **Plant dynamics:** If the plant has fast modes near $\omega_s/2$, they alias; must be addressed.

**(b) Stability Analysis (7 marks):**

**Characteristic Equation:**
$$1 + G(z)D(z) \;=\; 0$$
$$1 + \frac{0.368\,z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)} \;=\; 0$$
$$(z-1)(z-0.368) + 0.368\,z + 0.264 \;=\; 0$$
$$z^2 - 1.368\,z + 0.368 + 0.368\,z + 0.264 \;=\; 0$$
$$z^2 - z + 0.632 \;=\; 0$$

So the characteristic polynomial is $F(z) = z^2 - z + 0.632 = 0$, with $a_0 = 0.632$, $a_1 = -1$, $a_2 = 1$.

**(i) Jury's Test (3 marks):**

| Condition | Evaluation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $F(1) > 0$ | $1 - 1 + 0.632 = 0.632$ | $> 0$ ✓ |
| $F(-1) > 0$ (n=2 even) | $1 + 1 + 0.632 = 2.632$ | $> 0$ ✓ |
| $\vert a_0 \vert < a_n$ | $\vert 0.632 \vert < 1$ | ✓ |

All three Jury conditions are satisfied ⇒ **closed-loop stable**.

(Direct verification: roots of $z^2 - z + 0.632 = 0$ are $z = 0.5 \pm j\,0.617$, both with $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.381} = \sqrt{0.631} \approx 0.794 < 1$ ✓.)

**(ii) Bilinear + Routh–Hurwitz (4 marks):**

Substitute $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$:
$$F(z) \;=\; \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^{\!2} - \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.632$$

Multiply through by $(1-w)^2$:
$$(1+w)^2 - (1+w)(1-w) + 0.632\,(1-w)^2 \;=\; 0$$

Expand:
- $(1+w)^2 = 1 + 2w + w^2$
- $(1+w)(1-w) = 1 - w^2$
- $(1-w)^2 = 1 - 2w + w^2$

$$(1 + 2w + w^2) - (1 - w^2) + 0.632(1 - 2w + w^2) \;=\; 0$$
$$1 + 2w + w^2 - 1 + w^2 + 0.632 - 1.264 w + 0.632 w^2 \;=\; 0$$
$$(1 + 0.632) + (2 - 1.264)w + (1 + 1 + 0.632)w^2 \;=\; 0$$
$$1.632 + 0.736 w + 2.632 w^2 \;=\; 0$$

Equivalently $F_w(w) = 2.632 w^2 + 0.736 w + 1.632$.

**Routh Array:**

| | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $w^2$ | $2.632$ | $1.632$ |
| $w^1$ | $0.736$ | $0$ |
| $w^0$ | $1.632$ | |

First column: $2.632, 0.736, 1.632$ — **all positive** ⇒ no sign changes ⇒ **all w-plane roots in LHP** ⇒ **closed-loop stable** in z-domain.

**Conclusion:** Both methods agree — the system is **stable**.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** in a digital control system. Derive the pulse transfer function of a system consisting of a ZOH in cascade with a plant
$$G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{s(s+1)}$$
for a sampling period $T = 1$ s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mapping between the s-plane and the z-plane**. Show that the **left half of the s-plane** maps to the **interior of the unit circle** in the z-plane. State the **BIBO stability criterion** for a discrete-time system and verify it for the pulse transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{z + 0.5}{(z - 0.8)(z - 0.3)}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ZOH Block Diagram & PTF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
```
[Discrete input x*(t)] → [ZOH] → [Continuous plant Gp(s)] → [Output y(t)]
```
The ZOH holds each sampled value flat until the next sample arrives, providing a piecewise-constant actuation signal to the plant.

**ZOH Transfer Function (1 mark):**
$$G_{h0}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Pulse Transfer Function (4 marks):**
$$G(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

Compute $\dfrac{G_p(s)}{s} = \dfrac{1}{s^2(s+1)}$.

**Partial Fractions:**
$$\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)} \;=\; \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^2} + \frac{C}{s+1}$$

Multiply by $s^2(s+1)$:
$$1 \;=\; A\,s(s+1) + B(s+1) + C\,s^2$$
$$1 \;=\; (A + C)s^2 + (A + B)s + B$$

Equate coefficients:
- $s^0$: $B = 1$
- $s^1$: $A + B = 0 \Rightarrow A = -1$
- $s^2$: $A + C = 0 \Rightarrow C = 1$

So:
$$\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)} \;=\; -\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{1}{s+1}$$

**z-Transforms (with $T = 1$):**
$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s}\right\} = \frac{z}{z-1}, \quad \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^2}\right\} = \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}, \quad \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s+1}\right\} = \frac{z}{z - e^{-T}}$$

With $T = 1$, $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$:
$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} = -\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{z}{z - 0.368}$$

**Combine over common denominator** $(z-1)^2(z-0.368)$:
$$= \frac{-z(z-1)(z-0.368) + z(z-0.368) + z(z-1)^2}{(z-1)^2(z-0.368)}$$

Expand:
- $-z(z-1)(z-0.368) = -z(z^2 - 1.368z + 0.368) = -z^3 + 1.368z^2 - 0.368z$
- $z(z-0.368) = z^2 - 0.368z$
- $z(z-1)^2 = z(z^2 - 2z + 1) = z^3 - 2z^2 + z$

Sum:
$$(-z^3 + 1.368z^2 - 0.368z) + (z^2 - 0.368z) + (z^3 - 2z^2 + z)$$
$$= 0\cdot z^3 + (1.368 + 1 - 2)z^2 + (-0.368 - 0.368 + 1)z$$
$$= 0.368 z^2 + 0.264 z \;=\; z\,(0.368 z + 0.264)$$

Therefore:
$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} = \frac{z(0.368z + 0.264)}{(z-1)^2(z-0.368)}$$

**Apply $(1 - z^{-1})$ factor** (equivalently multiply numerator by $(1-z^{-1}) = (z-1)/z$):
$$G(z) \;=\; \frac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \frac{z(0.368z + 0.264)}{(z-1)^2(z-0.368)}$$

$$\boxed{\,G(z) \;=\; \frac{0.368z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}\,}$$

This is a **textbook canonical result** for the canonical second-order plant with ZOH.

**(b) s-z Mapping and BIBO Stability (7 marks):**

**Mapping Derivation (4 marks):**
Starting from the fundamental relationship:
$$z \;=\; e^{sT} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \;=\; \sigma + j\omega \;\Rightarrow\; z = e^{\sigma T}\,e^{j\omega T}$$

Hence:
- $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$
- $\angle z = \omega T \pmod{2\pi}$

**LHP ↔ Interior of unit circle:**
For $s$ in the LHP, $\sigma < 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T} < e^0 = 1$. Hence the LHP maps strictly inside the unit disc. Conversely, $\vert z \vert < 1 \Rightarrow \sigma < 0$, confirming the bijection.

**BIBO Stability Criterion (1 mark):**
> A discrete-time LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if** all poles of its pulse transfer function lie strictly inside the unit circle, $\vert z_i \vert < 1$.

**Verification for $G(z) = \dfrac{z + 0.5}{(z - 0.8)(z - 0.3)}$ (2 marks):**

Poles: $z_1 = 0.8$, $z_2 = 0.3$.
- $\vert z_1 \vert = 0.8 < 1$ ✓
- $\vert z_2 \vert = 0.3 < 1$ ✓

Both poles are strictly inside the unit circle ⇒ the system is **BIBO stable**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this module (from KTU 2024 valuation key patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\omega_s$ with $f_s$ (rad/s vs Hz):** Always check units. $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1 - z^{-1})$ factor** when computing $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G_{ZOH}G_p(s)\}$. Use the standard formula $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$ — *never* $\mathcal{Z}\{G_{h0}(s)G_p(s)\}$ directly. (−2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing Jury's parity condition for $F(-1)$:** $n$ even ⇒ $F(-1) > 0$; $n$ odd ⇒ $F(-1) < 0$. Many students remember one but not the other. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Bilinear substitution error:** Forgetting to multiply through by $(1-w)^n$ to clear the denominator *before* building the Routh array. (−2 marks)
> 5. **Configuration confusion:** Students often assume Configuration 1 and Configuration 2 give the same closed-loop PTF — they do *not*. (−2 marks)
> 6. **Stability region confusion:** The unit circle, **not** the origin, is the stability boundary. A pole at the origin is *fine*; a pole on the unit circle is **marginally unstable**. (−1 mark)
> 7. **Skipping partial fractions** in $G_p(s)/s$ expansions — examiners will mark each missing term (−1 mark each).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Sampling Theorem & Impulse Sampling
- Sampling theorem: $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$; minimum rate is the **Nyquist rate**.
- Impulse-sampled spectrum is **periodic** with period $\omega_s$ and scaled by $1/T$.
- **Aliasing** is irreversible information loss.

### Sampling Rate Selection
- Choose $\omega_s \approx 5$–$10\times$ signal bandwidth in practice.
- For closed-loop control, $\omega_s \geq 10\,\omega_{BW}$.

### Data Hold
- **ZOH:** Holds value constant; $G_{h0}(s) = (1-e^{-sT})/s$. Introduces $\sim T/2$ delay.
- **FOH:** Linear extrapolation; smoother but has slope discontinuities; less common in industry.
- ZOH is the **default** in nearly all real DACs.

### Pulse Transfer Function
- $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$ for ZOH-then-plant.
- $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}$ for sampler-then-plant without hold.
- Configuration 1 and 3 ⇒ same PTF. Configuration 2 is **asymmetric**.

### s-plane ↔ z-plane Mapping
- $z = e^{sT}$, $s = (1/T)\ln z$.
- $\sigma < 0 \Leftrightarrow \vert z \vert < 1$.
- Primary strip $-\pi/T \leq \omega \leq \pi/T$ maps to one full traversal of the unit circle.

### Stability Tests
- **BIBO stable** ⟺ all poles inside $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- **Jury's test:** Tabular, no root finding; conditions $F(1) > 0$, $F(-1)$ sign rule, $\vert a_0 \vert < a_n$, plus table reductions.
- **Schur–Cohn test:** Determinant-based; equivalent to Jury's.
- **Bilinear + Routh–Hurwitz:** Substitute $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$, build Routh array, all-positive first column ⇒ stable.

### Engineering Intuition
- Discrete-time stability is geometrically a **circle**, not a vertical line.
- The bilinear transformation is the *Rosetta Stone* that lets the entire continuous-time stability toolkit apply to discrete-time problems.
- ZOH is the silent partner of every digital control loop — its delay and low-pass character shape closed-loop performance.

### Quick Numerical Crib
- $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$, $e^{-2} \approx 0.135$, $1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$.
- For $G_p(s) = 1/[s(s+1)]$ with ZOH and $T=1$: $G(z) = (0.368z + 0.264)/[(z-1)(z-0.368)]$ — *memorise* this canonical form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Design Based on Root Locus: Design of Lag Compensator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET744 - DISCRETE TIME CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Design Based on Root Locus: Design of Lag Compensator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lag Compensator** in the discrete-time domain is a causal compensator whose transfer function $D(z)$ has a **pole closer to the unit circle boundary ($z=1$)** than its zero, i.e., it introduces a *lag* in the phase response at the lower frequencies while providing a substantial boost to the **low-frequency (DC) gain**.

The general form of a discrete-time lag compensator is:

$$D(z) = K_c \cdot \frac{z - z_c}{z - p_c}, \quad 0 < p_c < z_c < 1$$

where:
- $z_c$ is the compensator **zero** (closer to $z = 1$)
- $p_c$ is the compensator **pole** (further from $z = 1$)
- $K_c$ is the compensator gain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the *direct root-locus design method* of a lag compensator, we operate directly in the **z-plane** (not the w-plane), use the angle and magnitude conditions of the root locus, and place the zero-pole pair so close to $z=1$ that their angular contribution at the desired closed-loop pole is *negligible* (typically $< 5°$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Lag"?** A pole-zero pair with the pole slower (closer to $z=1$ in discrete-time / closer to origin in s-plane) than the zero produces a phase *lag* (negative phase contribution) at moderate frequencies. The "direct design" route means we skip bilinear transformation to w-plane and design straight in z-plane.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control Steering Wheel:** Imagine you are driving a car with a *sluggish steering* (small transient response lag). To fix the steady-state drift without changing how the car feels while turning, you tighten the steering mechanism's *restoring spring* just a little. The driver still experiences roughly the same steering feel during the turn, but the car tracks the lane much more accurately on long straight roads. The **lag compensator** does exactly this in a discrete control system: it **boosts the steady-state tracking accuracy** (straight-road accuracy) without disturbing the **transient response** (the "feel" of the turn).

### 1.3 Primary Engineering Purpose

The lag compensator is employed when:
- Transient response (damping, settling time) of the uncompensated system is already **acceptable**, OR can be made acceptable by a small **gain $K$** at the desired pole.
- The **steady-state error specification** is *not* satisfied at that $K$.
- Goal: increase the **error constant** ($K_p$, $K_v$, or $K_a$) by a factor of $1/\alpha$ while leaving the transient response almost intact.

### 1.4 DC Gain (Low-Frequency Boost) — Key Property

The DC gain (low-frequency gain in z-domain corresponds to behavior near $z=1$):

$$\lim_{z \to 1} D(z) = \frac{1 - z_c}{1 - p_c} = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

where the lag ratio $\alpha$ is defined as:

$$\alpha = \frac{1 - p_c}{1 - z_c}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1 \;\; (\text{typical: } 0.01 \leq \alpha \leq 0.1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Steady-state error constant improvement factor = $1/\alpha$**. For example, choosing $\alpha = 0.1$ boosts $K_v$ by a factor of 10.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — Why Lag Compensator Works on Root Locus

The design relies on the **angle condition** and **magnitude condition** of the z-plane root locus:

**Angle condition:** 
$$\angle G(z) + \angle D(z) = \pm 180° \cdot (2k+1)$$

**Magnitude condition:** 
$$\vert K \cdot G(z) \cdot D(z) \vert = 1$$

**Key insight:** By placing the zero-pole pair of $D(z)$ *very close* to $z = 1$, the **vector from $z_c$ to the desired pole** and the **vector from $p_c$ to the desired pole** are nearly **parallel and almost equal in length**. Therefore:

1. **Angular contribution** of $D(z)$ at the desired pole $\approx 0°$
2. **Magnitude contribution** of $D(z)$ at the desired pole $\approx 1$
3. The compensated root locus near the desired pole is **almost identical** to the uncompensated locus
4. The gain $K$ required is therefore **almost unchanged**
5. But the **DC gain is multiplied by $1/\alpha$** → steady-state error improves dramatically

### 2.2 Effect on Steady-State Error Constants

For a Type-1 plant (one pole at $z = 1$):

$$K_v^{\text{comp}} = \frac{1}{T} \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) \, K \cdot G(z) \cdot D(z) = \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot K_v^{\text{uncomp}}$$

Similar relations hold for $K_p$ and $K_a$ as the lag pole-zero pair contributes a factor of $1/\alpha$ to whichever error constant is dominant.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Lag Compensator | $D(z) = K_c \dfrac{z - z_c}{z - p_c}$ | $0 < p_c < z_c < 1$ |
| Lag Ratio | $\alpha = \dfrac{1 - p_c}{1 - z_c}$ | Typically $0.01 \le \alpha \le 0.1$ |
| DC Gain Boost | $\lim_{z \to 1} D(z) = 1/\alpha$ | Multiplies all error constants |
| Angle Condition (z-plane) | $\angle G(z) + \angle D(z) = \pm 180°(2k+1)$ | For $k = 0$: $\pm 180°$ |
| Magnitude Condition | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(z) \cdot D(z) \vert}$ | Evaluated at desired pole $z_d$ |
| Velocity Error Constant | $K_v = \dfrac{1}{T} \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) G(z)$ | Type-1 plant; units: $s^{-1}$ |
| Position Error Constant | $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ | Type-0 plant; dimensionless |
| Acceleration Error Constant | $K_a = \dfrac{1}{T^2} \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)^2 G(z)$ | Type-2 plant; units: $s^{-2}$ |
| s→z Mapping | $z = e^{sT}$ | $T$ = sampling period (seconds) |
| Damping from z-pole | $\zeta = -\dfrac{\ln \vert z \vert}{\sqrt{(\ln \vert z \vert)^2 + (\angle z)^2}}$ | $\angle z$ in radians |
| Settling Time (2%) | $T_s \approx \dfrac{4 T}{\vert \ln \vert z \vert \vert}$ | Discrete-time approximation |
| Damped Frequency | $\omega_d = \dfrac{\angle z}{T}$ | rad/s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lag compensators are heavily used in:
- **Servo motor position control** (improving tracking accuracy in CNC machines)
- **Disk drive head positioning** (steady-state accuracy crucial for track following)
- **Antenna pointing systems** (radar/satellite dishes need precise steady-state pointing)
- **Process control** (chemical plants where steady-state setpoint accuracy is paramount)

They are favored over lead compensators when **speed of response is not the bottleneck** but **accuracy** is.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Design Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Design Procedure — The 7 Logical Steps

**Step 1 — Translate time-domain specs to s-plane**
Using standard second-order relations:
$$T_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}, \quad \%OS = 100 e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Solve for $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$, then form the desired s-plane pole:
$$s_d = -\zeta \omega_n \pm j \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

**Step 2 — Map desired s-plane pole to z-plane**
$$z_d = e^{s_d T}$$

Express $z_d$ in polar form: $z_d = \vert z_d \vert \, e^{j \theta_d}$ where $\theta_d = \omega_d T$.

**Step 3 — Verify whether uncompensated system can place pole at $z_d$**
Check the angle condition for the uncompensated plant:
$$\angle G(z_d) \stackrel{?}{=} \pm 180°(2k+1)$$

If yes (within tolerance), find uncompensated gain $K_1$ from magnitude condition:
$$K_1 = \frac{1}{\vert G(z_d) \vert}$$

If no → add a **lead compensator first** (not covered in this module's focus).

**Step 4 — Compute uncompensated error constant $K_v^{\text{uncomp}}$ (or whichever applies)**
$$K_v^{\text{uncomp}} = \frac{1}{T} \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) G(z)$$

**Step 5 — Determine required boost factor $1/\alpha$**
$$\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{K_v^{\text{required}}}{K_v^{\text{uncomp}}}$$

If $K_v^{\text{uncomp}}$ already exceeds the spec, no lag compensator is needed.

**Step 6 — Place the zero-pole pair of $D(z)$**
Choose the zero $z_c$ very close to $z = 1$ (e.g., $z_c = 0.99$), then:
$$p_c = 1 - \alpha(1 - z_c)$$

Verify angular contribution at $z_d$:
$$\Delta \phi_D = \angle (z_d - z_c) - \angle (z_d - p_c) \quad \text{should satisfy} \quad \vert \Delta \phi_D \vert < 5°$$

**Step 7 — Compute the new gain $K_2$ from the magnitude condition**
$$K_2 = \frac{1}{\vert G(z_d) \cdot D(z_d) \vert}$$

**Step 8 — Verify** transient response (using digital simulation or z-plane root locus) and steady-state error.

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example

> **Plant & Specifications**
> 
> Plant (unity feedback, $T = 1$ s):
> $$G(z) = \frac{0.5}{(z - 1)(z - 0.4)}$$
> 
> Specifications:
> - Damping ratio: $\zeta = 0.6$
> - Natural frequency: $\omega_n = 0.5$ rad/s
> - Sampling period: $T = 1$ s
> - Velocity error constant: $K_v \ge 1.5$

**Step 1 — Translate to s-plane**

$$\zeta \omega_n = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.3$$

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 0.5 \sqrt{1 - 0.36} = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$s_d = -0.3 \pm j 0.4$$

**Step 2 — Map to z-plane**

$$\vert z_d \vert = e^{\sigma_d T} = e^{-0.3 \times 1} = e^{-0.3} = 0.7408$$

$$\theta_d = \omega_d T = 0.4 \times 1 = 0.4 \text{ rad}$$

$$z_d = 0.7408 \, e^{j 0.4} = 0.7408 (\cos 0.4 + j \sin 0.4)$$

$$z_d = 0.7408 (0.9211 + j 0.3894) = 0.6824 + j 0.2885$$

**Step 3 — Angle condition check (uncompensated)**

Evaluate the four vectors at $z_d$:

$$z_d - 1 = -0.3176 + j 0.2885, \quad \vert z_d - 1 \vert = \sqrt{0.1009 + 0.0832} = 0.4292$$

$$\angle (z_d - 1) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2885}{0.3176}\right) = 180° - 42.27° = 137.73°$$

$$z_d - 0.4 = 0.2824 + j 0.2885, \quad \vert z_d - 0.4 \vert = \sqrt{0.0797 + 0.0832} = 0.4033$$

$$\angle (z_d - 0.4) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2885}{0.2824}\right) = 45.62°$$

Net angle of $G(z_d)$:

$$\angle G(z_d) = 0° - 137.73° - 45.62° = -183.35°$$

Since $-183.35° \approx -180°$ (within tolerance, as we are operating near the desired pole on the uncompensated root locus), the uncompensated system **can** place a pole at $z_d$. ✓

**Step 4 — Uncompensated gain $K_1$ from magnitude condition**

$$K_1 = \frac{1}{\vert G(z_d) \vert} = \frac{\vert z_d - 1 \vert \cdot \vert z_d - 0.4 \vert}{0.5}$$

$$K_1 = \frac{0.4292 \times 0.4033}{0.5} = \frac{0.1731}{0.5} = 0.3462$$

**Step 5 — Compute uncompensated velocity error constant**

$$K_v^{\text{uncomp}} = \frac{1}{T} \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) G(z) = 1 \times \frac{0.5}{(1 - 0.4)} = \frac{0.5}{0.6} = 0.833$$

Required: $K_v \ge 1.5$. So we need a boost factor of:

$$\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1.5}{0.833} = 1.80 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha = 0.556$$

To get a *clean* design (good margin), we typically round up and select $\alpha = 0.1$ (boost factor of 10). This gives $K_v^{\text{new}} = 8.33$, comfortably above 1.5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's choice:** Picking $\alpha = 0.1$ over the theoretical 0.556 gives engineering margin against modeling errors. The trade-off: a larger $\alpha$ requires placing the lag pole *very* close to $z = 1$ (practical realizability concern with finite-precision arithmetic).

**Step 6 — Place zero-pole pair of $D(z)$**

Choose $z_c = 0.99$ (very close to $z = 1$):

$$p_c = 1 - \alpha(1 - z_c) = 1 - 0.1 (1 - 0.99) = 1 - 0.1 \times 0.01 = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999$$

So $D(z) = \dfrac{z - 0.99}{z - 0.999}$

**Verify angular contribution at $z_d$:**

$$z_d - 0.99 = 0.6824 - 0.99 + j 0.2885 = -0.3076 + j 0.2885$$

$$\vert z_d - 0.99 \vert = \sqrt{0.0946 + 0.0832} = 0.4221$$

$$\angle (z_d - 0.99) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2885}{0.3076}\right) = 180° - 43.16° = 136.84°$$

$$z_d - 0.999 = 0.6824 - 0.999 + j 0.2885 = -0.3166 + j 0.2885$$

$$\vert z_d - 0.999 \vert = \sqrt{0.1002 + 0.0832} = 0.4292$$

$$\angle (z_d - 0.999) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.2885}{0.3166}\right) = 180° - 42.34° = 137.66°$$

$$\Delta \phi_D = 136.84° - 137.66° = -0.82° \quad (\text{Excellent — within } 5° \text{ tolerance})$$ ✓

$$\vert D(z_d) \vert = \frac{0.4221}{0.4292} = 0.9834 \quad (\text{Close to 1 — transient response preserved})$$ ✓

**Step 7 — New gain $K_2$ from magnitude condition**

$$K_2 = \frac{1}{\vert G(z_d) \vert \cdot \vert D(z_d) \vert} = \frac{1}{2.890 \times 0.9834} = \frac{1}{2.842} = 0.3519$$

Note: $K_2 \approx K_1 = 0.3462$ (only 1.65% change), confirming the lag compensator does not significantly alter the transient response.

**Step 8 — Verify new $K_v$**

$$K_v^{\text{comp}} = \frac{1}{T} \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot K_2 \cdot G(z) \cdot D(z)$$

The $(z-1)$ term cancels the plant pole at $z = 1$:

$$K_v^{\text{comp}} = K_2 \times \frac{0.5}{(1 - 0.4)} \times \frac{(1 - 0.99)}{(1 - 0.999)}$$

$$K_v^{\text{comp}} = 0.3519 \times \frac{0.5}{0.6} \times \frac{0.01}{0.001}$$

$$K_v^{\text{comp}} = 0.3519 \times 0.8333 \times 10 = 0.3519 \times 8.333 = 2.933$$

$$K_v^{\text{comp}} = 2.933 \ge 1.5 \;\; \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Final Compensated Design Summary

$$\boxed{\,D(z) = 0.3519 \cdot \frac{z - 0.99}{z - 0.999}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Table — Before vs After Compensation**
> 
> | Parameter | Uncompensated | Compensated | Spec |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Damping $\zeta$ | 0.6 | $\approx 0.6$ | 0.6 ✓ |
> | Natural freq $\omega_n$ (rad/s) | 0.5 | $\approx 0.5$ | 0.5 ✓ |
> | Settling time $T_s$ (s) | $\approx 4$ | $\approx 4$ | — |
> | Velocity const $K_v$ | 0.833 | 2.933 | $\ge 1.5$ ✓ |
> | Gain $K$ | 0.3462 | 0.3519 | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Unity Feedback Discrete-Time System with Lag Compensator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input R of z] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B --> C[D of z, Lag Compensator]
    C --> D[Zero Order Hold, G of z, Plant]
    D --> E[Output C of z]
    E --> F1[Feedback Path, unity]
    F1 --> B
    G[Clock, period T] -.-> C
    G -.-> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The reference input $R(z)$ enters the summing junction.
- The error $E(z) = R(z) - C(z)$ is fed into the lag compensator $D(z)$.
- $D(z)$ drives the discrete plant $G(z)$ (which includes the ZOH).
- The output $C(z)$ is fed back with unity gain.
- The clock signal (sampling period $T$) synchronizes the digital elements.

### 4.2 Design Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Translate time specs to s plane, zeta, omega_n] --> stepB[Step 2: Map s to z using z = exp sT]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Check angle condition of uncompensated G of z at z_d]
    stepC --> stepD{Angle approx equal to 180 deg?}
    stepD -- No --> stepE[Use lead compensator first, then return to lag]
    stepD -- Yes --> stepF[Step 4: Compute K1 from magnitude condition]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 5: Compute K_v_uncomp and required 1 by alpha factor]
    stepG --> stepH{Uncompensated K_v sufficient?}
    stepH -- Yes --> stepI[No lag compensator needed, stop]
    stepH -- No --> stepJ[Step 6: Choose z_c near 1, compute p_c = 1 minus alpha times 1 minus z_c]
    stepJ --> stepK[Verify angular contribution of D at z_d is less than 5 deg]
    stepK --> stepL{Angular check passed?}
    stepL -- No --> stepJ
    stepL -- Yes --> stepM[Step 7: Compute K2 from new magnitude condition]
    stepM --> stepN[Step 8: Verify K_v_comp and transient response by simulation]
```

### 4.3 Pole-Zero Placement Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pt0[ORIGIN, z = 0]
    ptA[Pole of G, z = 0.4]
    ptB[Pole of G, z = 1, integrator]
    ptC[Compensator Zero, z = 0.99, close to 1]
    ptD[Compensator Pole, z = 0.999, very close to 1]
    ptE[Desired Closed Loop Pole, z_d, dominant]
    pt0 --- ptA
    ptA --- ptB
    ptC --- ptD
    ptB --- ptC
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization takeaway:** Notice that the compensator zero and pole are clustered **very near the unit circle boundary at $z=1$**, while the desired closed-loop pole $z_d$ sits well *inside* the unit circle. The angle subtended at $z_d$ by this zero-pole cluster is tiny, hence root locus shape near $z_d$ is virtually undisturbed.

### 4.4 Frequency-Domain Interpretation (s-plane Lag Analog)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp1[Origin, s = 0] --> sp2[Pole at s = 0, slow]
    sp3[Zero at s = 10, faster] --> sp4[Compensator Zero, s = minus 0.01, close to origin]
    sp4 --> sp5[Compensator Pole, s = minus 0.001, very close to origin]
    sp5 --> sp6[DC gain boost = 1 by alpha = 10]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> 
> **What is a lag compensator? Justify why its zero is placed closer to $z=1$ than its pole in the discrete-time z-plane.**

**Model Answer:**

A lag compensator is a controller whose transfer function $D(z) = K_c \dfrac{z - z_c}{z - p_c}$ has a zero $z_c$ closer to $z = 1$ than the pole $p_c$ (i.e., $p_c < z_c < 1$). This arrangement is called "lag" because the pole is *slower* than the zero, producing a phase lag at moderate frequencies.

The zero is placed closer to $z = 1$ than the pole for two reasons:
1. **Steady-state error reduction:** The DC gain of the compensator is $\dfrac{1 - z_c}{1 - p_c} = 1/\alpha > 1$, which directly multiplies the system error constant.
2. **Minimal transient disturbance:** When $z_c$ and $p_c$ are both placed very close to $z = 1$, the angular contribution at the desired closed-loop pole is very small ($\Delta \phi_D < 5°$). Thus the root locus shape near the dominant pole is preserved, and the transient response is virtually unaffected.

---
> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> 
> **State any three design steps involved in the direct root-locus design of a discrete-time lag compensator.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Translate time-domain specifications** ($\zeta$, $\omega_n$, $T_s$) **into a desired z-plane pole** $z_d = e^{s_d T}$ and verify the uncompensated system satisfies the angle condition at $z_d$.

2. **Compute the uncompensated error constant** $K_v^{\text{uncomp}}$ and the required boost factor $1/\alpha = K_v^{\text{required}} / K_v^{\text{uncomp}}$.

3. **Place the compensator zero** $z_c$ very close to $z = 1$ and compute the pole $p_c = 1 - \alpha(1 - z_c)$. Verify that the angular contribution $\Delta \phi_D = \angle (z_d - z_c) - \angle (z_d - p_c)$ is less than $5°$ at $z_d$.

4. **Compute the new gain** $K_2 = 1/[\vert G(z_d) \cdot D(z_d) \vert]$ using the magnitude condition.

5. **Verify** the new error constant and transient response.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **Q.3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (14 Marks)**
> 
> A unity feedback discrete-time control system has the plant:
> $$G(z) = \frac{0.5}{(z - 1)(z - 0.4)}, \quad T = 1 \text{ s}$$
> 
> Design a **lag compensator** using the direct root-locus method to meet the following specifications:
> - Damping ratio: $\zeta = 0.6$
> - Natural frequency: $\omega_n = 0.5$ rad/s
