## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Mapping s-Plane Specifications to z-Plane

**Goal:** Convert continuous-time transient specifications into the corresponding discrete-time closed-loop pole location $z_d$.

**Step 1 — Specify the desired continuous-time second-order response.**
Given $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$, the desired dominant continuous poles are:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\,\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\,\omega_d$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mapping $z = e^{sT}$.**
The mapping is exact only for ideal samplers and ZOH-free systems. For ZOH input, an additional delay $e^{-sT/2}$ is sometimes folded into $G(z)$; here we use the direct mapping for clarity.

$$\begin{aligned}
z_d &= e^{s_d T} \\
    &= e^{(-\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_d)T} \\
    &= e^{-\zeta\omega_n T}\cdot e^{j\omega_d T}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Decompose into magnitude and angle.**

$$\vert z_d\vert = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T}, \qquad \angle z_d = \omega_d T \;(\text{radians})$$

**Step 4 — Convert angle to degrees for hand calculation.**

$$\theta_d = \omega_d T \cdot \dfrac{180°}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Express the discrete pole in Cartesian form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
z_d &= e^{-\zeta\omega_n T}\big(\cos(\omega_d T) + j\sin(\omega_d T)\big) \\
    &= \underbrace{e^{-\zeta\omega_n T}\cos(\omega_d T)}_{\text{Re}(z_d)} \;+\; j\,\underbrace{e^{-\zeta\omega_n T}\sin(\omega_d T)}_{\text{Im}(z_d)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The discrete pole lies inside the unit circle, on the locus $r = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T}$, at angle $\omega_d T$ measured from the positive real axis.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Angle Condition for Lead Compensator Design

**Goal:** Determine the pole location $p_c$ of the compensator once a zero $z_c$ has been selected.

**Step 1 — Write the angle condition at $z = z_d$.**
For a point to lie on the root locus of $KG_c(z)G(z) = -1$, the total angle must equal an odd multiple of $180°$:

$$\angle G(z_d) + \angle(z_d - z_c) - \angle(z_d - p_c) = \pm 180° (2k+1)$$

**Step 2 — Define the angle deficiency.**
Let $\theta_G = \angle G(z_d)$ (the angle contributed by the plant alone). The compensator must supply:

$$\phi_m = \pm 180° - \theta_G \quad (\text{taken in } (-180°, 180°])$$

**Step 3 — Choose the compensator zero $z_c$.**
A common KTU-style choice is to place $z_c$ so that it cancels an undesirable plant pole $p_{\text{plant}}$ (provided $p_{\text{plant}}$ is real and inside the unit circle):

$$z_c = p_{\text{plant}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \angle(z_d - z_c) \text{ is known}$$

Alternatively, place $z_c$ slightly to the left of $z_d$ on the real axis (e.g., $z_c = \alpha\,\text{Re}(z_d)$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$).

**Step 4 — Solve for $p_c$ using the remaining angle.**
Let $\theta_1 = \angle(z_d - z_c)$ and $\theta_2 = \angle(z_d - p_c)$ (unknown). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 - \theta_2 &= \phi_m \\
\theta_2 &= \theta_1 - \phi_m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Geometric construction.**
Draw the ray from $z_c$ through $z_d$ and extend it backwards — the ray at angle $\theta_2$ from $z_d$ intersects the real axis at $p_c$.

$$\boxed{p_c = \text{Re}(z_d) - \dfrac{\text{Im}(z_d)}{\tan(\theta_2)}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify lead condition.**
Confirm $\vert p_c\vert > \vert z_c\vert$ and $0 < \vert p_c\vert < 1$. If violated, redesign by shifting $z_c$.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Magnitude Condition Solves the Gain K

**Goal:** Once $z_c$ and $p_c$ are fixed, compute the compensator gain $K$.

**Step 1 — Magnitude condition at $z = z_d$.**

$$\left\vert K \cdot \frac{z_d - z_c}{z_d - p_c} \cdot G(z_d) \right\vert = 1$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $K$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{1}{\vert G(z_d)\vert} \cdot \frac{\vert z_d - p_c\vert}{\vert z_d - z_c\vert}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each modulus.**
For complex $z_d$, use $\vert a + jb\vert = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert z_d - z_c\vert &= \sqrt{(\text{Re}(z_d) - z_c)^2 + \text{Im}(z_d)^2} \\
\vert z_d - p_c\vert &= \sqrt{(\text{Re}(z_d) - p_c)^2 + \text{Im}(z_d)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the steady-state error.**
Compute $K_p = \lim_{z\to 1}(1-z^{-1})G_c(z)G(z) \cdot K$ and ensure the required error constant is satisfied. If not, augment with an additional integrator (lag network), which is **outside the scope of pure lead design** but commonly tested in KTU exams.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Plant:**
$$G(z) = \frac{0.0484(z+0.717)}{(z-1)(z-0.606)}$$
Sampling period $T = 0.1$ s.

**Specifications:** $\zeta = 0.6$, $\omega_n = 5$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Compute desired $s$-plane pole.**

$$s_d = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_d = -3 + j(5\sqrt{1-0.36}) = -3 + j4$$

**Step 2 — Map to $z$-plane.**

$$\begin{aligned}
z_d &= e^{s_d T} = e^{-0.3 + j0.4} \\
    &= e^{-0.3}\big(\cos 0.4 + j\sin 0.4\big) \\
    &= 0.7408\,(0.9211 + j\,0.3894) \\
    &= 0.6824 + j\,0.2885
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude and angle of $z_d$.**

$$\vert z_d\vert = 0.7408, \qquad \angle z_d = 0.4\;\text{rad} = 22.92°$$

**Step 4 — Plant angle at $z_d$.**
For brevity, assume the calculation yields $\angle G(z_d) = -175°$. Then phase needed:

$$\phi_m = -180° - (-175°) = -5°$$
Equivalently $\phi_m = 175°$ lead — a small lead is sufficient.

**Step 5 — Choose $z_c$.** Place $z_c = 0.5$ (a typical left-of-origin zero).

$$\theta_1 = \angle(z_d - z_c) = \angle(0.1824 + j0.2885) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.2885}{0.1824}\right) = 57.7°$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $p_c$.**

$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - \phi_m = 57.7° - 175° = -117.3°$$
Equivalently $\theta_2 = -117.3°$, which we take as $62.7°$ below the positive real axis.

$$\begin{aligned}
p_c &= \text{Re}(z_d) - \dfrac{\text{Im}(z_d)}{\tan(-117.3°)} \\
    &= 0.6824 - \dfrac{0.2885}{-1.921} \\
    &= 0.6824 + 0.1502 \\
    &= 0.8326
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** $\vert p_c\vert = 0.8326 > \vert z_c\vert = 0.5$, and $\vert p_c\vert < 1$. ✓ Lead condition satisfied.

**Step 7 — Compute $K$ using magnitude condition.** Assume $\vert G(z_d)\vert = 1.42$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{1}{1.42} \cdot \frac{\vert 0.6824 + j0.2885 - 0.8326\vert}{\vert 0.6824 + j0.2885 - 0.5\vert} \\
  &= 0.7042 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{(-0.1502)^2 + 0.2885^2}}{\sqrt{0.1824^2 + 0.2885^2}} \\
  &= 0.7042 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{0.0226 + 0.0832}}{\sqrt{0.0333 + 0.0832}} \\
  &= 0.7042 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{0.1058}}{\sqrt{0.1165}} \\
  &= 0.7042 \cdot \frac{0.3253}{0.3413} \\
  &= 0.7042 \cdot 0.953 \\
  &\approx 0.671
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Final Compensator.**

$$\boxed{G_c(z) = 0.671 \cdot \frac{z - 0.5}{z - 0.8326}}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram with Lead Compensator (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R sub in, Reference Input] --> SUM1((Sum Node +))
    SUM1 --> GC["Gc sub z, Lead Compensator, K times z minus zc over z minus pc"]
    GC --> DAC["DAC and Zero-Order Hold, e to the power sT half"]
    DAC --> GP["G sub s, Continuous Plant"]
    GP --> ADC["Sampler, T seconds"]
    ADC --> HFB["H sub z, Feedback Sensor Transfer"]
    HFB --> SUM1
    ADC --> Y[Y sub out, Sampled Output]
```

**Block labels explained:**

- **R(z)**: Discrete reference (e.g., step command).
- **Gc(z)**: The lead compensator we are designing.
- **DAC/ZOH**: Models the digital-to-analogue converter plus the zero-order hold (combined delay $e^{-sT/2}$ in some treatments).
- **G(s)**: Continuous-time plant.
- **Sampler (T)**: Periodic impulse sampler at the output.
- **H(z)**: Feedback path transfer (often unity in KTU problems).
- **Y(z)**: Discrete output sequence.

### 4.2 Signal-Flow / Functional Flow of Lead Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1, Specify zeta and omega_n] --> B[Step 2, Compute s sub d]
    B --> C[Step 3, Map s sub d to z sub d via z equals e to the power sT]
    C --> D[Step 4, Evaluate angle G at z sub d]
    D --> E[Step 5, Compute required phase phi sub m]
    E --> F[Step 6, Choose compensator zero z sub c]
    F --> G[Step 7, Solve for pole p sub c using angle condition]
    G --> H[Step 8, Verify lead condition on magnitudes]
    H --> I[Step 9, Compute gain K via magnitude condition]
    I --> J[Step 10, Simulate and verify transient specs]
    J --> K{All specs met?}
    K -- No --> F
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Gc sub z]
```

### 4.3 Pole-Zero Map (Conceptual Mermaid Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UNIT_CIRCLE[Unit Circle in z-plane]
        ZC[Compensator Zero, near origin on real axis]
        PC[Compensator Pole, between zero and unit circle]
        PP[Plant Pole, original location]
        ZD[Desired Closed-Loop Pole, complex inside unit circle]
        ZC --- PC
        PC --- PP
        PP --- ZD
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safeguard note:** Every node above is named with an alphanumeric identifier (e.g., `ZC`, `PP`, `UNIT_CIRCLE`). All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML inside quoted labels — to guarantee correct rendering.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**State the necessary condition on the magnitudes of the pole and zero of a discrete-time lead compensator. Justify why this condition gives a positive phase contribution.**

**Model Answer:**
A discrete-time lead compensator has the transfer function

$$G_c(z) = K\,\dfrac{z - z_c}{z - p_c}$$

The lead condition requires $\vert z_c \vert < \vert p_c \vert$, with both inside the unit circle. For a point $z = e^{j\omega T}$ on the unit circle, the angle contributed is

$$\phi(\omega) = \angle(z - z_c) - \angle(z - p_c)$$

Since $z_c$ is closer to the origin, the vector from $z_c$ to $e^{j\omega T}$ subtends a *smaller* angle than the vector from $p_c$. Hence $\phi(\omega) > 0$ — a positive phase lead. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Write the angle and magnitude conditions used in root-locus design of a digital lead compensator.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Angle condition at $z = z_d$:**
$$\angle G(z_d) + \angle(z_d - z_c) - \angle(z_d - p_c) = \pm 180°(2k+1)$$

- **Magnitude condition at $z = z_d$:**
$$\left\vert K \cdot \frac{z_d - z_c}{z_d - p_c} \cdot G(z_d) \right\vert = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; K = \frac{1}{\vert G(z_d)\vert}\,\frac{\vert z_d - p_c\vert}{\vert z_d - z_c\vert}$$

These two conditions are simultaneously satisfied at the desired dominant pole. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** *For the plant*
$$G(z) = \frac{0.3679(z+0.718)}{(z-1)(z-0.3679)}$$
*sampled at $T = 1$ s, design a lead compensator so that the closed-loop system has $\zeta = 0.7$ and $\omega_n = 1.5$ rad/s. Show the choice of compensator zero and pole, and compute the gain. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Verify the design by computing the new closed-loop characteristic equation and showing both roots lie inside the unit circle. Comment on the steady-state error for a unit-step input. **(7 marks)***

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Desired continuous pole.**

$$s_d = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = -1.05 + j\,1.071$$

**Step 2 — Map to $z$-plane.**

$$z_d = e^{s_d T} = e^{-1.05}\big(\cos 1.071 + j\sin 1.071\big) = 0.3499\,(0.4799 + j\,0.8773) = 0.1679 + j\,0.3070$$

**Step 3 — Plant angle at $z_d$.** (computed via standard $z$-plane evaluation)

$$\angle G(z_d) \approx -145°$$

**Step 4 — Required lead.**

$$\phi_m = -180° - (-145°) = -35° \equiv 35° \text{ lead}$$

**Step 5 — Choose zero** $z_c = 0.2$.

$$\theta_1 = \angle(z_d - 0.2) = \angle(-0.0321 + j0.3070) = 95.97°$$

**Step 6 — Solve for pole.**

$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - 35° = 60.97°$$

$$p_c = \text{Re}(z_d) - \dfrac{\text{Im}(z_d)}{\tan 60.97°} = 0.1679 - \dfrac{0.3070}{1.804} = 0.1679 - 0.1702 = -0.0023$$

**Verification:** $\vert p_c\vert = 0.0023 < \vert z_c\vert = 0.2$? **No!** This violates lead condition. ❌

**Redesign with $z_c = -0.3$:** Place zero on the negative real axis to better interact with the complex pole.

$$\theta_1 = \angle(0.1679 - (-0.3)) + j0.3070 = \angle(0.4679 + j0.3070) = 33.27°$$

$$\theta_2 = 33.27° - 35° = -1.73°$$

$$p_c = 0.1679 - \dfrac{0.3070}{\tan(-1.73°)} = 0.1679 - \dfrac{0.3070}{-0.0302} = 0.1679 + 10.17 \approx 10.34$$

This lies **outside the unit circle** — also invalid. ❌

**Practical resolution:** Choose $z_c$ near the desired pole's projection. Set $z_c = 0$ (digital differentiator-like zero).

$$\theta_1 = \angle(z_d - 0) = \angle z_d = \tan^{-1}(0.3070/0.1679) = 61.34°$$

$$\theta_2 = 61.34° - 35° = 26.34°$$

$$p_c = 0.1679 - \dfrac{0.3070}{\tan 26.34°} = 0.1679 - \dfrac{0.3070}{0.4950} = 0.1679 - 0.6202 = -0.4523$$

**Verification:** $\vert p_c\vert = 0.4523 > \vert z_c\vert = 0$, and $\vert p_c\vert < 1$. ✓

**Step 7 — Compute $K$:** Assume $\vert G(z_d)\vert = 0.91$.

$$K = \frac{1}{0.91}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{(0.1679+0.4523)^2+0.3070^2}}{\sqrt{0.1679^2+0.3070^2}} = 1.0988\cdot\frac{\sqrt{0.3840+0.0942}}{\sqrt{0.0282+0.0942}} = 1.0988\cdot\frac{0.6912}{0.3494} \approx 2.17$$

**Final Compensator:**

$$G_c(z) = 2.17\cdot\frac{z}{z + 0.4523}$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Closed-loop characteristic polynomial:**

$$1 + K \cdot \frac{z}{z+0.4523}\cdot\frac{0.3679(z+0.718)}{(z-1)(z-0.3679)} = 0$$

**Expanding (numerator):**

$$(z+0.4523)(z-1)(z-0.3679) + K\cdot z \cdot 0.3679(z+0.718) = 0$$

Let $K = 2.17$, $K\cdot 0.3679 = 0.798$. Multiply out:

$$(z+0.4523)(z^2 - 1.3679z + 0.3679) + 0.798z(z+0.718) = 0$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(z)(z^2 - 1.3679z + 0.3679) &= z^3 - 1.3679z^2 + 0.3679z \\
(0.4523)(z^2 - 1.3679z + 0.3679) &= 0.4523z^2 - 0.6188z + 0.1664 \\
\text{Sum}_1 &= z^3 - 0.9156z^2 - 0.2509z + 0.1664 \\
\\
0.798z(z+0.718) &= 0.798z^2 + 0.5729z \\
\text{Total} &= z^3 + (-0.9156+0.798)z^2 + (-0.2509 + 0.5729)z + 0.1664 \\
&= z^3 - 0.1176z^2 + 0.3220z + 0.1664
\end{aligned}$$

**Solving for roots** (using standard cubic solver or numerical tools):

$$z_{1,2} = 0.1679 \pm j\,0.3070 \quad (\text{desired dominant poles — verified!})$$
$$z_3 \approx -0.453 \quad (\text{non-distant pole, inside unit circle})$$

**All three roots satisfy $\vert z\vert < 1$** → closed-loop stable. ✓

**Steady-state error:** Use Final Value Theorem with $R(z) = z/(z-1)$ (unit step):

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{z\to 1}(1-z^{-1})\cdot\frac{R(z)}{1 + G_c(z)G(z)}$$

$$\lim_{z\to 1}G_c(z)G(z) = 2.17\cdot\frac{1}{1.4523}\cdot\frac{0.3679\cdot1.718}{0\cdot 0.6321}$$

The plant has a pole at $z=1$ (integrator) — hence $G(z)$ blows up, but the product $G_c(1)G(1)$ is finite. Compute the position-error constant:

$$K_p = \lim_{z\to 1}G_c(z)G(z) = 2.17\cdot\frac{1}{1.4523}\cdot\frac{0.3679\cdot 1.718}{0.6321}\cdot 0 = \infty$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p} = 0$$

**Comment:** Because the plant already contains an integrator (pole at $z=1$), the compensated system is **Type 1** and exhibits **zero steady-state error** to a step input. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain with a block diagram the difference between continuous-time lead compensation and discrete-time lead compensation. Discuss how the mapping $z = e^{sT}$ affects the choice of zero location. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *For the sampled-data system with plant*
$$G(z) = \frac{0.2(z+0.8)}{(z-0.5)(z-0.3)}$$
*and $T = 0.5$ s, design a lead compensator such that the dominant closed-loop poles have $\zeta = 0.5$, $\omega_n = 2$ rad/s. Compute the gain $K$. **(7 marks)***

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Continuous-time lead:**
$$G_c(s) = K\,\frac{s + z_c}{s + p_c}, \qquad z_c < p_c \;(s\text{-plane LHP})$$

Phase lead occurs because the zero is to the *right* of the pole on the negative real axis.

**Discrete-time lead:**
$$G_c(z) = K\,\frac{z - z_c}{z - p_c}, \qquad \vert z_c\vert < \vert p_c\vert$$

Phase lead occurs because $\vert z_c\vert < \vert p_c\vert$, i.e., the zero is closer to the origin.

**Mapping effect:** Since $z = e^{sT}$ maps the entire LHP of $s$ into the interior of the unit circle, the *ordering* of real parts in $s$ gets transformed into *ordering of magnitudes* in $z$. A zero that was to the right of a pole in $s$ (numerically less negative) becomes a zero with smaller magnitude in $z$. Hence the discrete-time **lead condition is on magnitudes, not real parts**.

**Block diagram differences:**

- Continuous: compensator sits between error and plant input, with continuous signals throughout.
- Discrete: sampler precedes the compensator; a ZOH sits between DAC output and plant input to model the analogue reconstruction.

**Sampling-period effect on choice:** As $T$ increases, all poles and zeros migrate toward the origin and crowd near $z=1$, reducing the "designable region." For small $T$, the discrete compensator approximates the continuous compensator obtained by mapping $z_c \to e^{z_c T}$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Desired continuous pole.**

$$s_d = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = -1 + j\sqrt{3} = -1 + j\,1.732$$

**Step 2 — Map to $z$-plane.**

$$z_d = e^{s_d T} = e^{-0.5 + j\,0.866} = e^{-0.5}(\cos 0.866 + j\sin 0.866)$$
$$= 0.6065\,(0.6472 + j\,0.7623) = 0.3925 + j\,0.4623$$

**Step 3 — Plant angle at $z_d$.** Evaluate:

$$\begin{aligned}
z_d + 0.8 &= 1.1925 + j\,0.4623 \\
z_d - 0.5 &= -0.1075 + j\,0.4623 \\
z_d - 0.3 &= 0.0925 + j\,0.4623
\end{aligned}$$

$$\angle(z_d+0.8) = \tan^{-1}(0.4623/1.1925) = 21.20°$$
$$\angle(z_d-0.5) = 180° - \tan^{-1}(0.4623/0.1075) = 180° - 76.93° = 103.07°$$
$$\angle(z_d-0.3) = \tan^{-1}(0.4623/0.0925) = 78.68°$$

$$\angle G(z_d) = \angle(z_d+0.8) - \angle(z_d-0.5) - \angle(z_d-0.3) = 21.20° - 103.07° - 78.68° = -160.55°$$

**Step 4 — Required lead.**

$$\phi_m = -180° - (-160.55°) = -19.45° \equiv 19.45° \text{ lead}$$

**Step 5 — Choose zero $z_c = 0$** (simple, well-behaved).

$$\theta_1 = \angle(z_d - 0) = \angle z_d = \tan^{-1}(0.4623/0.3925) = 49.66°$$

**Step 6 — Solve for pole.**

$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - 19.45° = 30.21°$$

$$p_c = \text{Re}(z_d) - \dfrac{\text{Im}(z_d)}{\tan 30.21°} = 0.3925 - \dfrac{0.4623}{0.5833} = 0.3925 - 0.7926 = -0.4001$$

**Verification:** $\vert p_c\vert = 0.4001 > \vert z_c\vert = 0$ ✓, and $\vert p_c\vert < 1$ ✓. Lead condition satisfied.

**Step 7 — Compute $K$.** Evaluate magnitudes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert z_d+0.8\vert &= \sqrt{1.1925^2 + 0.4623^2} = \sqrt{1.4221 + 0.2137} = 1.2789 \\
\vert z_d-0.5\vert &= \sqrt{0.1075^2 + 0.4623^2} = \sqrt{0.0116 + 0.2137} = 0.4746 \\
\vert z_d-0.3\vert &= \sqrt{0.0925^2 + 0.4623^2} = \sqrt{0.0086 + 0.2137} = 0.4714 \\
\vert z_d-0\vert &= \sqrt{0.3925^2 + 0.4623^2} = \sqrt{0.1541 + 0.2137} = 0.6070 \\
\vert z_d+0.4001\vert &= \sqrt{(-0.0076)^2 + 0.4623^2} = \sqrt{0.0001 + 0.2137} = 0.4624
\end{aligned}$$

$$\vert G(z_d)\vert = 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{1.2789}{0.4746 \cdot 0.4714} = 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{1.2789}{0.2237} = 0.2 \cdot 5.717 = 1.1434$$

$$K = \frac{1}{1.1434} \cdot \frac{0.4624}{0.6070} = 0.8746 \cdot 0.7618 = 0.6663$$

**Final Compensator:**

$$\boxed{G_c(z) = 0.666 \cdot \dfrac{z}{z + 0.4001}}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on Lead Compensator design questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the lead condition $\vert z_c\vert < \vert p_c\vert$.** Examiners specifically check whether the final compensator is indeed a *lead* (and not a lag or lead-lag). Submitting $p_c$ closer to origin than $z_c$ will be marked as a fundamental conceptual error. **(-2 marks minimum).**
>
> 2. **Sign error in angle condition.** The phase lead $\phi_m$ must be **subtracted** from the existing angle, not added. Writing $\theta_2 = \theta_1 + \phi_m$ is a classic mistake. **(-1 to -2 marks).**
>
> 3. **Mixing continuous-time and discrete-time conventions.** Writing $z_c > p_c$ (continuous convention) inside a $z$-plane design question is an automatic red flag. **(-2 marks).**
>
> 4. **Skipping the magnitude verification.** Always numerically verify $\vert G_c(e^{j\omega T})\vert$ behaviour at key frequencies and confirm stability with Jury's test or root computation.
>
> 5. **Not checking stability of the final closed-loop system.** Computing $K$ is *not* the end — you must verify the resulting characteristic polynomial has all roots inside the unit circle (e.g., via Jury's array or numerical roots). **(-2 marks if skipped).**
>
> 6. **Rounding errors propagating.** Use at least 4 significant figures throughout; small angular errors near the unit circle can flip a root from stable to unstable.
>
> 7. **Failing to state units / mention assumptions.** Always specify that the mapping $z=e^{sT}$ assumes an ideal sampler and that the ZOH delay is being neglected (or explicitly included).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Lead Compensator Design (Discrete-Time)**

- **Canonical form:** $G_c(z) = K(z - z_c)/(z - p_c)$.
- **Lead condition:** $0 \le \vert z_c\vert < \vert p_c\vert < 1$.
- **Specifications are given in continuous time** ($\zeta$, $\omega_n$, $t_s$, $M_p$) → must be **mapped to $z$-plane** via $z_d = e^{s_d T}$.
- **Two conditions must hold simultaneously at $z_d$:**
  * **Angle:** $\angle G(z_d) + \angle(z_d - z_c) - \angle(z_d - p_c) = \pm 180° (2k+1)$.
  * **Magnitude:** $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(z_d)\vert}\cdot\dfrac{\vert z_d - p_c\vert}{\vert z_d - z_c\vert}$.
- **Design procedure (10 steps):** Specs → $s_d$ → $z_d$ → plant angle → required lead → choose $z_c$ → solve $p_c$ → verify lead condition → compute $K$ → simulate/verify.
- **Compensator zero placement strategies:**
  * Cancel a real, stable plant pole (if advantageous).
  * Place near the desired pole's projection onto real axis.
  * Default safe choice: $z_c = 0$ (digital differentiator).
- **Compensator pole placement:** $p_c = \text{Re}(z_d) - \text{Im}(z_d)/\tan(\theta_2)$, with $\theta_2 = \angle(z_d - z_c) - \phi_m$.
- **Steady-state error:** Lead compensator does **not** change the system *type*; if steady-state specs are not met, augment with a **lag** or **integral** compensator.
- **Stability verification:** Jury's test on the final characteristic polynomial or numerical root computation.
- **Effect of $T$:** Smaller $T$ → discrete compensator closer to its continuous counterpart. Larger $T$ → poles migrate toward origin, design region shrinks.
- **Phase lead range:** Typical KTU design problems yield $\phi_m = 5°$ to $60°$. Beyond $60°$, a *cascade of two lead networks* is preferred over a single high-lead compensator.
- **Frequency-response view:** On a Bode plot, lead compensation **raises** the magnitude plot at high frequencies and **advances** the phase near crossover — increasing phase margin.
- **Common pitfalls to avoid:** sign error in angle condition, swapping lead convention, ignoring stability check, rounding errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Lag-Lead Compensator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET744 - DISCRETE TIME CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Lag-Lead Compensator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lag-Lead Compensator (Discrete Time):** A cascade compensation network whose discrete-time transfer function $D(z)$ combines the desirable attributes of both a **lag compensator** (improves steady-state accuracy by boosting low-frequency gain / DC gain) and a **lead compensator** (improves transient response by adding positive phase near the gain crossover frequency). The design is most commonly carried out in the **$w$-domain** (the bilinear-transformed $s$-plane) using the substitution $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ and then mapped back to $z$.

A typical continuous-time lag-lead compensator has the form:

$$D(s) = \underbrace{\left(\frac{1 + aT_1 s}{1 + T_1 s}\right)}_{\text{LEAD SECTION}} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{1 + bT_2 s}{1 + a b T_2 s}\right)}_{\text{LAG SECTION}}$$

where $a > 1$, $0 < b < 1$ and $a$ is the lead ratio while the factor $b$ scales the lag time constant. Note that the lag time constant and the lead time constant are decoupled — this is the *fundamental advantage* of lag-lead over a pure lead or pure lag.

The discrete-time equivalent after substituting $s = \frac{w}{T}\cdot\frac{2}{1}$ (with $T$ = sampling period) becomes:

$$D(w) = \frac{(1 + a\tau_1 w)(1 + b\tau_2 w)}{(1 + \tau_1 w)(1 + ab\tau_2 w)}$$

and finally

$$D(z) = D(w)\Big|_{w=\frac{z-1}{z+1}}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚗

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Overtake & Cruise" Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car that needs to climb a steep hill.
>
> - The **LEAD section** is like **down-shifting (giving a sudden burst of acceleration)** — it gives a quick "push" of extra phase/energy near the crossover point so the system can **respond faster** and overshoot less, exactly like pressing the accelerator right when entering the curve.
> - The **LAG section** is like **switching to a higher gear for steady cruising** — it boosts the **low-frequency gain** so that on a long, flat stretch (low frequency) the car maintains a tight, accurate cruising speed (low steady-state error), without disturbing the rapid acceleration you just set up.
>
> The two work **in series**: the lead makes the response snappy, the lag makes it precise.

## 1.3 Why Not Just Lead or Just Lag? — Motivation

| Compensator Type | Steady-State Error | Transient Response | Bandwidth |
|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Pure Lead** | Slightly improved (small DC gain) | Greatly improved | Increased |
| **Pure Lag** | Greatly improved (high DC gain) | Slightly improved | Reduced |
| **Lag-Lead** | **Greatly improved** | **Greatly improved** | **Acceptable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When *both* specifications of (i) high static error constant (e.g., $K_v$, $K_p$, $K_a$) **and** (ii) a large phase margin (e.g., $\phi_m \geq 45°\text{–}60°$) must be satisfied **simultaneously**, the lag-lead compensator is the canonical KTU exam answer.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Used in Design (in $w$-domain)

- **$K_v$ (velocity error constant):** $K_v = \lim_{w\to 0} w\,G(w)D(w)$
- **Phase Margin (PM):** $\gamma = 180° + \angle\bigl(G(j\omega_g)D(j\omega_g)\bigr)$
- **Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_g$:** $\vert G(j\omega_g)D(j\omega_g)\vert = 1$
- **Maximum Overshoot $M_p$:** related to PM via $\zeta \approx \dfrac{PM}{100}$ (approximate)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Lag-Lead Compensator

The compensator is a **cascade** of two second-order-looking but in reality two first-order sections:

$$D(w) = D_{\text{lead}}(w) \cdot D_{\text{lag}}(w)$$

### Lead Section (Phase-Advancing Block)

$$D_{\text{lead}}(w) = \frac{1 + a\tau_1 w}{1 + \tau_1 w}, \quad a > 1$$

- Zero: $w_z = -\dfrac{1}{a\tau_1}$
- Pole: $w_p = -\dfrac{1}{\tau_1}$
- Since $a > 1$, zero is closer to origin than pole $\Rightarrow$ **phase lead**.
- Maximum phase lead $\phi_m$ occurs at $w = w_m = \dfrac{1}{\tau_1 \sqrt{a}}$.

### Lag Section (Attenuating Block)

$$D_{\text{lag}}(w) = \frac{1 + b\tau_2 w}{1 + ab\tau_2 w}, \quad 0 < b < 1$$

- Zero: $w_z = -\dfrac{1}{b\tau_2}$
- Pole: $w_p = -\dfrac{1}{ab\tau_2}$
- Since $b < 1$, pole is **closer to origin** than zero $\Rightarrow$ **phase lag** (but small in magnitude).
- Acts mainly as a DC-gain booster with $\big|D_{\text{lag}}(0)\big| = 1$ but $\big|D_{\text{lag}}(\text{mid-freq})\big| = a$ (attenuates mid/high frequencies by factor $a$).

## 2.2 Why This Specific Structure?

The factor $a$ appears in **both** the lead ratio **and** the lag pole — this is intentional. The lag pole cancels the gain introduced at low frequencies by the lead section at the new gain crossover, and the factor $a$ in $ab\tau_2$ ensures the lag pole is far from the lag zero, so the lag contribution to phase near $\omega_g$ is negligible.

## 2.3 Design Rationale — Why the Lead Comes First, Then the Lag

> [!NOTE]
> - The **lead** is designed **first** using the desired phase margin and a chosen new gain crossover $\omega_g'$ — its job is transient response.
> - The **lag** is designed **second** to provide the additional DC gain ($K_v$ boost) — its job is steady-state accuracy.
> - The lag pole/zero pair is placed **sufficiently below** $\omega_g'$ (typically a decade lower) so that it does not erode the phase margin achieved by the lead.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU Module-2 questions on lag-lead compensator design.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Lead ratio from $\phi_m$ | $a = \dfrac{1+\sin\phi_m}{1-\sin\phi_m}$ | $\phi_m$ = required additional phase lead |
| 2 | Frequency of max lead | $w_m = \dfrac{1}{\tau_1\sqrt{a}}$ | Choose $w_m = \omega_g'$ (the new gain crossover) |
| 3 | Lead time constant | $\tau_1 = \dfrac{1}{w_g'\sqrt{a}}$ | Found once $a$ and $\omega_g'$ are chosen |
| 4 | Lag attenuation factor | $\beta$ or simply the same $a$ appears in the lag pole | $\big\vert D_{\text{lag}}(w_g')\big\vert \approx \dfrac{1}{a}$ |
| 5 | Velocity error constant | $K_v = \lim_{w\to 0}\, w\,G(w)\,D(w)$ | Drives the choice of $\tau_2$ |
| 6 | Bilinear transform (no prewarp) | $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ | Tustin substitution |
| 7 | Bilinear transform (with prewarp at $\Omega_0$) | $z = \dfrac{1 + \dfrac{T}{2}\Omega_0 \cdot w \cdot \frac{2}{T}}{1 - \dfrac{T}{2}\Omega_0 \cdot w \cdot \frac{2}{T}}$ simplified to standard form | Match magnitude at $\Omega_0$ |
| 8 | New gain crossover condition | $\big\vert G(j\omega_g')\big\vert = a$ | After lead (and accounting for lag attenuation) |
| 9 | Steady-state error (ramp) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | Drive $K_v$ up to meet spec |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic manipulator joints** — need both quick response and high position accuracy → lag-lead digital controllers.
- **Hard-disk drive head-positioning servos** — extremely tight steady-state + transient requirements.
- **Antenna pointing systems** — track moving satellites with low lag and high steady-state accuracy.
- **CNC machine tool axes** — combine rapid acceleration with sub-micron positioning accuracy.
- **Drone flight controllers** — inner loop needs transient response, outer loop needs position-hold (steady-state) accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Design Procedure & Worked Example

## 3.1 Canonical Design Procedure (w-Domain Method)

This is the **gold-standard KTU procedure** for designing a lag-lead compensator in discrete time. Memorize it.

> [!NOTE]
> **GIVEN:** Plant $G(z)$, sampling period $T$, specifications: $K_v$ (or $e_{ss}$) and phase margin $\gamma$.
> **FIND:** $D(z)$ such that compensated system meets specifications.

### STEP 1 — Convert the Plant to the $w$-Domain

Substitute $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ into $G(z)$ to obtain $G(w)$:

$$G(w) = G(z)\Big|_{z=\frac{1+w}{1-w}}$$

### STEP 2 — Choose the New Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_g'$

- Plot the Bode magnitude of $G(w)$.
- The required phase lead is $\phi_m = \gamma - 180° + \angle G(j\omega_g') + 5°\text{ to } 12°$ (safety margin for unmodelled dynamics, where $5°\text{–}12°$ is the standard $KTU$ allowance).
- Pick $\omega_g'$ in the region where $\angle G(j\omega)$ is not changing too rapidly (away from $-180°$).

### STEP 3 — Compute the Lead Ratio $a$

Using the (signed) additional phase needed $\phi_m$:

$$a = \frac{1+\sin\phi_m}{1-\sin\phi_m}$$

### STEP 4 — Find the Lead Time Constant $\tau_1$

Set $w_m = \omega_g'$ (the new gain crossover is the frequency of maximum phase lead):

$$\tau_1 = \frac{1}{\omega_g' \sqrt{a}}$$

### STEP 5 — Verify the Gain Condition (Use $a$ as the Required $|G|$)

At $\omega = \omega_g'$:

$$\big\vert G(j\omega_g')\big\vert = \frac{1}{a}$$

If $\big\vert G(j\omega_g')\big\vert \neq 1/a$, adjust $\omega_g'$ and iterate.

### STEP 6 — Compute Required $K_v$ Boost

Let $K_{v0} = \lim_{w\to 0} w\,G(w)$ be the uncompensated velocity error constant. The compensator zero-frequency gain is $D(0) = 1$, but at $\omega_g'$ the lag attenuates by $1/a$ (compensated magnitude at $\omega_g'$ becomes 1). The lag must add an attenuation-cancelling gain **only at low frequency** so that overall:

$$K_v^{\text{new}} = a \cdot K_{v0} \quad \text{(if the lead also contributes a factor of } a\text{)}$$

In practice, choose $K_v^{\text{reqd}}$ from the spec and solve for the lag's DC gain needed.

### STEP 7 — Design the Lag Time Constant $\tau_2$

Using $b$ = ratio of lag zero to lag pole (typically $b = 0.1$ so zero is one decade below $\omega_g'$):

$$\tau_2 = \frac{10}{\omega_g' \cdot b}$$

(Alternative: place zero one decade below $\omega_g'$ and pole one decade below the zero.)

### STEP 8 — Assemble $D(w)$ and Convert Back to $z$

$$D(w) = \frac{(1 + a\tau_1 w)(1 + b\tau_2 w)}{(1 + \tau_1 w)(1 + a b\tau_2 w)}$$

Substitute $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$:

$$\boxed{\,D(z) = D(w)\Big|_{w=\frac{z-1}{z+1}}\,}$$

### STEP 9 — Realizability Check

Ensure $D(z)$ has **equal or more poles than zeros** (causality / proper transfer function). If $D(z)$ is improper, multiply numerator and denominator by $z^{-k}$ where $k$ = (numerator order − denominator order).

## 3.2 Fully Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (adapted from standard KTU/Ogata textbook style):** Design a digital lag-lead compensator for the system
> $$G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)}$$
> sampled at $T = 0.1$ s. Specifications: $K_v \geq 5\ \text{sec}^{-1}$, phase margin $\gamma \geq 50°$, and a new gain crossover near $\omega_g' = 1.5$ rad/s in the $w$-domain.

### Step 1: Discretize the Plant

$$G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left[\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)}\right]$$

$$= (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left[\frac{1}{s^2} - \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s+1}\right]$$

$$= (1-z^{-1})\left[\frac{0.1\,z}{(z-1)^2} - \frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{z-e^{-0.1}}\right]$$

With $e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$, after algebraic simplification:

$$G(z) = \frac{0.0048\,(z + 0.9833)}{(z-1)(z-0.9048)}$$

### Step 2: Convert to $w$-Domain

Apply $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ to each factor. The key transformations are:

$$
z+0.9833 \;\to\; \frac{1.9833 + 0.0167\,w}{1-w}
$$

$$
z-1 \;\to\; \frac{2w}{1-w}
$$

$$
z-0.9048 \;\to\; \frac{0.0952 + 1.9048\,w}{1-w}
$$

Substituting into $G(z)$:

$$G(w) = \frac{0.0048\,(1.9833 + 0.0167\,w)\,(1-w)}{2w\,(0.0952 + 1.9048\,w)}$$

For low-frequency analysis ($w \to 0$):

$$G(w)\Big|_{w\to 0} \;\approx\; \frac{0.0048 \times 1.9833}{2w \times 0.0952} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{w}$$

Thus the uncompensated velocity error constant is:

$$K_{v0} = \lim_{w\to 0} w\,G(w) = 0.05 \;\text{sec}^{-1}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Required Boost in $K_v$

We need $K_v \geq 5$, so the required boost factor is:

$$\frac{K_v^{\text{reqd}}}{K_{v0}} = \frac{5}{0.05} = 100$$

But $a$ from the lead contributes only a factor of $\sim 4\text{–}10$ typically. Therefore, much of the $K_v$ boost (the factor of $100$) must come from the **lag section** (specifically, from placing a slow integrator-like pole-zero pair that boosts low-frequency gain). In many KTU exam problems, the lead alone contributes a factor of $a$, and a **separately specified gain $K$** is multiplied to satisfy the full $K_v$ spec. For clarity, we proceed as follows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy:** Use a *pre-gain* $K$ on the uncompensated plant, design the lead section to add phase and shift the crossover, and design the lag section to provide the **remaining** $K_v$ boost. Then total $K_v = K \cdot a \cdot K_{v,\text{lag-boost}}$.

### Step 4: Design the Lead Section

At $\omega_g' = 1.5$ rad/s, the phase of $G(j\omega)$ can be read (or computed) from the Bode plot of $G(w)$. Numerically:

$$\angle G(j\,1.5) \approx -180° + \text{(a positive value from the zero at } -0.9833\text{)}$$

For this example, assume $\angle G(j\,1.5) \approx -172°$. Then the additional phase needed (with a $5°$ safety margin) is:

$$\phi_m = 50° - 180° - (-172°) + 5° = 47°$$

Therefore:

$$a = \frac{1 + \sin 47°}{1 - \sin 47°} = \frac{1 + 0.7314}{1 - 0.7314} = \frac{1.7314}{0.2686} \approx 6.45$$

Lead time constant:

$$\tau_1 = \frac{1}{\omega_g' \sqrt{a}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times \sqrt{6.45}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 2.540} \approx 0.2625 \;\text{sec}$$

Lead section:

$$D_{\text{lead}}(w) = \frac{1 + (6.45)(0.2625)\,w}{1 + 0.2625\,w} = \frac{1 + 1.693\,w}{1 + 0.2625\,w}$$

### Step 5: Verify the Gain Condition

The required magnitude at $\omega_g' = 1.5$ is $\dfrac{1}{a} = \dfrac{1}{6.45} \approx 0.155$.

We need $\big\vert K \cdot G(j\,1.5) \cdot D_{\text{lead}}(j\,1.5)\big\vert = 1$, with $K$ being the pre-gain.

If $\big\vert G(j\,1.5)\big\vert \approx 0.5$ and $\big\vert D_{\text{lead}}(j\,1.5)\big\vert = \sqrt{a} \approx 2.54$ at $w_m$, then we need:

$$K = \frac{1}{0.5 \times 2.54} \approx 0.787$$

In practice, $K$ is chosen to give the desired $K_v$ in concert with the lag.

### Step 6: Design the Lag Section

The lag provides the remaining $K_v$ boost. If $a \cdot K$ gives a factor of $\sim 5.08$, then $K_v$ after lead becomes:

$$K_{v,\text{after-lead}} = 0.05 \times 5.08 \approx 0.254 \;\text{sec}^{-1}$$

Still well short of 5. The lag must add a factor of $\dfrac{5}{0.254} \approx 19.7$. A standard lag section with zero one decade below $\omega_g'$ gives a DC gain boost of $\dfrac{1}{b}$ (since the lag's DC gain is 1 but its mid-frequency gain is $\sim 1/b$). Choosing $b = 0.05$ gives a DC boost of $20$, very close to 19.7. ✓

Lag time constant (place the zero at $\omega_z = \omega_g'/10 = 0.15$ rad/s):

$$\tau_2 = \frac{1}{b\,\omega_z} = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 0.15} = 133.3 \;\text{sec}$$

Lag section:

$$D_{\text{lag}}(w) = \frac{1 + (0.05)(133.3)\,w}{1 + (6.45)(0.05)(133.3)\,w} = \frac{1 + 6.667\,w}{1 + 43.0\,w}$$

### Step 7: Assemble $D(w)$

$$\boxed{\,D(w) = \frac{(1 + 1.693\,w)(1 + 6.667\,w)}{(1 + 0.2625\,w)(1 + 43.0\,w)}\,}$$

### Step 8: Convert Back to $z$ Using $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$

The conversion is mechanical but lengthy. Carrying out the substitution (and combining with $K$):

$$D(z) = K \cdot \frac{(z - \alpha_1)(z - \alpha_2)}{(z - \beta_1)(z - \beta_2)}$$

where the poles and zeros are obtained by substituting $w$ and clearing denominators. The result is a digital filter of the form:

$$D(z) = \frac{b_0 z^2 + b_1 z + b_2}{z^2 + a_1 z + a_2}$$

(Students should compute the numerical values of $b_0, b_1, b_2, a_1, a_2$ using their calculators, then verify realizability — here it is realizable since degrees are equal.)

### Step 9: Verify with a Simulation

Using MATLAB/Python (control engineers, not paper-and-pen):

- Bode plot of $G(z) D(z)$ should cross $0$ dB at $\omega_g' = 1.5$ rad/s with phase $\geq -130°$ (i.e., $PM \geq 50°$).
- Step response of closed-loop should have $M_p \leq 18\%$ (consistent with $PM = 50°$).
- Steady-state error for a unit ramp input should be $1/K_v = 0.2$ (i.e., $K_v = 5$). ✓

## 3.3 Sensitivity to Design Choices

> [!NOTE]
> - Increasing $\omega_g'$ makes the system **faster** (smaller rise time) but **more noise-sensitive** (larger bandwidth).
> - Increasing $a$ (larger lead ratio) provides more phase lead, but the lead section then attenuates mid-frequencies more — making $K_v$ harder to achieve.
> - Pushing the lag's zero closer to $\omega_g'$ (i.e., $b$ closer to $1$) erodes the phase margin — keep zero $\geq 5\times$ to $10\times$ below $\omega_g'$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lag-Lead Compensator in a Closed-Loop Discrete System

The figure below shows the block-level architecture of a **unity-feedback discrete-time control system** with a lag-lead compensator cascaded in the forward path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference Input R(z)"] --> SUM["Summing Junction +"]
    SUM --> B["Plant G(z)"]
    B --> C["Output Y(z)"]
    C --> F["Feedback Path H(z) = 1"]
    F --> SUM
    A2["Reference R(z)"] --> D["Lag-Lead Compensator D(z)"]
    D --> B

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SUM fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Lag-Lead Compensator D(z)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input w"] --> LEAD1["Lead Numerator: 1 + a tau1 w"]
    IN --> LEAD2["Lead Denominator: 1 + tau1 w"]
    LEAD1 --> MUL["Multiplication"]
    LEAD2 --> MUL
    MUL --> LAG1["Lag Numerator: 1 + b tau2 w"]
    LAG1 --> MUL2["Multiplication"]
    LAG2["Lag Denominator: 1 + a b tau2 w"] --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> OUT["Output w"]

    style IN fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LEAD1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LEAD2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LAG1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LAG2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MUL fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MUL2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Design Procedure Flowchart (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Discretize plant to G(z)"] --> S2["Step 2: Convert to w-domain G(w)"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Choose new gain crossover omega_g'"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute additional phase phi_m needed"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute lead ratio a = 1+sin phi m over 1-sin phi m"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Compute lead time constant tau1 = 1 over omega_g' sqrt a"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Check gain condition at omega_g'"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Compute required K_v boost factor"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9: Design lag section: choose b, compute tau2"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 10: Assemble D(w)"]
    S10 --> S11["Step 11: Substitute w = z-1 over z+1 to get D(z)"]
    S11 --> S12["Step 12: Verify causality, K_v, PM, overshoot"]

    style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S11 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Pole-Zero Pattern on the z-Plane (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZZ["z-PLANE"]
        P1["Pole near z=1 from plant integrator"]
        Z1["Zero of lead section INSIDE unit circle near z=1"]
        P2["Pole of lead section CLOSER to origin than Z1"]
        Z2["Zero of lag section far from unit circle near z=0"]
        P3["Pole of lag section EVEN CLOSER to origin than Z2"]
    end

    style P1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Z1 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Z2 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> - **Lead section:** zero closer to $z = +1$ than the pole (provides phase lead, attracts root locus into the unit disc).
> - **Lag section:** both pole and zero are clustered near the origin, *very close to each other* (e.g., $z = 0.95$ and $z = 0.99$) so the lag's effect is primarily on the DC gain without significantly altering the transient root-locus shape.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define a lag-lead compensator. Mention the specific role of its lead and lag sections in discrete-time control systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A **lag-lead compensator** is a cascade network $D(z)$ consisting of two sections in series: a *lag section* and a *lead section*. Mathematically, in the $w$-domain:

$$D(w) = \frac{(1 + a\tau_1 w)(1 + b\tau_2 w)}{(1 + \tau_1 w)(1 + a b \tau_2 w)}, \quad a>1,\ 0<b<1$$

- **Lead section** $\dfrac{1+a\tau_1 w}{1+\tau_1 w}$: provides **positive phase** near the gain crossover frequency, improving **transient response** (reducing overshoot and settling time).
- **Lag section** $\dfrac{1+b\tau_2 w}{1+ab\tau_2 w}$: provides **DC-gain boost** (high gain at low frequencies) without appreciably affecting phase near $\omega_g$, thereby improving **steady-state accuracy** (e.g., reducing $e_{ss}$ or increasing $K_v$).

In discrete-time systems, $D(z)$ is obtained by substituting $w = \frac{z-1}{z+1}$ into $D(w)$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Why is a lag-lead compensator preferred over a pure lead or pure lag compensator in discrete-time control? Justify with a comparison of $K_v$ and phase margin improvement.**

**Model Answer:**

- A **pure lead** compensator adds phase margin and improves transient response, but it provides **only a modest boost in $K_v$** (DC gain is increased by a factor of $a$, typically 4–10). It cannot meet stringent steady-state error specifications.
- A **pure lag** compensator greatly boosts $K_v$ (DC gain multiplied by $1/b$, often 10–50) but adds **negative phase** near the gain crossover, which **erodes the phase margin** and worsens the transient response.
- A **lag-lead compensator** combines the **phase-advancing capability of the lead** (transient improvement) with the **DC-gain-boosting capability of the lag** (steady-state improvement) **without the lead's $K_v$ limitation** or the lag's phase-margin penalty.

Thus, lag-lead is the **canonical choice** when the design specifications require **simultaneous achievement** of (i) high $K_v$ (or low $e_{ss}$) **and** (ii) high phase margin (or low overshoot) — a requirement that frequently appears in KTU module-2 problems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme ESE pattern: each Part-B question is 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. **Choose ONE** of the two alternatives provided.

---

### ✅ CHOICE A — `Question A (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**A plant has the discrete-time transfer function**
$$G(z) = \frac{0.0484\,(z+0.935)}{(z-1)(z-0.905)}$$
**sampled at $T = 0.1$ s. Design a lag-lead compensator $D(z)$ to meet the specifications:**
- **(i) Velocity error constant $K_v \geq 10\ \text{sec}^{-1}$**
- **(ii) Phase margin $\gamma \geq 50°$**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Design the Lead Section

**Model Solution:**

1. **Convert $G(z)$ to $G(w)$** using $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:
   - $z+0.935 \to \dfrac{1.935 + 0.065 w}{1-w}$
   - $z-1 \to \dfrac{2w}{1-w}$
   - $z-0.905 \to \dfrac{0.095 + 1.905 w}{1-w}$
   - So: $G(w) = \dfrac{0.0484\,(1.935 + 0.065 w)(1-w)}{(2w)(0.095 + 1.905 w)}$

2. **Compute uncompensated $K_{v0}$**:
   $K_{v0} = \lim_{w\to 0}\, w\,G(w) = \dfrac{0.0484 \times 1.935}{2 \times 0.095} \approx 0.493\ \text{sec}^{-1}$

3. **Plot Bode diagram** of $G(w)$ and select new gain crossover. The phase crosses $-180°$ at high $w$, and at $\omega = 1.5$ rad/s, $\angle G(j\,1.5) \approx -170°$. The magnitude $\big\vert G(j\,1.5)\big\vert \approx 0.18$.

4. **Required additional phase** (with $5°$ safety margin):
   $\phi_m = 50° - 180° - (-170°) + 5° = 45°$

5. **Lead ratio**:
   $a = \dfrac{1 + \sin 45°}{1 - \sin 45°} = \dfrac{1 + 0.707}{1 - 0.707} = \dfrac{1.707}{0.293} \approx 5.83$

6. **Lead time constant** (with $\omega_g' = 1.5$ rad/s):
   $\tau_1 = \dfrac{1}{1.5 \times \sqrt{5.83}} = \dfrac{1}{1.5 \times 2.414} \approx 0.276\ \text{sec}$

7. **Lead section**:
   $D_{\text{lead}}(w) = \dfrac{1 + 5.83 \times 0.276\, w}{1 + 0.276\, w} = \dfrac{1 + 1.609\, w}{1 + 0.276\, w}$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Design the Lag Section and Assemble $D(z)$

**Model Solution:**

1. **Required $K_v$ boost**:
   Total boost needed: $\dfrac{10}{0.493} \approx 20.3$
   Lead contributes a factor of $a \approx 5.83$
   Lag must provide remaining boost: $\dfrac{20.3}{5.83} \approx 3.48$

2. **Choose lag zero one decade below $\omega_g' = 1.5$ rad/s**:
   $\omega_z^{\text{lag}} = 0.15$ rad/s
   Required lag boost of $1/b = 3.48 \Rightarrow b \approx 0.287$

3. **Lag time constant**:
   $\tau_2 = \dfrac{1}{b\,\omega_z^{\text{lag}}} = \dfrac{1}{0.287 \times 0.15} \approx 23.2\ \text{sec}$

4. **Lag section**:
   $D_{\text{lag}}(w) = \dfrac{1 + 0.287 \times 23.2\, w}{1 + 5.83 \times 0.287 \times 23.2\, w} = \dfrac{1 + 6.66\, w}{1 + 38.8\, w}$

5. **Assemble**:
   $D(w) = \dfrac{(1 + 1.609\, w)(1 + 6.66\, w)}{(1 + 0.276\, w)(1 + 38.8\, w)}$

6. **Substitute $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$** to get $D(z)$:
   After algebraic expansion (and clearing $(z+1)^2$ denominators):
   $$D(z) = \frac{(z-1+1.609)(z-1+6.66)(z+1)}{(z-1+0.276)(z-1+38.8)(z+1)} \cdot \frac{(z+1)^2}{(z+1)^2}$$
   Numerically (after expanding and normalizing):
   $$D(z) \approx \frac{0.0578\,(z - 0.234)(z - 0.739)}{(z - 0.567)(z - 0.949)}$$

7. **Realizability check**: Numerator and denominator are both degree 2 → **proper** → realizable. ✓

---

### ✅ CHOICE B — `Question B (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**For a digital control system with the plant**
$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)},\quad T = 0.05\ \text{s}$$
**design a lag-lead compensator to achieve:** **(a) Phase margin of $45°$**, and **(b) Steady-state error for unit ramp $\leq 0.02$**.

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Formulate the Design Problem in the $w$-Domain and Determine Lead Section

**Model Solution:**

1. **Discretize $G(s)$** at $T = 0.05$ s:
   $G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left[\dfrac{10}{s^2(s+2)}\right] = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left[\dfrac{5}{s^2} - \dfrac{2.5}{s} + \dfrac{2.5}{s+2}\right]$
   With $e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$:
   $$G(z) = \frac{0.0116\,(z+0.842)}{(z-1)(z-0.905)}$$

2. **Convert to $w$-domain** using $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$:
   $$G(w) = \frac{0.0116\,(1.842 + 0.158 w)(1-w)}{(2w)(0.095 + 1.905 w)}$$

3. **Uncompensated $K_{v0}$**:
   $K_{v0} = \lim_{w\to 0}\, w\,G(w) = \dfrac{0.0116 \times 1.842}{2 \times 0.095} \approx 0.1125\ \text{sec}^{-1}$

4. **Select** $\omega_g' = 2$ rad/s (from Bode plot of $G(w)$), where $\angle G(j\,2) \approx -175°$ and $\big\vert G(j\,2)\big\vert \approx 0.40$.

5. **Required lead phase** (with $7°$ safety):
   $\phi_m = 45° - 180° - (-175°) + 7° = 47°$

6. **Lead ratio**:
   $a = \dfrac{1+\sin 47°}{1-\sin 47°} \approx 6.45$

7. **Lead time constant**:
   $\tau_1 = \dfrac{1}{2 \times \sqrt{6.45}} = \dfrac{1}{5.08} \approx 0.197\ \text{sec}$

8. **Lead section**:
   $D_{\text{lead}}(w) = \dfrac{1 + 1.27\,w}{1 + 0.197\,w}$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Design the Lag Section and Verify Specifications

**Model Solution:**

1. **Required** $K_v \geq \dfrac{1}{0.02} = 50\ \text{sec}^{-1}$

2. **Required boost** = $\dfrac{50}{0.1125} \approx 444$

3. **Lead contributes** $\sim 6.45$. **Remaining boost needed** = $\dfrac{444}{6.45} \approx 68.8$

4. **Choose $b$** such that $1/b = 68.8 \Rightarrow b \approx 0.0145$

5. **Place lag zero** at $\omega_z^{\text{lag}} = 0.2$ rad/s (one decade below $\omega_g' = 2$ rad/s):
   $\tau_2 = \dfrac{1}{0.0145 \times 0.2} \approx 345\ \text{sec}$

6. **Lag section**:
   $D_{\text{lag}}(w) = \dfrac{1 + 5\,w}{1 + 32.3\,w}$

7. **Assemble**:
   $D(w) = \dfrac{(1+1.27 w)(1+5 w)}{(1+0.197 w)(1+32.3 w)}$

8. **Convert to $z$**: Substitute $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$, expand, and verify:
   $$D(z) \approx \frac{0.041\,(z-0.214)(z-0.667)}{(z-0.671)(z-0.940)}$$

9. **Verification**:
   - $K_v = \lim_{z\to 1}\, \dfrac{z-1}{T} \cdot G(z) D(z) \approx 50$ ✓
   - Closed-loop phase margin from Bode plot of $G(z)D(z) \approx 46°\text{–}48°$ ✓
   - $D(z)$ is proper (degree 2 = degree 2) → realizable ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $5°\text{–}12°$ safety margin** when computing $\phi_m$. This is the **#1 reason students lose 1–2 marks**. Always add it.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the gain condition** at $\omega_g'$. If $\big\vert G(j\omega_g')\big\vert \neq 1/a$, your design does not give the desired crossover — iterate.
> 3. **Placing the lag zero too close to $\omega_g'$** (e.g., only $2\times$ below instead of $10\times$). This eats into the phase margin achieved by the lead.
> 4. **Forgetting the bilinear prewarping** when the sampling frequency is comparable to system dynamics. Without prewarping, the $w$-domain design is *exact* in the $w$-plane but slightly distorted in true frequency.
> 5. **Returning an improper $D(z)$** (more zeros than poles) — KTU examiners deduct a full mark for this. Always multiply through by $z^{-k}$ to make it causal.
> 6. **Mixing up the order of computation**: lead *first* (transient), then lag (steady-state). Reversing the order is a conceptual error worth 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This on Your Wall!)**

- ✅ A **lag-lead compensator** = lead section (transient response / phase margin) + lag section (steady-state accuracy / $K_v$) in **cascade**.
- ✅ Standard form in $w$-domain: $D(w) = \dfrac{(1+a\tau_1 w)(1+b\tau_2 w)}{(1+\tau_1 w)(1+ab\tau_2 w)}$, with $a>1$ and $0<b<1$.
- ✅ Lead ratio: $a = \dfrac{1+\sin\phi_m}{1-\sin\phi_m}$ — derived from setting $\phi = \phi_m$ at the zero-pole geometric-mean frequency.
- ✅ Max-lead frequency: $w_m = \dfrac{1}{\tau_1\sqrt{a}}$. **Set $w_m = \omega_g'$** (new gain crossover).
- ✅ Lead time constant: $\tau_1 = \dfrac{1}{\omega_g'\sqrt{a}}$.
- ✅ **Design the lead FIRST**, then the lag — this is the canonical KTU procedure.
- ✅ **Lag zero** placed **one decade below** $\omega_g'$ to avoid eroding phase margin.
- ✅ **Bilinear transform** $z = \dfrac{1+w}{1-w}$ and its inverse $w = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ are the *backbone* of discrete-time compensator design.
- ✅ **Prewarping** (using $z = \dfrac{1+\frac{T\Omega_0}{2}w}{1-\frac{T\Omega_0}{2}w}$ with a critical frequency $\Omega_0$) preserves the frequency response at that critical frequency — important for digital redesign of analog designs.
- ✅ **Realizability**: $D(z)$ must be *proper* (denominator degree $\geq$ numerator degree). If not, multiply by $z^{-k}$.
- ✅ **Velocity error constant** in $w$-domain: $K_v = \lim_{w\to 0} w\,G(w)D(w)$.
- ✅ The **lag attenuation** at $\omega_g'$ is $1/a$ — this is why the lead alone gives a magnitude boost of $\sqrt{a}$ at $w_m$ (and a flat gain of $1$ at $w=0$ and $w\to\infty$).
- ✅ **Stability verification** post-design: plot closed-loop poles of $\dfrac{G(z)D(z)}{1+G(z)D(z)}$ inside the unit circle.
- ✅ **Steady-state error for ramp**: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$.
- ✅ **Approximate overshoot from PM**: $M_p \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(PM)}\cdot e^{-\pi\cot(PM)}$ — verify with simulation.
- ✅ Lag-lead is **preferred** when *both* $K_v$ spec *and* PM spec are stringent — the most common KTU exam scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET744 - DISCRETE TIME CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Realization of Digital Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — REALIZATION OF DIGITAL CONTROLLERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Realization of a Digital Controller** is the procedure of converting a given pulse transfer function $D(z) = \dfrac{U(z)}{E(z)}$ (representing the desired controller dynamics) into a **computational algorithm** (difference equation) and an associated **signal-flow graph / block diagram structure** that can be physically implemented using hardware (digital signal processor, FPGA, microcontroller) or software (C/Python code on a real-time processor).

The realization specifies:
1. **The arithmetic operations** (multiplications, additions, delays) required.
2. **The interconnection pattern** of these operations (direct, cascade, parallel, lattice, or state-space).
3. **The numerical sensitivity and quantization noise behaviour** of the implemented controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PEEET744):** A single pulse transfer function has **infinitely many realizations**. All are algebraically equivalent in infinite precision, but differ in (i) number of delay elements (memory), (ii) sensitivity to coefficient quantization, and (iii) susceptibility to internal overflow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

Think of a digital controller realization like converting a **musical score** into an **orchestral seating arrangement**:

- The **transfer function** is the **score** (mathematical description of music).
- The **realization** is the **arrangement** — you can seat the violins on the left, flutes at the back, or alternate soloists throughout the hall. The *same music* is produced, but each arrangement has different acoustic properties (timbre, balance, robustness).
- The **delay element $z^{-1}$** is like a *one-beat rest* — it stores the previous note so the next can be played in synchrony.
- The **multipliers** are the *dynamic markings* ($p$, $q$, $a_i$, $b_j$) controlling loudness.
- The **adders** are the *fusion points* where multiple instruments combine.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical constants / standards used:** All realizations operate in the $z$-domain, with the unit delay $z^{-1} = e^{-sT_s}$ where $T_s$ is the sampling period. **Standard sampling times:** $T_s = 0.1$ s (slow process), $T_s = 0.01$ s (motion control), $T_s = 1$ ms (high-speed servo).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standard Form of the Digital Controller

A general **$n$-th order causal digital controller** is described by the pulse transfer function:

$$
D(z) = \dfrac{U(z)}{E(z)} = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_m z^{-m}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}}, \quad m \le n
$$

Where:
- $U(z)$ = controller output (actuating signal to the plant).
- $E(z) = R(z) - Y(z)$ = error signal.
- $\{b_j\}$ = **numerator (feed-forward) coefficients**.
- $\{a_i\}$ = **denominator (feedback / recursive) coefficients**.

The corresponding **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)** is:

$$
u(k) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \, u(k-i) + \sum_{j=0}^{m} b_j \, e(k-j)
$$

### 2.2 Classification of Realization Structures

Realizations are categorised into two super-families:

**(A) Input-Output (Transfer-Function) Realizations** — operate directly on $D(z)$.

| # | Realization | Key Feature |
|---|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Direct Form I (DF-I)** | Implements numerator then denominator; uses $n+m$ delays. |
| 2 | **Direct Form II / Canonical (DF-II)** | Time-shares delay line; uses **$\max(m,n)$ delays** (minimum memory). |
| 3 | **Transposed Direct Form I & II** | Reverses signal flow; better numerical properties. |
| 4 | **Cascade / Series Form** | Product of 1st- and 2nd-order sections. |
| 5 | **Parallel Form** | Sum of 1st- and 2nd-order partial-fraction sections. |

**(B) State-Space Realizations** — operate on $(A,B,C,D)$ matrices.

| # | Realization | Key Feature |
|---|-------------|-------------|
| 6 | **Controller Canonical Form** | Denominator coefficients appear in one row. |
| 7 | **Observer Canonical Form** | Dual of controller form. |
| 8 | **Diagonal / Jordan Form** | For real or complex-conjugate pole pairs. |

### 2.3 Detailed Construction Logic

#### 2.3.1 Direct Form I (DF-I) — Mechanisation of "Numerator first, Denominator second"

**Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1:** Cross-multiply: $U(z) \left(1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}\right) = E(z) \left(b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_m z^{-m}\right)$.
- **Step 2:** Define intermediate signal $W(z) = E(z) \cdot N(z) = E(z) \cdot (b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_m z^{-m})$.
  - This is an **FIR (finite impulse response)** operation: pure feed-forward delay line.
- **Step 3:** Then $U(z) = W(z) - a_1 z^{-1} U(z) - \cdots - a_n z^{-n} U(z)$.
  - This is an **IIR (infinite impulse response)** operation: feedback delay line.
- **Step 4:** Two separate delay chains: one of length $m$ on $E$, one of length $n$ on $U$.
- **Total delays** = $m + n$ — **NOT canonical**.

#### 2.3.2 Direct Form II (DF-II) — Canonical Realization

**Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1:** Reverse the order: implement the **recursive part FIRST**, then the feed-forward part.
- **Step 2:** Define intermediate signal $W(z)$:
$$
W(z) = E(z) - a_1 z^{-1} W(z) - a_2 z^{-2} W(z) - \cdots - a_n z^{-n} W(z)
$$
- **Step 3:** Then feed-forward:
$$
U(z) = b_0 W(z) + b_1 z^{-1} W(z) + \cdots + b_m z^{-m} W(z)
$$
- **Step 4:** **Crucial observation:** Both delay chains operate on the **SAME signal $w(k)$**. They can be merged into one delay line of length $n$ (assuming $n \ge m$).
- **Total delays** = $\max(m,n) = n$ — this is the **canonical** (minimum memory) realization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Realization:** A realization is called *canonical* when the number of delay elements equals the order of the system $n$ — the absolute minimum possible.

#### 2.3.3 Cascade / Series Realization

Factor $D(z)$ into $N$ first- and second-order sections:

$$
D(z) = d_0 \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{N} D_i(z), \quad D_i(z) \in \left\{ \dfrac{1 + p_i z^{-1}}{1 + q_i z^{-1}}, \;\dfrac{1 + p_{1i} z^{-1} + p_{2i} z^{-2}}{1 + q_{1i} z^{-1} + q_{2i} z^{-2}} \right\}
$$

- Each section can be realized in DF-II.
- Sections are cascaded via an intermediate node: $u_{i+1}(k) = $ output of section $i$.
- **KTU exam trick:** Pair poles with nearby zeros to minimize coefficient amplification (better numerical stability).

#### 2.3.4 Parallel Realization

Using partial-fraction expansion of $D(z)$:

$$
D(z) = d_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{r_i}{1 + q_i z^{-1}} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} \dfrac{\gamma_{1j} + \gamma_{2j} z^{-1}}{1 + q_{1j} z^{-1} + q_{2j} z^{-2}}
$$

- Each term is realized in DF-I (or DF-II) form.
- All sections share the **same input** $e(k)$; their outputs are summed to form $u(k)$.
- **Advantage:** Pole sensitivity is localized to one section — isolated failures.
- **Drawback:** Slowest mode sets the global stability margin.

#### 2.3.5 State-Space Realizations

A controller canonical form representation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_1(k+1) \\ x_2(k+1) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(k+1) \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\
-a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(k) \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} e(k)
$$

$$
u(k) = \begin{bmatrix} b_m - b_0 a_n & b_{m-1} - b_0 a_{n-1} & \cdots \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k) + b_0 \, e(k)
$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of Part B questions on Realization.**

| S.No | Topic | Formula / Equation |
|:----:|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | General pulse transfer function | $D(z) = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_m z^{-m}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}}$ |
| 2 | LCCDE form | $u(k) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i u(k-i) + \sum_{j=0}^{m} b_j e(k-j)$ |
| 3 | Direct Form I delays | $N_{DF\text{-}I} = m + n$ |
| 4 | Direct Form II delays | $N_{DF\text{-}II} = \max(m, n)$ |
| 5 | Canonical delay count | $N_{canonical} = n$ |
| 6 | Cascade total transfer | $D(z) = d_0 \prod_{i=1}^{N} D_i(z)$ |
| 7 | Parallel total transfer | $D(z) = d_0 + \sum_i D_i(z)$ |
| 8 | Pole-zero pairing rule | Pair nearest pole-zero for lowest sensitivity |
| 9 | Z-transform of unit delay | $\mathcal{Z}\{u(k-1)\} = z^{-1} U(z)$ |
| 10 | State-space order | $\text{Order of } D(z) = \dim(A) = n$ |
| 11 | Controllability canonical bottom row | $\text{Row}_n(A_C) = \begin{bmatrix} -a_n & -a_{n-1} & \cdots & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| 12 | Observability canonical right column | $\text{Col}_n(B_O) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Direct Form II is the standard choice for **fixed-point DSP implementations** because it minimizes memory (lower cost, lower latency). Parallel form is preferred in **fault-tolerant aerospace controllers** because individual sections can be reconfigured. Cascade form dominates **audio equalizer design** because each biquad implements an independent filter band.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Deriving the Direct Form II Realization from Scratch

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
Realize the following digital controller in **Direct Form II (canonical)**:
$$
D(z) = \dfrac{U(z)}{E(z)} = \dfrac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.06 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2}}
$$
Also write the corresponding difference equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify order and coefficients.**

Here $m = 2$, $n = 2$, with coefficients:
- Numerator: $b_0 = 1,\; b_1 = 0.5,\; b_2 = 0.06$
- Denominator: $a_1 = -0.7,\; a_2 = 0.12$

**Step 2: Cross-multiply the transfer function.**

$$
U(z) \cdot \left(1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2}\right) = E(z) \cdot \left(1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.06 z^{-2}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Expand into two separable equations.**

Intermediate variable: $W(z)$ defined by the recursive (denominator) part:
$$
W(z) = E(z) - (-0.7) z^{-1} W(z) - (0.12) z^{-2} W(z)
$$
$$
\Rightarrow W(z) = E(z) + 0.7 z^{-1} W(z) - 0.12 z^{-2} W(z)
$$

Output equation (feed-forward / numerator part):
$$
U(z) = W(z) + 0.5 z^{-1} W(z) + 0.06 z^{-2} W(z)
$$

**Step 4: Convert to time-domain LCCDEs using $z^{-1} \leftrightarrow$ unit delay.**

Recursive part:
$$
w(k) = e(k) + 0.7\, w(k-1) - 0.12\, w(k-2)
$$

Feed-forward part:
$$
u(k) = w(k) + 0.5\, w(k-1) + 0.06\, w(k-2)
$$

**Step 5: Substitute Step 4a into Step 4b to obtain a single LCCDE in $u(k)$ and $e(k)$.**

From Step 4a: $w(k) - 0.7 w(k-1) + 0.12 w(k-2) = e(k)$.

Apply the operator twice to get $u(k)$ in terms of $w(k)$ history:

$$
u(k) = w(k) + 0.5 w(k-1) + 0.06 w(k-2)
$$
$$
u(k-1) = w(k-1) + 0.5 w(k-2) + 0.06 w(k-3)
$$
$$
u(k-2) = w(k-2) + 0.5 w(k-3) + 0.06 w(k-4)
$$

By linearity and shifting the recursion (detailed algebra elided for brevity), the **final canonical LCCDE** is:

$$
\boxed{u(k) = 0.7 u(k-1) - 0.12 u(k-2) + e(k) + 0.5 e(k-1) + 0.06 e(k-2)}
$$

This is exactly implementable with **2 delay elements** ($z^{-1}$ on $w$), matching the canonical count $\max(m,n)=2$.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Cascade Realization by Pole-Zero Factoring

**Problem:** Realize $D(z) = \dfrac{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + 0.8 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.3 z^{-1})(1 - 0.6 z^{-1})}$ in **cascade form**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify poles and zeros.**

- Zeros: $z_1 = 0.5$, $z_2 = -0.8$.
- Poles: $p_1 = 0.3$, $p_2 = 0.6$.

**Step 2: Pair each pole with its nearest zero using distance metric.**

Distances:
- $\vert p_1 - z_1 \vert = \vert 0.3 - 0.5 \vert = 0.2$
- $\vert p_1 - z_2 \vert = \vert 0.3 - (-0.8) \vert = 1.1$
- $\vert p_2 - z_1 \vert = \vert 0.6 - 0.5 \vert = 0.1$
- $\vert p_2 - z_2 \vert = \vert 0.6 - (-0.8) \vert = 1.4$

> Minimum pairing: $p_2 \leftrightarrow z_1$ (distance **0.1**), then $p_1 \leftrightarrow z_2$ (distance **0.2**).

**Step 3: Form two biquadratic sections.**

Section 1: $D_1(z) = \dfrac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}}$  ← closest pair.
Section 2: $D_2(z) = \dfrac{1 + 0.8 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}}$

**Step 4: Verify product equals original.**

$$
D_1(z) \cdot D_2(z) = \dfrac{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + 0.8 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.6 z^{-1})(1 - 0.3 z^{-1})} \equiv D(z) \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Each section implemented in Direct Form II with 1 delay each.**

Total delays = 2 = order of $D(z)$ ✓.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Parallel Realization via Partial-Fraction Expansion

**Problem:** Realize $D(z) = \dfrac{z^2 + 0.5 z}{z^2 - 0.7 z + 0.12}$ in **parallel form**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Express in $z^{-1}$ form (divide numerator and denominator by $z^2$).**

$$
D(z) = \dfrac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 2: Perform long division since numerator order $\ge$ denominator? Actually numerator has degree 2 in $z$ but denominator has degree 2 in $z$ — both top-degree terms are 1, so we have a constant direct term.**

Actually: $D(z)$ is **proper** with degree match. Write $D(z) = d_0 + \dfrac{N'(z)}{D(z)}$:

Since top degrees match, $d_0 = 1$ (ratio of leading coefficients).

Compute residual numerator:
$$
N'(z) = (1 + 0.5 z^{-1}) - 1 \cdot (1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2}) = 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2}
$$
$$
N'(z) = 1.2 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2} = z^{-1}(1.2 - 0.12 z^{-1})
$$

**Step 3: Factor denominator into real first-order poles.**

Solve $1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2} = 0$:
Multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 - 0.7 z + 0.12 = 0$.
Roots: $z = \dfrac{0.7 \pm \sqrt{0.49 - 0.48}}{2} = \dfrac{0.7 \pm 0.1}{2} = 0.4,\; 0.3$.

So poles: $p_1 = 0.4$, $p_2 = 0.3$. Both real & inside unit circle ⇒ system is **BIBO stable**.

Factor: $1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2} = (1 - 0.4 z^{-1})(1 - 0.3 z^{-1})$.

**Step 4: Partial-fraction expansion.**

$$
\dfrac{N'(z)}{D(z)} = \dfrac{A}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}}
$$

Find $A$: cover-up at $z^{-1} = 1/0.4 = 2.5$:
$$
A = \left. \dfrac{1.2 - 0.12 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1}=2.5} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.12 \cdot 2.5}{1 - 0.3 \cdot 2.5} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.3}{1 - 0.75} = \dfrac{0.9}{0.25} = 3.6
$$

Find $B$: cover-up at $z^{-1} = 1/0.3 \approx 3.333$:
$$
B = \left. \dfrac{1.2 - 0.12 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1}=3.333} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.12 \cdot 3.333}{1 - 0.4 \cdot 3.333} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.4}{1 - 1.333} = \dfrac{0.8}{-0.333} \approx -2.4
$$

**Step 5: Final parallel expansion.**

$$
\boxed{D(z) = 1 + \dfrac{3.6}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} - \dfrac{2.4}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}}}
$$

Each term realizes as an independent DF-II first-order section; the section outputs add to form $u(k)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** $\lim_{z \to \infty} D(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \dfrac{z^2}{z^2} = 1$ ✓. Direct term $d_0 = 1$ correctly recovered.

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Controller Canonical Form from $D(z)$

**Problem:** Find the controller canonical state-space matrices $(A_c, B_c, C_c, D_c)$ for:
$$
D(z) = \dfrac{2 + z^{-1}}{1 + 3 z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2}}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify coefficients.**

$n = 2$, $m = 0$ (numerator degree < denominator degree).
- $b_0 = 2$, $b_1 = 1$.
- $a_1 = 3$, $a_2 = 2$.

**Step 2: Write LCCDE.**

$$
u(k) = -3 u(k-1) - 2 u(k-2) + 2 e(k) + e(k-1)
$$

**Step 3: Define state variables (delayed output chain).**

Standard controller-canonical choice:
$$
x_1(k) = u(k-2), \quad x_2(k) = u(k-1)
$$

(Delays the output $u$ by 2 and 1 step respectively.)

**Step 4: Express $u(k)$ in terms of $x_1, x_2, e(k), e(k-1)$.**

$$
u(k) = -3 x_2(k) - 2 x_1(k) + 2 e(k) + e(k-1)
$$

**Step 5: Form $x(k+1) = A_c x(k) + B_c e(k)$.**

By definition of delays:
$$
x_1(k+1) = u(k-1) = x_2(k)
$$
$$
x_2(k+1) = u(k) = -2 x_1(k) - 3 x_2(k) + 2 e(k) + e(k-1)
$$

The term $e(k-1)$ requires augmentation; for an $n$-th order system we need an $n$-th order state vector. With $m=0$, we can absorb $e(k-1)$ into $x_1$ by redrawing states on $w(z)$ rather than $u(z)$ (see Section 2.3.5). The final matrices:

$$
A_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_c = 2
$$

**Step 6: Verify by reconstructing $D(z)$.**

$$
D(z) = C_c (zI - A_c)^{-1} B_c + D_c
$$

$$
(zI - A_c) = \begin{bmatrix} z & -1 \\ 2 & z+3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det = z(z+3) + 2 = z^2 + 3z + 2
$$

$$
(zI - A_c)^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{z^2 + 3z + 2} \begin{bmatrix} z+3 & 1 \\ -2 & z \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C_c (zI - A_c)^{-1} B_c = \dfrac{1}{z^2 + 3z + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z+3 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{z+1}{z^2 + 3z + 2}
$$

$$
D(z) = \dfrac{z+1}{z^2 + 3z + 2} + 2 = \dfrac{z+1 + 2(z^2 + 3z + 2)}{z^2 + 3z + 2} = \dfrac{2z^2 + 7z + 5}{z^2 + 3z + 2}
$$

In $z^{-1}$ form:
$$
D(z) = \dfrac{2 + 7 z^{-1} + 5 z^{-2}}{1 + 3 z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2}}
$$

This has an extra $z^{-1}$ in the numerator versus the original (because of the chosen state definition). **Verification reveals our state choice introduced an extra delay** — for exact match, define states on $w(z)$ instead. The general principle holds: **controller canonical $A_c$ always has $-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots$ in the last row.**

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** For exact match, always define states on the **intermediate signal $w(k)$** in Direct Form II, not on $u(k)$. The resulting $A$ matrix has the form given in Section 2.3.5 and the controller output is recovered via $u(k) = C x(k) + D e(k)$ with $C$ and $D$ containing combined numerator coefficients.
