# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is meant by realization of a digital controller? List any two advantages of Direct Form II over Direct Form I.**

**Model Answer:**
Realization of a digital controller is the process of converting the pulse transfer function $D(z)$ into a computational structure (difference equation + block diagram) that can be implemented on hardware/software.

**Advantages of Direct Form II over Direct Form I:**
1. **Canonical (minimum memory):** Uses $\max(m,n)$ delay elements instead of $m+n$.
2. **Reduced quantization error:** A single delay chain means coefficients $a_i$ and $b_j$ are exposed to fewer round-off operations.
3. **Lower computational latency:** Fewer registers mean faster sampling-throughput.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between cascade and parallel realization of a digital controller.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cascade Realization | Parallel Realization |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Structure | Product of sections $D(z) = \prod D_i(z)$ | Sum of sections $D(z) = d_0 + \sum D_i(z)$ |
| Signal flow | Series — output of one is input of next | Parallel — common input, summed output |
| Synthesis method | Pole-zero factoring and pairing | Partial-fraction expansion |
| Sensitivity | Distributed across all sections | Localized per section (fault tolerant) |
| Best for | Audio equalizers, biquad filter banks | Aerospace controllers, modular designs |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Realize the following pulse transfer function in **Direct Form II (canonical)** and write the corresponding difference equation:
$$
D(z) = \dfrac{1 + 0.4 z^{-1} - 0.32 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}}
$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] Realize the same controller in **cascade form** by appropriate pole-zero pairing. Comment on the effect of pairing on coefficient magnitude.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Identify $m=2$, $n=2$, coefficients: $b_0=1, b_1=0.4, b_2=-0.32$; $a_1=-0.8, a_2=0.15$.

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply:
$$
U(z) (1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}) = E(z)(1 + 0.4 z^{-1} - 0.32 z^{-2})
$$

**Step 3:** Define intermediate $W(z)$ (DF-II canonical):
$$
W(z) = E(z) + 0.8 z^{-1} W(z) - 0.15 z^{-2} W(z)
$$
$$
U(z) = W(z) + 0.4 z^{-1} W(z) - 0.32 z^{-2} W(z)
$$

**Step 4:** Time-domain:
$$
w(k) = e(k) + 0.8 w(k-1) - 0.15 w(k-2)
$$
$$
u(k) = w(k) + 0.4 w(k-1) - 0.32 w(k-2)
$$

**Step 5:** Eliminate $w$:
$$
\boxed{u(k) = 0.8 u(k-1) - 0.15 u(k-2) + e(k) + 0.4 e(k-1) - 0.32 e(k-2)}
$$

**Delays used:** $\max(m,n) = 2$ ✓ canonical.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Find poles of $1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2} = 0$.
Multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 - 0.8 z + 0.15 = 0$.
Roots: $z = \dfrac{0.8 \pm \sqrt{0.64 - 0.6}}{2} = \dfrac{0.8 \pm 0.2}{2} = 0.5, 0.3$.

**Step 2:** Find zeros of $1 + 0.4 z^{-1} - 0.32 z^{-2} = 0$.
Multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 + 0.4 z - 0.32 = 0$.
Roots: $z = \dfrac{-0.4 \pm \sqrt{0.16 + 1.28}}{2} = \dfrac{-0.4 \pm 1.2}{2} = 0.4, -0.8$.

**Step 3:** Pair by minimum distance:
- $\vert 0.5 - 0.4 \vert = 0.1$ → pair $p_1 = 0.5$ with $z_1 = 0.4$.
- $\vert 0.3 - (-0.8) \vert = 1.1$ → pair $p_2 = 0.3$ with $z_2 = -0.8$.

**Step 4:** Cascade sections:
$$
D_1(z) = \dfrac{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}, \quad D_2(z) = \dfrac{1 + 0.8 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}}
$$

**Comment on coefficient magnitude:** Optimal pairing produces sections with coefficients $\le 1$, ensuring bounded internal node amplitudes and **lower round-off noise**. Poor pairing (e.g., $p_1 \leftrightarrow z_2$) would amplify intermediate signals and degrade numerical stability.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Realize $D(z) = \dfrac{2(1 - 0.6 z^{-1})(1 + 0.25 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.3 z^{-1})(1 + 0.4 z^{-1})}$ in **parallel form** using partial-fraction expansion. Also find the controller canonical state-space realization.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the same controller, comment on the **BIBO stability** by locating the poles and discuss the **steady-state response** to a unit step input $R(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Expand the transfer function in $z^{-1}$ form.

Numerator: $(1 - 0.6 z^{-1})(1 + 0.25 z^{-1}) = 1 + 0.25 z^{-1} - 0.6 z^{-1} - 0.15 z^{-2} = 1 - 0.35 z^{-1} - 0.15 z^{-2}$.
Denominator: $(1 - 0.3 z^{-1})(1 + 0.4 z^{-1}) = 1 + 0.4 z^{-1} - 0.3 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2} = 1 + 0.1 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2}$.

So:
$$
D(z) = \dfrac{2(1 - 0.35 z^{-1} - 0.15 z^{-2})}{1 + 0.1 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 2:** Perform long division — top degree matches, so $d_0 = 2$.
$$
N'(z) = 2(1 - 0.35 z^{-1} - 0.15 z^{-2}) - 2(1 + 0.1 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2})
$$
$$
= 2 - 0.7 z^{-1} - 0.3 z^{-2} - 2 - 0.2 z^{-1} + 0.24 z^{-2}
$$
$$
= -0.9 z^{-1} - 0.06 z^{-2}
$$

**Step 3:** Factor denominator to get individual poles.

$1 + 0.1 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2} = 0$ → multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 + 0.1 z - 0.12 = 0$.
Roots: $z = \dfrac{-0.1 \pm \sqrt{0.01 + 0.48}}{2} = \dfrac{-0.1 \pm 0.7}{2} = 0.3, -0.4$.

So poles $p_1 = 0.3$, $p_2 = -0.4$.

**Step 4:** Partial fractions:
$$
D(z) = 2 + \dfrac{A}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}}
$$

Compute $A$ (cover-up at $z^{-1} = 1/0.3 \approx 3.333$):
$$
A = \left. \dfrac{-0.9 - 0.06 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = 3.333} = \dfrac{-0.9 - 0.06 \cdot 3.333}{1 + 0.4 \cdot 3.333} = \dfrac{-0.9 - 0.2}{1 + 1.333} = \dfrac{-1.1}{2.333} \approx -0.471
$$

Compute $B$ (cover-up at $z^{-1} = -1/0.4 = -2.5$):
$$
B = \left. \dfrac{-0.9 - 0.06 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = -2.5} = \dfrac{-0.9 - 0.06 \cdot (-2.5)}{1 - 0.3 \cdot (-2.5)} = \dfrac{-0.9 + 0.15}{1 + 0.75} = \dfrac{-0.75}{1.75} \approx -0.429
$$

**Final Parallel Form:**
$$
\boxed{D(z) = 2 + \dfrac{-0.471}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{-0.429}{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}}}
$$

**Controller Canonical Form:** Denominator is $1 + 0.1 z^{-1} - 0.12 z^{-2}$, i.e., $a_1 = 0.1$, $a_2 = -0.12$, $b_0 = 2$, $b_1 = -0.7$, $b_2 = -0.3$.

$$
A_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0.12 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
C_c = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 - b_0 a_1 & b_0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7 - 0.2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_c = b_0 = 2
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Stability:** Poles are at $p_1 = 0.3$ and $p_2 = -0.4$. Both satisfy $\vert p_i \vert < 1$, hence **inside the unit circle**. Therefore the controller is **BIBO stable**.

**Steady-state response (Final Value Theorem):**
$$
u_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} \left(1 - z^{-1}\right) D(z) R(z), \quad R(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$
$$
u_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} D(z) = D(1) = \dfrac{2(1 - 0.6)(1 + 0.25)}{(1 - 0.3)(1 + 0.4)} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 1.25}{0.7 \cdot 1.4} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.98} \approx 1.0204
$$

So the steady-state controller output for unit step error is approximately **1.02** — slightly overshooting unity (gain margin at DC = **1.02**).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the canonical delay count** — writing $m + n$ delays for DF-II (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Improper pole-zero pairing in cascade** — always compute distances and state the pairing rule (lose up to 2 marks).
> 3. **Missing the direct term $d_0$** in parallel realization when numerator/denominator degrees are equal (lose 2 marks).
> 4. **Sign errors on feedback coefficients** — recall $D(z)$ denominator is $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots$, so LCCDE has $-a_i u(k-i)$ (lose 2 marks).
> 5. **Forgetting to verify BIBO stability** — always comment on $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ (lose 1 mark).
> 6. **Drawing DF-II without merging the delay chain** — full marks only when both delay lines are explicitly shown merged on $w(k)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Module 2: Realization of Digital Controllers**

- A **realization** converts $D(z)$ into a difference equation + block diagram.
- The **same $D(z)$ has many realizations**; they differ in memory, sensitivity, and parallelism.
- **Direct Form I** uses $m + n$ delays (non-canonical); easy to derive, but wasteful.
- **Direct Form II** uses $\max(m,n)$ delays — the **canonical** (minimum memory) realization.
- Always cross-multiply $D(z) \cdot$ Denominator $=$ Numerator $\cdot E(z)$ before drawing.
- The **intermediate signal $w(k)$** in DF-II is the recursion variable; $u(k)$ is its filtered feed-forward version.
- **Cascade (Series) Form**: $D(z) = d_0 \prod D_i(z)$. **Always pair poles with nearest zeros** to keep coefficients small.
- **Parallel Form**: $D(z) = d_0 + \sum D_i(z)$. Obtained via partial-fraction expansion. Best when **fault isolation** matters.
- **State-space canonical forms** (controller, observer) consume exactly $n$ state variables = $n$ delays.
- **Controller canonical $A_c$ last row** = $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$.
- **BIBO stability** = all poles strictly inside unit circle ($\vert p_i \vert < 1$).
- **LCCDE standard form**: $u(k) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i u(k-i) + \sum_{j=0}^{m} b_j e(k-j)$.
- **DF-II is preferred in fixed-point DSP**; **parallel form preferred in safety-critical control**; **cascade preferred in audio/equalizer applications**.
- **Transposed Direct Forms** reverse the signal-flow direction and are often more robust to overflow — used in hardware FPGA implementations.
- Total number of **multiplications per sample** for DF-II = $m + n + 1$; for DF-I = same. So both have identical computational cost — DF-II only saves **memory**.
- For high-order ($n > 3$) systems, prefer **cascade biquads** to control numerical errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Variable Model of Discrete Data Systems with S/H Devices - State Transition Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET744 - DISCRETE TIME CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** State Variable Model of Discrete Data Systems with S/H Devices - State Transition Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Discrete Data System** is a control system in which one or more signals are sampled (discrete in time) but may be continuous in amplitude. When a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) device** — specifically the **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** — is placed at the input of a continuous-time plant, the resulting **hybrid (sampled-data) system** can be described by a state-variable model expressed entirely at the sampling instants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box — State Variable Model of a Sampled-Data System with S/H**
> A continuous-time plant $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t), \; y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$ preceded by an ideal **Zero-Order Hold** is exactly equivalent, **only at the sampling instants $t = kT$**, to the discrete-time state model:
> $$x[(k+1)T] = \Phi(T)\,x(kT) + \Gamma(T)\,u(kT)$$
> $$y(kT) = C\,x(kT) + D\,u(kT)$$
> where $\Phi(T) = e^{AT}$ is the **state transition matrix** and $\Gamma(T) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \, B$ is the **discrete input matrix**.

The **State Transition Equation** is the closed-form solution of the discrete state equation, which expresses the state at any sampling instant $k$ in terms of the initial state and the past input sequence:
$$x(kT) = \Phi^{k}(T)\,x(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \Phi^{k-1-i}(T)\,\Gamma(T)\,u(iT)$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase Photograph" Intuition

Imagine you are watching a **continuous movie** of a swinging pendulum (this is the continuous plant). Now, suppose a low-budget news channel decides to record the motion using a camera that:
- **Takes a snapshot** of the pendulum position every $T$ seconds (this is the **sampler**), and
- **Freezes that snapshot** on the screen until the next snapshot arrives (this is the **Zero-Order Hold**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy**
> - **Sampler** = Camera shutter click every $T$ seconds.
> - **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** = The frozen photograph held on screen — the value stays *constant* (zero-order polynomial) until the next click.
> - **State Transition Matrix $\Phi(T)$** = The "physics rulebook" that tells the pendulum: *"Given where you were at click $k$, here is where you will be at click $k+1$, assuming no external push."*
> - **Discrete Input Matrix $\Gamma(T)$** = The accumulated effect of all the pushes that occurred *during* the frozen photograph interval.

The **S/H device** is essential in practice because:
1. The DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter) used between the digital controller and the analog plant inherently holds its output constant between updates.
2. The ZOH is the **most common, simplest, and most realistic** model of this physical behavior.
3. It allows us to **ignore the inter-sample behavior** and work purely with the sequence of values at $t = 0, T, 2T, 3T, \ldots$

### 1.3 Standard KTU Notation & Units

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit / Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T$ | **Sampling period** | **seconds (s)** |
| $f_s = 1/T$ | **Sampling frequency** | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| $A$ | Continuous system matrix | $n \times n$ |
| $B$ | Continuous input matrix | $n \times p$ |
| $C$ | Output matrix | $q \times n$ |
| $\Phi(T)$ | Discrete state transition matrix | $n \times n$ |
| $\Gamma(T)$ | Discrete input matrix | $n \times p$ |
| $k$ | Discrete time index | dimensionless integer |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always state clearly that the equivalence between the continuous plant + ZOH and the discrete model holds **only at the sampling instants**. Between samples, the continuous system continues to evolve, but the discrete model does not describe it.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This is a **high-weightage** topic because it is the **bridge** between the continuous-time state space (Module 1/2) and the discrete-time controller design methods (Module 4/5). Without a correct discrete state model, you cannot:
- Design a **state feedback digital controller** (Dead-Beat, Pole Placement)
- Build a **Kalman Filter** for estimation
- Perform **Lyapunov stability** analysis of digital systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PEEET744 Module 3):**
> *"State variable model of discrete data systems with sample and hold devices — derivation of state transition equations and their solution."*
> Expected cognitive level (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy): **Apply (Level 3) and Analyze (Level 4)** — students must derive, simplify, and compute discrete matrices for a given continuous plant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) — Working Principle

A **Zero-Order Hold** reconstructs a continuous-time signal $u(t)$ from its samples $u(kT)$ using a **zero-order polynomial** (i.e., a constant). Mathematically:

$$u(t) = u(kT), \quad \text{for } kT \le t < (k+1)T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Consequence of ZOH:** Within the interval $[kT, (k+1)T]$, the input to the plant is **not a unit impulse** (as some beginners incorrectly assume) but a **rectangular pulse of constant height** $u(kT)$ and **width** $T$.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation Logic

**Step 1 — Start with the continuous plant:**
$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $u(t)$ is constant over one sampling interval** (due to ZOH):
$$u(t) = u(kT) = \text{constant}, \quad \text{for } kT \le t < (k+1)T$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear ODE with constant forcing** over the interval $[kT, (k+1)T]$:
The solution of $\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(kT)$ with initial condition $x(kT)$ is given by the variation-of-parameters formula:
$$x(t) = e^{A(t - kT)}\,x(kT) + \int_{kT}^{t} e^{A(t - \tau)}\,B\,u(kT)\,d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate only at the next sampling instant $t = (k+1)T$:**

Let $\sigma = (k+1)T - \tau$. When $\tau$ goes from $kT$ to $(k+1)T$, $\sigma$ goes from $T$ to $0$, and $d\tau = -d\sigma$:
$$x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}\,x(kT) + \left(\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\sigma}\,d\sigma\right) B\,u(kT)$$

**Step 5 — Define the discrete matrices:**
$$\Phi(T) = e^{AT}, \qquad \Gamma(T) = \left(\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau\right) B$$

**Step 6 — Write the final compact discrete state equation:**
$$\boxed{x[(k+1)T] = \Phi(T)\,x(kT) + \Gamma(T)\,u(kT)}$$

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why exponentiate $A$?** Because the matrix exponential $e^{A(t-t_0)}$ is the **state transition matrix** of the continuous (unforced) system — it propagates the state forward in time.
- **Why integrate from 0 to $T$?** Because during one sample period, the ZOH output is constant, so we accumulate the *integrated effect* of that constant input.
- **Why ignore $D\,u(kT)$ in state equation?** The matrix $D$ only appears in the output equation and does not affect the dynamics.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are guaranteed to appear in KTU examinations.**

| # | Formula | Name / Description | Dimensions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $\Phi(T) = e^{AT} = I + AT + \dfrac{(AT)^2}{2!} + \dfrac{(AT)^3}{3!} + \cdots$ | **State Transition Matrix** (Taylor series form) | $n \times n$ |
| 2 | $\Gamma(T) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \cdot B$ | **Discrete Input Matrix** | $n \times p$ |
| 3 | $\Gamma(T) = A^{-1}\left(e^{AT} - I\right)B$ (if $A$ is nonsingular) | **Alternative form** of $\Gamma$ | $n \times p$ |
| 4 | $x[(k+1)T] = \Phi(T)x(kT) + \Gamma(T)u(kT)$ | **Discrete State Equation** | $n \times 1$ |
| 5 | $y(kT) = Cx(kT) + Du(kT)$ | **Discrete Output Equation** | $q \times 1$ |
| 6 | $x(kT) = \Phi^{k}(T)x(0) + \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\Phi^{k-1-i}(T)\Gamma(T)u(iT)$ | **General State Transition Equation** | $n \times 1$ |
| 7 | $\Phi(0) = I$ | **Identity property** at $t=0$ | scalar |
| 8 | $\Phi[(k+m)T] = \Phi^{k}(T)\cdot\Phi^{m}(T)$ | **Semigroup (transition) property** | $n \times n$ |
| 9 | $\Phi^{-1}(T) = \Phi(-T) = e^{-AT}$ | **Inverse / reversibility** property | $n \times n$ |
| 10 | $\det[\Phi(T)] = e^{\text{tr}(A)\cdot T}$ | **Determinant property** (always nonzero) | scalar |

> **Note on notation in tables:** Absolute value and determinant are written as `\vert \cdot \vert` and `\det[\cdot]` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Properties of the State Transition Matrix $\Phi(T)$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Properties — memorize with proofs:**

1. **$\Phi(0) = I$** — At zero elapsed time, the state does not change.
2. **$\Phi(T_1 + T_2) = \Phi(T_1)\,\Phi(T_2)$** — Transitioning for $T_1 + T_2$ is the same as first $T_1$, then $T_2$.
3. **$\Phi^{-1}(T) = \Phi(-T)$** — The matrix is always invertible because $\det(\Phi) = e^{\text{tr}(A)T} \ne 0$ for finite $T$.
4. **$\Phi(kT) = [\Phi(T)]^{k}$** — Integer multiples compose by matrix powers.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Motion Control:** Industrial servo drives (e.g., CNC machines) use ZOH-based DACs to deliver piecewise-constant voltage to the motor windings; the discrete state model is what the digital controller actually "sees."
- **Power Electronics:** Inverters and DC-DC converters apply constant duty-ratio pulses (a form of ZOH) every switching period $T_s$.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** LiDAR samples the environment every $T$ ms; the path-planner's state model is discrete even though the world is continuous.
- **Aerospace GNC:** Spacecraft thrusters fire in pulses; the discrete state transition equation predicts attitude evolution between firings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Continuous ODE to Discrete State Equation

**Given:** Continuous-time plant
$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)$$

with ZOH input $u(t) = u(kT)$ constant over $[kT, (k+1)T]$.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the ODE into the standard linear form** by multiplying both sides by $e^{-At}$:
$$e^{-At}\dot{x}(t) - e^{-At}A\,x(t) = e^{-At}B\,u(kT)$$

The left side is exactly the derivative of the product $e^{-At}x(t)$:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-At}x(t)\right] = e^{-At}B\,u(kT)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides** from $kT$ to $(k+1)T$:
$$\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} \frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-At}x(t)\right] dt = \int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{-At}B\,u(kT)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left integral** using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:
$$e^{-A(k+1)T}x[(k+1)T] - e^{-AkT}x(kT) = \left(\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{-At}\,dt\right) B\,u(kT)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x[(k+1)T]$** by multiplying both sides on the left by $e^{A(k+1)T}$:
$$x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}x(kT) + e^{A(k+1)T}\left(\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{-At}\,dt\right) B\,u(kT)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute** $\tau = t - kT$ in the integral. When $t = kT$, $\tau = 0$; when $t = (k+1)T$, $\tau = T$; and $e^{-At} = e^{-A(kT+\tau)} = e^{-AkT}e^{-A\tau}$:
$$e^{A(k+1)T}\int_{0}^{T} e^{-AkT}e^{-A\tau}\,d\tau = e^{A(k+1)T}e^{-AkT}\int_{0}^{T} e^{-A\tau}\,d\tau = e^{AT}\int_{0}^{T}e^{-A\tau}\,d\tau$$

Let $\sigma = -\tau$ (so $d\sigma = -d\tau$); when $\tau = 0$, $\sigma = 0$; when $\tau = T$, $\sigma = -T$:
$$e^{AT}\int_{0}^{T}e^{-A\tau}\,d\tau = e^{AT}\int_{0}^{-T}e^{A\sigma}(-d\sigma) = e^{AT}\int_{-T}^{0}e^{A\sigma}\,d\sigma$$

**Step 6 — Apply the substitution** $\sigma' = T + \sigma$ (so when $\sigma = -T$, $\sigma' = 0$; when $\sigma = 0$, $\sigma' = T$; $d\sigma = d\sigma'$):
$$e^{AT}\int_{0}^{T}e^{A(\sigma'-T)}\,d\sigma' = e^{AT}e^{-AT}\int_{0}^{T}e^{A\sigma'}\,d\sigma' = \int_{0}^{T}e^{A\sigma'}\,d\sigma'$$

**Step 7 — Rename** $\sigma'$ as $\tau$ and collect terms**:
$$x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}x(kT) + \left(\int_{0}^{T}e^{A\tau}\,d\tau\right) B\,u(kT)$$

**Step 8 — Define the discrete matrices** and obtain the canonical discrete state equation:
$$\boxed{x[(k+1)T] = \Phi(T)x(kT) + \Gamma(T)u(kT)}$$
with
$$\Phi(T) = e^{AT}, \qquad \Gamma(T) = \int_{0}^{T}e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \cdot B$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Examiners often award partial credit for *recognizing the correct form of $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$* even if the integration step is skipped. Always write both definitions explicitly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form State Transition Equation (Iterative Solution)

**Given:** The discrete state equation $x[(k+1)T] = \Phi(T)x(kT) + \Gamma(T)u(kT)$ with initial state $x(0)$.

**Step 1 — Write the recurrence for $k = 0$:**
$$x(T) = \Phi x(0) + \Gamma u(0)$$

**Step 2 — For $k = 1$:**
$$x(2T) = \Phi x(T) + \Gamma u(T) = \Phi[\Phi x(0) + \Gamma u(0)] + \Gamma u(T) = \Phi^{2}x(0) + \Phi\Gamma u(0) + \Gamma u(T)$$

**Step 3 — For $k = 2$:**
$$x(3T) = \Phi x(2T) + \Gamma u(2T) = \Phi^{3}x(0) + \Phi^{2}\Gamma u(0) + \Phi\Gamma u(T) + \Gamma u(2T)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the general pattern** by induction. After $k$ iterations, we obtain:
$$x(kT) = \Phi^{k}x(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\Phi^{k-1-i}\Gamma u(iT)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the two physically distinct terms:**
- **Zero-input response:** $x_{zs}(kT) = \Phi^{k}x(0)$ — state evolution due to initial conditions alone.
- **Zero-state response:** $x_{zi}(kT) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\Phi^{k-1-i}\Gamma u(iT)$ — contribution from past inputs.

> [!TIP]
> **Compact Matrix Form (Convolution Sum):** Using block diagonal notation, the state transition equation can be written as a discrete convolution, which is the state-space analog of the $z$-transfer function $G(z) = C(zI - \Phi)^{-1}\Gamma + D$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Computing $\Phi(T)$ and $\Gamma(T)$ for a Scalar System

**Given:** Single-input scalar system $\dot{x} = ax + bu$ with sampling period $T$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Phi(T)$ using the Taylor series of $e^{aT}$:**
$$\Phi(T) = e^{aT} = 1 + aT + \frac{(aT)^{2}}{2!} + \frac{(aT)^{3}}{3!} + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Gamma(T)$ by direct integration:**
$$\Gamma(T) = \int_{0}^{T}e^{a\tau}\,d\tau \cdot b = b\left[\frac{e^{a\tau}}{a}\right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{b}{a}\left(e^{aT} - 1\right)$$

For the special case $a = 0$: $\Gamma(T) = bT$.

**Step 3 — Verify by the alternative formula** $\Gamma = A^{-1}(\Phi - I)B$:
$$\frac{1}{a}\left(e^{aT} - 1\right) \cdot b = \frac{b}{a}\left(e^{aT} - 1\right) \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Example — Second-Order System

**Problem:** A continuous plant is given by
$$\dot{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} x(t) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(t)$$

A ZOH precedes the plant with sampling period $T = 1$ s. **Find $\Phi(1)$ and $\Gamma(1)$.**

**Step 1 — Compute $A^{2}$ and $A^{3}$ (required for the truncated series):**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A^{2} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A^{3} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 7 \\ -14 & -15 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Phi(1) = I + A + \dfrac{A^{2}}{2!} + \dfrac{A^{3}}{3!} + \dfrac{A^{4}}{4!} + \cdots$**

Truncating after the third term (acceptable for KTU since higher terms decay quickly):
$$\Phi(1) \approx I + A + \frac{A^{2}}{2} + \frac{A^{3}}{6}$$

$$\Phi(1) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1.5 \\ 3 & 3.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1.167 \\ -2.333 & -2.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Adding term by term:
$$\Phi(1) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.667 \\ -1.333 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Gamma(1)$ using** $\Gamma = A^{-1}(e^{A} - I)B$ (requires inverse of $A$):
$$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det A}\text{adj}(A) = \frac{1}{(0)(-3) - (1)(-2)} \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -1 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -3 & -1 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula:**
$$\Gamma(1) = A^{-1}(\Phi - I)B = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.667 \\ -1.333 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.667 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 \times 0.667 + (-0.5)(-2) \\ 1 \times 0.667 + 0 \times (-2) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} -1.0 + 1.0 \\ 0.667 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.667 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of the example:** The first row of $\Gamma$ is zero because the first state $x_1$ is the *output position* which cannot change *instantaneously* under a constant-force input over an infinitesimal time — a physically sensible result.

---

### 3.5 Stability Insight from $\Phi(T)$

A discrete system $x[(k+1)T] = \Phi x(kT) + \Gamma u(kT)$ is **asymptotically stable** if and only if all eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $\Phi$ lie strictly inside the unit circle in the $z$-plane:
$$\vert \lambda_i(\Phi) \vert < 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to continuous stability:** If all eigenvalues $\mu_i$ of $A$ satisfy $\text{Re}(\mu_i) < 0$ (continuous left half-plane), then for **any** sampling period $T > 0$, the eigenvalues $\lambda_i = e^{\mu_i T}$ of $\Phi$ automatically satisfy $\vert \lambda_i \vert = e^{\text{Re}(\mu_i)T} < 1$. Hence, if the continuous plant is stable, the discretized model is also stable for *any* choice of $T$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Discrete Data System with S/H

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **canonical architecture** of a sampled-data system in which a digital controller drives a continuous plant through a ZOH. The state variable model developed in this module characterizes the **continuous plant + ZOH block** as a single discrete-time equivalent.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference r(kT)"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> CTRL["Digital Controller D(z)"]
    CTRL --> DA["DAC / ZOH u(t)"]
    DA --> PLANT["Continuous Plant G(s) = C(sI-A)^-1 B"]
    PLANT --> Y["Continuous Output y(t)"]
    Y --> SAM["Sampler"]
    SAM --> FB["Feedback Signal y(kT)"]
    FB --> SUM1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The signal flow moves from the **discrete reference** $r(kT)$, through the **digital controller**, into the **DAC/ZOH** which produces the piecewise-constant continuous signal $u(t)$.
- The **plant** evolves continuously; its output $y(t)$ is sampled to form the discrete feedback $y(kT)$.
- The **state variable model derived in this module replaces the "DAC/ZOH + Plant" combination** with the equivalent discrete system $x[(k+1)T] = \Phi x(kT) + \Gamma u(kT)$.

---

### 4.2 State Transition Flow Diagram (Z-Domain)

The state transition equation
$$x(kT) = \Phi^{k}x(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\Phi^{k-1-i}\Gamma u(iT)$$

can be visualized as a **signal flow** in which the state at time $k$ is the sum of (a) the natural evolution of the initial state and (b) a weighted sum of past inputs:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0["x(0)"] --> P1["Phi to power k"]
    P1 --> XK["x(kT)"]
    U0["u(0)"] --> G1["Gamma"]
    G1 --> M1["Phi to power k-1"]
    M1 --> XK
    U1["u(T)"] --> G2["Gamma"]
    G2 --> M2["Phi to power k-2"]
    M2 --> XK
    UI["u(iT)"] --> GI["Gamma"]
    GI --> MI["Phi to power k-1-i"]
    MI --> XK
    XK --> P2["Phi"]
    P2 --> XK1["x((k+1)T)"]
```

---

### 4.3 Computational Algorithm (Conceptual Flow)

The following flowchart summarizes the **algorithmic procedure** a KTU student should follow when asked to "obtain the discrete state model of a continuous plant with ZOH":

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Start: Continuous plant A, B, C, D and sampling period T"] --> A2["Step 1: Form matrix AT and compute e to power AT via Taylor series or Laplace method"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 2: Set Phi = e to power AT"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 3: Compute integral of e to power A tau d tau from 0 to T, multiply by B"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 4: Set Gamma as that product"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 5: Write discrete state equation: x at k+1 = Phi x at k + Gamma u at k"]
    A6 --> A7["Step 6: Write output equation: y at k = C x at k + D u at k"]
    A7 --> A8["Step 7: Verify stability by checking eigenvalues of Phi inside unit circle"]
    A8 --> A9["End: Discrete model complete"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `A2`), and all labels are plain uppercase text without any markdown formatting tags, in strict compliance with the rendering safeguard rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the state transition matrix of a discrete data system. List any two of its important properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **state transition matrix** $\Phi(T)$ of a discrete data system (with sampling period $T$) is defined as the matrix that propagates the state from one sampling instant to the next in the absence of any input:
> $$\Phi(T) = e^{AT}$$
> where $A$ is the system matrix of the underlying continuous plant.
> In matrix form, $x[(k+1)T] = \Phi(T)\,x(kT)$ when $u(kT) = 0$.

**Two key properties:**

1. **Identity at $t = 0$:** $\Phi(0) = e^{A \cdot 0} = I$ (the $n \times n$ identity matrix).
2. **Semigroup (composition) property:** $\Phi(T_1 + T_2) = \Phi(T_1)\,\Phi(T_2)$ for any non-negative $T_1, T_2$.
   *Equivalently*, $\Phi(kT) = [\Phi(T)]^{k}$ for integer $k \ge 0$.

*(Alternative acceptable properties: $\Phi^{-1}(T) = \Phi(-T)$, $\det[\Phi(T)] = e^{\text{tr}(A) \cdot T} \ne 0$, or $\Phi^{-1}(T)$ exists for all finite $T$.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is a zero-order hold (ZOH)? Write the discrete input matrix $\Gamma(T)$ for a continuous plant with system matrix $A$ and input matrix $B$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is a sample-and-hold device that reconstructs a continuous-time signal $u(t)$ from its samples $u(kT)$ by holding each sample value **constant** over the entire sampling interval:
> $$u(t) = u(kT), \quad kT \le t < (k+1)T$$
> The ZOH is the most common model for a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) used at the output of a digital controller.
>
> The **discrete input matrix** $\Gamma(T)$ for a continuous plant $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ preceded by a ZOH with sampling period $T$ is:
> $$\Gamma(T) = \left(\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau\right) B$$
> When $A$ is nonsingular, this simplifies to:
> $$\Gamma(T) = A^{-1}\left(e^{AT} - I\right)B = A^{-1}\left[\Phi(T) - I\right]B$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Pattern**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

A continuous-time system is described by
$$\dot{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} x(t) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(t), \qquad y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x(t)$$

The system is preceded by a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) with sampling period $T = 0.5$ seconds.

**(a)** Derive the discrete-time state model of the system, clearly stating the state transition matrix $\Phi$ and the input matrix $\Gamma$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the response $x(T)$ and $x(2T)$ for a unit step input $u(kT) = 1$ for all $k \ge 0$, assuming zero initial state $x(0) = 0$. Also, comment on the stability of the discretized system. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A**

**Part (a) — Discrete State Model Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the matrices:**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A^{2}$** (useful for truncated series):
$$A^{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the State Transition Matrix $\Phi(T)$** using the Taylor series $e^{AT} = I + AT + \dfrac{(AT)^2}{2!} + \dfrac{(AT)^3}{3!} + \cdots$

For this upper-triangular $A$, the series terminates nicely (the system is essentially a chain of two integrators with damping on the second):
$$\Phi(T) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1.333 \end{bmatrix} + \cdots$$

Summing the $(1,2)$ entries: $0.5 - 0.5 + 0.1667 - 0.0556 + \cdots = \dfrac{1 - e^{-1}}{2} \cdot 1 \cdot \text{(scaled by } T\text{)}$. Using the exact closed form for this structure:
$$\Phi(T) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \dfrac{1 - e^{-2T}}{2} \\ 0 & e^{-2T} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $T = 0.5$:**
$$e^{-2T} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$
$$\frac{1 - e^{-2T}}{2} = \frac{1 - 0.3679}{2} = \frac{0.6321}{2} = 0.3161$$
$$\Phi(0.5) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3161 \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\Gamma(T)$** using $\Gamma = A^{-1}(\Phi - I)B$ (since $A$ is nonsingular here, $A^{-1}$ exists).

$$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0}\text{(singular! use integral form)}$$

Since $\det(A) = 0$, $A$ is **singular**; we cannot use $A^{-1}$. We must use the direct integral:
$$\Gamma(T) = \int_{0}^{T}e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \cdot B$$

For this system, $e^{A\tau}$ has the form
$$e^{A\tau} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \dfrac{1 - e^{-2\tau}}{2} \\ 0 & e^{-2\tau} \end{bmatrix}$$

Integrating entry-by-entry from 0 to $T = 0.5$:
$$\int_{0}^{T}e^{A\tau}\,d\tau = \begin{bmatrix} T & \dfrac{T}{2} + \dfrac{e^{-2T} - 1}{4} \\ 0 & \dfrac{1 - e^{-2T}}{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting $T = 0.5$:
$$\int_{0}^{0.5}e^{A\tau}\,d\tau = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.25 + 0.1580 \\ 0 & 0.3161 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4080 \\ 0 & 0.3161 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by $B = [0 \;\; 1]^{T}$:**
$$\Gamma(0.5) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4080 \\ 0 & 0.3161 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4080 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Write the discrete model:**
$$x[(k+1)T] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3161 \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix} x(kT) + \begin{bmatrix} 0.4080 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix} u(kT)$$
$$y(kT) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x(kT)$$

---

**Part (b) — Step Response & Stability (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute $x(T)$** using $x(T) = \Phi\,x(0) + \Gamma\,u(0) = \Phi \cdot 0 + \Gamma \cdot 1$:
$$x(T) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4080 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $x(2T)$:**
$$x(2T) = \Phi\,x(T) + \Gamma\,u(T)$$

$$\Phi\,x(T) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3161 \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.4080 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4080 + 0.0999 \\ 0.1163 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5079 \\ 0.1163 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$x(2T) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5079 \\ 0.1163 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.4080 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9159 \\ 0.4324 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Output samples:**
$$y(0) = C\,x(0) = 0, \quad y(T) = 0.4080, \quad y(2T) = 0.9159$$

**Step 4 — Stability analysis:**

Eigenvalues of $\Phi$ are the diagonal entries (since $\Phi$ is upper-triangular):
$$\lambda_1 = 1, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.3679$$

- $\vert \lambda_1 \vert = 1$ — on the unit circle.
- $\vert \lambda_2 \vert = 0.3679 < 1$ — strictly inside.

**Conclusion:** The system is **marginally stable** (not asymptotically stable). The eigenvalue at $\lambda = 1$ corresponds to the **integrator** in the plant ($A$ has an eigenvalue at $s = 0$), and it survives discretization unchanged. To make the closed-loop system asymptotically stable, a digital controller must move this eigenvalue strictly inside the unit circle.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** For a continuous-time plant with system matrix $A$ and input matrix $B$, derive the discrete-time state model when the input is applied through a Zero-Order Hold with sampling period $T$. Clearly state the expressions for the state transition matrix $\Phi(T)$ and the discrete input matrix $\Gamma(T)$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A scalar system is given by $\dot{x}(t) = -x(t) + u(t)$ with sampling period $T = 0.1$ s and ZOH at the input. (i) Find $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$. (ii) Compute $x(T)$ and $x(2T)$ for a unit step input and $x(0) = 0$. (iii) Verify whether the discrete system is stable. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Continuous plant with ZOH input:**
$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t), \quad u(t) = u(kT), \; kT \le t < (k+1)T$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $e^{-At}$ and recognize the exact derivative:**
$$e^{-At}\dot{x}(t) - e^{-At}Ax(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-At}x(t)\right] = e^{-At}Bu(kT)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $kT$ to $(k+1)T$:**
$$e^{-A(k+1)T}x[(k+1)T] - e^{-AkT}x(kT) = \left(\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{-At}\,dt\right)Bu(kT)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $e^{A(k+1)T}$ on the left** and simplify the integral using the substitution $\tau = t - kT$:
$$x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}x(kT) + \left(\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau\right)Bu(kT)$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**
$$\boxed{x[(k+1)T] = \Phi(T)x(kT) + \Gamma(T)u(kT)}$$
where
$$\boxed{\Phi(T) = e^{AT}, \qquad \Gamma(T) = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \cdot B}$$

If $A$ is nonsingular: $\Gamma(T) = A^{-1}(e^{AT} - I)B = A^{-1}[\Phi(T) - I]B$.

---

**Part (b) — Scalar System (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — System parameters:** $a = -1, \; b = 1, \; T = 0.1$ s.

**Step 2 — Compute $\Phi$:**
$$\Phi = e^{aT} = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Gamma$** using $\Gamma = \dfrac{b}{a}(e^{aT} - 1)$ (valid since $a \ne 0$):
$$\Gamma = \frac{1}{-1}(e^{-0.1} - 1) = -(0.9048 - 1) = 0.0952$$

**Step 4 — Discrete model:**
$$x[(k+1)T] = 0.9048\,x(kT) + 0.0952\,u(kT)$$

**Step 5 — Compute $x(T)$** with $x(0) = 0, \; u(0) = 1$:
$$x(T) = 0.9048(0) + 0.0952(1) = 0.0952$$

**Step 6 — Compute $x(2T)$** with $u(T) = 1$:
$$x(2T) = 0.9048(0.0952) + 0.0952(1) = 0.0862 + 0.0952 = 0.1814$$

**Step 7 — Stability check:**
Eigenvalue of $\Phi$ is $\lambda = 0.9048$. Since $\vert 0.9048 \vert < 1$, the discrete system is **asymptotically stable**. (This is consistent with the continuous system being stable, since $\text{Re}(a) = -1 < 0$.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the matrix $B$** in the definition of $\Gamma(T)$. Many students write $\Gamma = \int_0^T e^{A\tau}d\tau$ without multiplying by $B$. Marks will be deducted.
> 2. **Confusing the ZOH with an impulse sampler.** A ZOH delivers a *rectangular pulse* of width $T$, not a Dirac delta. The integral in $\Gamma$ runs from $0$ to $T$, not over an impulse.
> 3. **Using the inverse formula $A^{-1}(\Phi - I)B$ when $A$ is singular.** When $\det(A) = 0$, this formula fails. Use the direct integral.
> 4. **Forgetting to state $T$ explicitly.** Always write $\Phi(T)$ and $\Gamma(T)$, not just $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$.
> 5. **Stability conclusion errors:** A continuous-time eigenvalue at $s = 0$ (integrator) maps to $z = 1$ (on unit circle) — *not* to a stable location. Many students incorrectly conclude such systems are stable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — State Variable Model with S/H**

- [ ] **Continuous plant form:** $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$, $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$.
- [ ] **ZOH behavior:** $u(t) = u(kT)$ is constant over $[kT, (k+1)T)$.
- [ ] **State Transition Matrix:** $\Phi(T) = e^{AT} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(AT)^k}{k!}$.
- [ ] **Discrete Input Matrix:** $\Gamma(T) = \left(\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau\right) B$.
- [ ] **Alternative form** (nonsingular $A$ only): $\Gamma(T) = A^{-1}[\Phi(T) - I]B$.
- [ ] **Canonical discrete model:** $x[(k+1)T] = \Phi x(kT) + \Gamma u(kT)$, $y(kT) = Cx(kT) + Du(kT)$.
- [ ] **General solution:** $x(kT) = \Phi^{k}x(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\Phi^{k-1-i}\Gamma u(iT)$.
- [ ] **Two response components:** zero-input ($x_{zs}$) and zero-state ($x_{zi}$).
- [ ] **Properties of $\Phi$:** $\Phi(0) = I$, $\Phi(T_1 + T_2) = \Phi(T_1)\Phi(T_2)$, $\Phi^{-1}(T) = \Phi(-T)$, $\det[\Phi(T)] = e^{\text{tr}(A)T}$.
- [ ] **Stability criterion:** All eigenvalues of $\Phi$ must lie strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$).
- [ ] **Mapping property:** A continuous eigenvalue $\mu$ in the LHP maps to $\lambda = e^{\mu T}$, which always lies inside the unit circle.
- [ ] **Caveat:** $\mu = 0$ (integrator) maps to $\lambda = 1$ — the discrete system is *not* asymptotically stable.
- [ ] **Scalar shortcuts:** For $\dot{x} = ax + bu$ with ZOH: $\Phi = e^{aT}$, $\Gamma = \dfrac{b}{a}(e^{aT} - 1)$; for $a = 0$: $\Gamma = bT$.
- [ ] **The ZOH is the *most common* model** for the DAC in real digital control systems; the First-Order Hold (FOH) and higher-order holds exist but are rarely used.
- [ ] **Equivalence holds only at sampling instants** $t = kT$; inter-sample behavior is not captured by the discrete model.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Sentence Memory Aid:** *"ZOH gives you $\Phi = e^{AT}$ and $\Gamma = \int_0^T e^{A\tau}d\tau \cdot B$ — that's the entire discrete state model, full stop."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Diagrams. Relationship Between State Space Representation and Pulse Transfer Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET744 - DISCRETE TIME CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** State Diagrams. Relationship Between State Space Representation and Pulse Transfer Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **State Diagram** is a special type of signal flow graph (or block diagram) used to represent the dynamics of a linear discrete-time system described in state space form. It uses **unit delay elements** (represented as $1/z$ blocks) to capture the one-sample-shift relationship between the current state $x(k)$ and the next state $x(k+1)$.

A discrete-time state space system is generally expressed as:

$$
x(k+1) = A\, x(k) + B\, u(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = C\, x(k) + D\, u(k)
$$

where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the state matrix, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the input matrix, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ is the output matrix, and $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ is the direct (feed-forward) transmission matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The state diagram is essentially a "blueprint" of the system in the z-domain. Each node represents a state variable or signal, and the directed branches represent scalar gains taken from the $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ matrices. Mastering the construction of state diagrams is the gateway to deriving the pulse transfer function directly from state space.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a state diagram like the **circuit board of a digital washing machine controller**. Each "memory register" inside the microcontroller stores the current state (e.g., water level, spin speed, time remaining). The registers are connected through wires (branches) with specific gain values (resistor-like multipliers). At every clock tick, the values in the registers are **shifted** through unit delay blocks (which behave like a one-step "buffer") so that the "next" state becomes the "current" state on the next tick. The input commands (knob settings) feed into this network, and the output (display showing remaining time) is read off the system. Just as a circuit board shows the wiring between components, a state diagram shows the algebraic wiring between state variables and signals in the z-domain.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

- **State Variable:** The minimum set of variables that completely describes the future behavior of a system given the present and the future inputs.
- **State Vector $x(k)$:** A column vector containing all $n$ state variables at the $k$-th sampling instant.
- **Unit Delay Element $z^{-1}$:** A block that delays the input signal by one sampling period $T$.
- **Pulse Transfer Function $G(z)$:** The z-domain ratio $Y(z)/U(z)$ under zero initial conditions.
- **Characteristic Equation:** $\det(zI - A) = 0$, whose roots are the system poles (eigenvalues of $A$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between state space representation and pulse transfer function is **bijective** (one-to-one) for a controllable and observable system. That is, every state space model corresponds to a unique $G(z)$ and vice versa, but $G(z)$ alone cannot uniquely recover a state space model (different state space models can yield the same $G(z)$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Behind State Diagram Construction

Constructing a state diagram for a discrete-time state space system follows a strict, logical sequence:

1. **Identify the state equations** of the system. For an $n$-th order system, there are $n$ first-order difference equations: $x_i(k+1) = \sum_j a_{ij} x_j(k) + \sum_l b_{il} u_l(k)$.

2. **Draw one summing junction** for each state variable. Each junction adds all the incoming contributions that determine $x_i(k+1)$.

3. **Insert a unit delay block $1/z$** at the output of each summing junction. This converts $x_i(k+1)$ into $x_i(k)$, enforcing the one-step shift in discrete time.

4. **Draw outgoing branches from each $x_i(k)$ node** with gains equal to $a_{ij}$ leading to the summing junction of $x_j(k+1)$. This captures the "internal feedback" of the system (the $A$ matrix).

5. **Draw input branches** from $u(k)$ to each summing junction with gains $b_{ij}$, representing the influence of the input on each state (the $B$ matrix).

6. **Draw output branches** from each state node to a common output summing junction with gains $c_{ij}$, capturing the $C$ matrix. If a direct feed-through term $D$ exists, connect $u(k)$ directly to the output with gain $D$.

7. **Verify dimensions**: every branch gain must be a scalar (for SISO) or matrix (for MIMO), and signal flow must respect dimensions (e.g., a 2-element $u(k)$ cannot enter a 1-element state without proper $B$).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the State Diagram

- The unit delay $1/z$ is the **fundamental memory element** of any discrete-time system. Without it, there is no notion of "past vs. present" — both states would collapse into algebraic equations.
- The diagram visually exposes the **eigenstructure** of the system. The closed loops in the diagram correspond to the characteristic equation $\det(zI - A) = 0$.
- It allows application of **Mason's Gain Formula** to compute the pulse transfer function $G(z)$ directly from the diagram without manipulating $(zI - A)^{-1}$ matrix inversions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $x(k+1) = Ax(k) + Bu(k)$ | Discrete state equation |
| 2 | $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$ | Discrete output equation |
| 3 | $zX(z) = AX(z) + BU(z)$ (zero IC) | z-transform of state equation |
| 4 | $X(z) = (zI - A)^{-1} B\, U(z)$ | Solved state in z-domain |
| 5 | $G(z) = C(zI - A)^{-1} B + D$ | **Pulse Transfer Function from SS** |
| 6 | $\Delta(z) = \det(zI - A)$ | Characteristic polynomial |
| 7 | $\text{char. eq.: } \Delta(z) = 0$ | System poles (eigenvalues) |
| 8 | $G(z) = \dfrac{N(z)}{\Delta(z)}$ | Standard TF form |
| 9 | Mason: $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_k P_k \Delta_k$ | From signal flow graph |
| 10 | $G(z) = \dfrac{Y(z)}{U(z)} \Big\vert_{x(0)=0}$ | Definition of pulse TF |
| 11 | $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \dots$ | Graph determinant |
| 12 | $L_i$ | Loop gain of the $i$-th closed loop |
| 13 | $P_k$ | Forward path gain |
| 14 | $\Delta_k$ | Cofactor (loop set with $P_k$ removed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examination answers, when a question asks for the pulse transfer function from a state space model, writing $G(z) = C(zI - A)^{-1} B + D$ followed by explicit expansion is the cleanest, most marks-fetching approach. Substitute, invert, multiply, simplify — each step earns credit.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

State diagrams are used in designing **digital controllers** (microcontroller-based servo drives, robotic arm controllers), **digital filters** (FIR/IIR realizations in DSP chips), **flight control computers** in modern aircraft, and **battery management systems** in electric vehicles. The ability to translate a state space model into a state diagram allows engineers to design **observer-based estimators** (like the Kalman filter) and to analyze the stability, controllability, and observability of the system using the geometric structure of the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From State Space to Pulse Transfer Function

We are given the discrete-time state space representation:

$$
x(k+1) = A\, x(k) + B\, u(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = C\, x(k) + D\, u(k)
$$

**Step 1: Apply the one-sided z-transform to the state equation.**

The z-transform of a shifted sequence $x(k+1)$ (with zero initial conditions) is $zX(z) - zx(0)$. Applying this to both sides:

$$
zX(z) - zx(0) = A\, X(z) + B\, U(z)
$$

**Step 2: Collect $X(z)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$
zX(z) - A\, X(z) = zx(0) + B\, U(z)
$$

**Step 3: Factor out $X(z)$.**

$$
(zI - A)\, X(z) = zx(0) + B\, U(z)
$$

**Step 4: Pre-multiply both sides by $(zI - A)^{-1}$ to isolate $X(z)$.**

$$
X(z) = (zI - A)^{-1} zx(0) + (zI - A)^{-1} B\, U(z)
$$

**Step 5: Apply the z-transform to the output equation.**

$$
Y(z) = C\, X(z) + D\, U(z)
$$

**Step 6: Substitute the expression for $X(z)$ into the output equation.**

$$
Y(z) = C\, \left[ (zI - A)^{-1} zx(0) + (zI - A)^{-1} B\, U(z) \right] + D\, U(z)
$$

**Step 7: Apply the zero-initial-condition assumption for deriving the pulse transfer function** (i.e., $x(0) = 0$). This eliminates the free-response term:

$$
Y(z) = C\, (zI - A)^{-1} B\, U(z) + D\, U(z)
$$

**Step 8: Factor out $U(z)$ to obtain the ratio $Y(z)/U(z)$.**

$$
Y(z) = \left[ C\, (zI - A)^{-1} B + D \right] U(z)
$$

**Step 9: Identify the pulse transfer function.**

$$
G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C\, (zI - A)^{-1} B + D
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master formula** for KTU examinations. The denominator of $G(z)$ is exactly the characteristic polynomial $\Delta(z) = \det(zI - A)$, whose roots are the system poles. The numerator is a polynomial whose roots are the system zeros.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — From State Diagram to Pulse Transfer Function via Mason's Gain Formula

Given a state diagram, Mason's Gain Formula gives the transfer function as:

$$
T = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k} P_k \Delta_k
$$

where:
- $\Delta$ is the graph determinant: $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \dots$
- $P_k$ is the gain of the $k$-th forward path from input to output.
- $\Delta_k$ is the cofactor, computed as the determinant of the graph with all loops touching the $k$-th forward path removed.

**Worked Example 1 (Scalar Case, SISO):**

Consider a 2nd order discrete system with state space form:

$$
x(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1.2 \end{bmatrix} x(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x(k)
$$

Here $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1.2 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D = 0$.

**Step 1: Form the matrix $(zI - A)$.**

$$
zI - A = \begin{bmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} z & -1 \\ 0.5 & z - 1.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the determinant of $(zI - A)$.**

$$
\det(zI - A) = z(z - 1.2) - (-1)(0.5) = z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5
$$

**Step 3: Compute the adjugate of $(zI - A)$** (transpose of cofactor matrix).

$$
\text{adj}(zI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} z - 1.2 & 1 \\ -0.5 & z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $(zI - A)^{-1}$.**

$$
(zI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} z - 1.2 & 1 \\ -0.5 & z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $(zI - A)^{-1} B$.**

$$
(zI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} z - 1.2 & 1 \\ -0.5 & z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6: Compute $C(zI - A)^{-1} B$.**

$$
C(zI - A)^{-1} B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5}
$$

**Step 7: Add $D$ and finalize the pulse transfer function.**

$$
G(z) = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When $D = 0$ and $B$ has the special form $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & \dots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$ (controllable canonical form), the answer simplifies dramatically because $C$ picks out only one row of the inverse matrix.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Constructing the State Diagram for a Given State Space

Given the system in Section 3.2, we construct the state diagram. The two state equations are:

$$
x_1(k+1) = 0 \cdot x_1(k) + 1 \cdot x_2(k) + 0 \cdot u(k) = x_2(k)
$$

$$
x_2(k+1) = -0.5 \cdot x_1(k) + 1.2 \cdot x_2(k) + 1 \cdot u(k)
$$

The output equation is:

$$
y(k) = 1 \cdot x_1(k) + 0 \cdot x_2(k) + 0 \cdot u(k) = x_1(k)
$$

For each $x_i(k+1)$, draw:
- A summing junction.
- A $1/z$ block whose input is the sum and whose output is $x_i(k)$.
- A self-loop with gain $a_{ii}$ (for $a_{11} = 0$ and $a_{22} = 1.2$).
- Cross-connections with gain $a_{ij}$ ($a_{12} = 1$ and $a_{21} = -0.5$).
- An input branch with gain $b_i$ ($b_1 = 0$ and $b_2 = 1$).
- An output branch with gain $c_i$ ($c_1 = 1$ and $c_2 = 0$).

Applying Mason's Gain Formula on this diagram:
- There is **one forward path** from $u(k)$ to $y(k)$: through $b_2 = 1$, then through the chain to $x_1$ via the $a_{12} = 1$ and the cross-coupling, etc. The forward path gain is $P_1 = 1 \cdot z^{-1} \cdot z^{-1} = z^{-2}$ (each unit delay contributes one $z^{-1}$).
- There are **two feedback loops**: $L_1 = 1.2 z^{-1}$ (self-loop of $x_2$) and $L_2 = -0.5 z^{-2}$ (loop from $x_1$ to $x_2$ and back through $a_{12}$ and $a_{21}$).
- The graph determinant is $\Delta = 1 - (1.2 z^{-1} - 0.5 z^{-2}) = 1 - 1.2 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}$.
- The cofactor $\Delta_1 = 1$ (the only forward path touches both loops).

$$
G(z) = \frac{z^{-2}}{1 - 1.2 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}} = \frac{1}{z^2 - 1.2z + 0.5}
$$

This matches the result from Derivation 2, confirming the equivalence.

### 3.4 Equivalence Summary

| Aspect | State Space $(A, B, C, D)$ | Pulse Transfer Function $G(z)$ | State Diagram |
|--------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| Domain | Time (difference equations) | Frequency (z-domain) | Graphical |
| Order info | $n$ (dimension of $A$) | $n$ (degree of denominator) | Number of delay blocks |
| Poles | Eigenvalues of $A$ | Roots of $\Delta(z)$ | Closed-loop gains |
| Zeros | Implicit in $C(zI-A)^{-1}B+D$ | Roots of $N(z)$ | Forward path products |
| Synthesis | Direct from physical system | $G(z) = C(zI-A)^{-1}B + D$ | Diagram $\xrightarrow{\text{Mason}}$ $G(z)$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic State Diagram Block Architecture

The state diagram for an $n$-th order SISO discrete system with state vector $x(k) = [x_1(k), x_2(k), \dots, x_n(k)]^T$, input $u(k)$, and output $y(k)$ has a consistent structural pattern. The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture flow** of the generic state diagram, with two illustrative state variables $x_1$ and $x_2$ (for an $n=2$ system) and the corresponding feedback/feedforward branches.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeU["Input u_k"] --> nodeB1["Gain b1"]
    nodeU --> nodeB2["Gain b2"]
    nodeU --> nodeD["Gain D"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeSum1["Sum Junction for x1 k+1"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeSum2["Sum Junction for x2 k+1"]
    nodeD --> nodeYsum["Output Summer"]
    nodeX1["x1 k"] -- "Gain a11" --> nodeSum1
    nodeX1 -- "Gain a21" --> nodeSum2
    nodeX1 -- "Gain c1" --> nodeYsum
    nodeX2["x2 k"] -- "Gain a12" --> nodeSum1
    nodeX2 -- "Gain a22" --> nodeSum2
    nodeX2 -- "Gain c2" --> nodeYsum
    nodeSum1 -- "Unit Delay 1 by z" --> nodeX1
    nodeSum2 -- "Unit Delay 1 by z" --> nodeX2
    nodeYsum --> nodeY["Output y_k"]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Signal Flow for a Canonical 2nd-Order System

The Mermaid block below maps the full forward and feedback signal flow for the worked example $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 1.2 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D = 0$, where:

- Forward path 1: $u \to$ gain $1$ $\to$ summer of $x_2(k+1)$ $\to$ delay $\to x_2$ $\to$ gain $1$ $\to$ summer of $x_1(k+1)$ $\to$ delay $\to x_1$ $\to$ gain $1$ $\to$ output.
- Self-loop on $x_2$: $x_2 \to$ gain $1.2$ $\to$ summer of $x_2(k+1)$ $\to$ delay $\to x_2$ (loop gain $= 1.2 z^{-1}$).
- Cross-feedback loop: $x_1 \to$ gain $-0.5$ $\to$ summer of $x_2(k+1)$ $\to$ delay $\to x_2 \to$ gain $1$ $\to$ summer of $x_1(k+1)$ $\to$ delay $\to x_1$ (loop gain $= -0.5 z^{-2}$).

```mermaid
graph TD
    inpU["Input u_k"] -- "Gain b2 = 1" --> sumX2["Sum for x2 k+1"]
    inpU -- "Gain b1 = 0 no connection" --> sumX1["Sum for x1 k+1"]
    stateX1["State x1 k"] -- "Gain a21 = minus 0.5" --> sumX2
    stateX1 -- "Gain c1 = 1" --> outSum["Output Summer"]
    stateX2["State x2 k"] -- "Gain a12 = 1" --> sumX1
    stateX2 -- "Gain a22 = 1.2 self loop" --> sumX2
    stateX2 -- "Gain c2 = 0 no connection" --> outSum
    sumX1 -- "Delay 1 by z" --> stateX1
    sumX2 -- "Delay 1 by z" --> stateX2
    outSum --> outY["Output y_k"]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — From State Space to Pulse Transfer Function

The Mermaid flowchart below summarizes the analytical pathway connecting a state space model to its pulse transfer function. This conceptual map is high-yield for KTU theory questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ssA["State Space Model A B C D"] --> choiceA{"Approach"}
    choiceA -- "Direct Matrix Method" --> stepB["Form zI minus A"]
    choiceA -- "State Diagram then Mason" --> stepC["Draw State Diagram with 1 by z delays"]
    stepB --> stepD["Compute zI minus A inverse"]
    stepD --> stepE["Gz = C times inverse times B plus D"]
    stepC --> stepF["Identify forward paths and loops"]
    stepF --> stepG["Apply Mason Gain Formula"]
    stepE --> stepH["Final Gz in polynomial form"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI["Compare denominators to get char polynomial"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - Double arrows in Mermaid indicate a branch.
> - "Sum" nodes represent summing junctions.
> - "Delay 1 by z" represents the unit delay block $z^{-1}$.
> - "State $x_1 k$" represents the node carrying the value of $x_1(k)$.
> - Self-loops (drawn as a branch from a state node back to its own summer) are the diagonal elements $a_{ii}$.
> - Cross-branches (drawn as a branch from $x_j$ to the summer of $x_i$) are the off-diagonal elements $a_{ij}$.
